# Gravel God Race Database — Full Context

> 463 gravel, MTB, and bikepacking races rated on 14 criteria.
> Produced by Gravel God (gravelgodcycling.com). Generated: 2026-03-02

## Scoring Methodology

Each race is scored on 14 dimensions (1-5 scale):
logistics, length, technicality, elevation, climate, altitude, adventure, prestige, race_quality, experience, community, field_depth, value, expenses.

Overall score = round((sum of 14 scores / 70) * 100).

Tier assignment:
- Tier 1 (Elite): score >= 80, OR prestige=5 + score>=75
- Tier 2 (Strong): score >= 60, OR prestige=5 + score<75 (capped at T2)
- Tier 3 (Solid): score >= 45
- Tier 4 (Developing): score < 45
- Prestige 4 promotes one tier (but not into T1)

## Tier 1 & Tier 2 Races (213 races)

### Transcontinental Race
Tier 1 | Score: 97/100 | 3100 mi | 210,000 ft | Across Europe (route changes annually) | July | bikepacking
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/transcontinental-race/

The flagship European ultra — 4,000-5,000km self-supported across a continent. Mike Hall's legacy. The ultimate test of human endurance on a road bike. A continental crossing by bicycle. Start and finish points change each year — London to Istanbul, Geraardsbergen to Thessaloniki, Roubaix to Istanbul, Santiago de Compostela to Constanta, and Trondheim to Kalamata. The free-route format means riders plan their own path between 4 mandatory control points, each spaced 500-1,200km apart. Between checkpoints, you could ride 800km on flat highways or 600km through mountain passes — the strategic choice is yours. Mandatory parcours at each control point (5-100+ km of prescribed route, often on gravel through mountains) force every rider through the same crucible. The TCR is not about any single course — it is about the concept: cross Europe by bike, self-supported, as fast as you can. Signature challenge: 4,000-5,000km self-supported across a continent. Free-route navigation between mandatory control points. 30,000-64,000m climbing depending on edition and route choice. 0-40C temperature range. Clock never stops. No support. The ultimate test of human endurance, navigation, and self-reliance on a road bike. The Transcontinental Race is ultra-distance cycling's most iconic European event. Founded by Mike Hall in 2013, it established the template for self-supported racing that defines the sport. 4,000-5,000km across a continent, free-route format, clock never stops, completely self-supported. TCR does not compare to other races — it is the standard against which all European ultra races are measured. Finishing is a lifetime achievement. Winning is legend. Yes if you have 2+ years ultra-distance experience, can ride 300-400km days consistently, handle extreme sleep deprivation, and want cycling's ultimate European challenge. Yes if you have completed Paris-Brest-Paris, a 1,200km+ audax, or a shorter ultra-distance race. Skip if you have not completed multi-day unsupported rides. Skip if you cannot commit to 6+ months of dedicated training. Skip if you expect it to be 'just a long race' — this is an expedition that happens to be timed. Non-negotiables: Ultra-distance riding experience — minimum 1,200km+ unsupported ride completed; Proven equipment setup tested over 1,000+ km including charging system, lighting, and navigation; Route planning for all 4 checkpoint-to-checkpoint segments with contingency alternatives

### Tour Divide
Tier 1 | Score: 94/100 | 2745 mi | 200,000 ft | Banff, Alberta to Antelope Wells, New Mexico | June | bikepacking
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/tour-divide/

The longest mountain bike time trial on earth—2,745 miles from Banff to Mexico, completely self-supported, pure amateur ethos. A 2,745-mile expedition along the Continental Divide from Canada to Mexico. The route passes through 5 US states and 2 Canadian provinces, crossing the Divide 30+ times. Terrain ranges from glaciated Canadian Rockies to Wyoming's Red Desert to Colorado's 11,000+ foot passes to New Mexico's rocky Gila Wilderness. Surface is roughly 90% unpaved—mostly gravel and forest service roads with some singletrack and paved connectors. The character changes daily as you ride south through changing ecosystems, elevations, and climates. Signature challenge: The complete package—2,745 miles of self-supported suffering through every climate zone, every terrain type, and every mental state across 2-4 weeks on the Continental Divide. Tour Divide is bikepacking racing at its purest and most extreme. 2,745 miles from Banff to Mexico, completely self-supported, no entry fee, no prizes, no sponsors—just you, your bike, and the Continental Divide for 2-4 weeks. It's not really comparable to other races; it's an expedition that happens to be timed. The combination of distance, elevation, terrain diversity, climate extremes, and complete self-reliance creates something unique in endurance sports. With only ~716 finishers in history and a 47-60% DNF rate, completing Tour Divide joins a very exclusive club. Yes if you have serious ultra-endurance experience and can commit 2-4 weeks to the Continental Divide. Yes if you can handle complete self-sufficiency across remote terrain. Yes if you want the ultimate test. Skip if you haven't completed multi-day self-supported events. Skip if you need race organization, aid stations, or support crews. Skip if you cannot repair your own bike.

### Unbound Gravel 200
Tier 1 | Score: 93/100 | 200 mi | 11,000 ft | Emporia, Kansas | June | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/unbound-200/

The gravel race by which all others are measured. Relentless. Not one killer climb, but 200 miles of constant attrition. At 200 miles, this is a full-day effort. With 11K+ feet of climbing, the vertical gain is relentless. Signature challenge: The heat. The duration. The Flint Hills don't negotiate. Heat adaptation isn't optional—it's survival. The combination of 200 miles and extreme temperatures creates a unique endurance challenge. Unbound is gravel racing's Mount Everest—not because it's the hardest course, but because it's the race that defines the sport. Riders don't 'do' Unbound casually. They plan for it, obsess over heat protocols, and show up knowing that crossing the line in Emporia means something. The course itself isn't revolutionary—rolling terrain, gravel, heat. What makes it legendary is the field, the atmosphere, and the weight of the finish line. If you finish Unbound, you've earned it. If you're asking whether Unbound is 'worth it,' the answer is probably yes—but only if you're willing to commit to the training, the logistics, and the reality that this race will test every assumption you have about your durability. It's not fun. It's not easy. But it's unforgettable. Non-negotiables: Heat adaptation protocol built into weeks 6-10; Flint-specific cornering and line selection skills; 9-hour dress rehearsal in week 9

### Traka 360
Tier 1 | Score: 91/100 | 224 mi | 11,000 ft | Girona, Catalonia, Spain | May | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/the-traka/

Europe's Unbound—360km of technical Mediterranean gravel with Garmin Hill waiting to break you at km 300. Relentless climbing on Mediterranean gravel. 85% gravel with rough sections better suited to MTB. Flowing early terrain gives way to rougher mid-course chunks. Signature challenge: Garmin Hill—the steepest, slowest, most brutal climb of the day, hitting when you have 300+ km in your legs. If you're serious about gravel and have any connection to European cycling culture, Traka is a must. It's not just a race—it's a pilgrimage to the sport's spiritual home. Where else can you suffer on the same roads that Pogačar trains on? Yes, if you want Europe's premier gravel experience and can handle 12-18+ hours of racing. Traka delivers WorldTour-level legitimacy, stunning Catalan terrain, and a field from 74 countries. Just be ready for rough sections, Garmin Hill after 300km, and run 44-50mm tires.

### Colorado Trail Race
Tier 1 | Score: 90/100 | 530 mi | 72,500 ft | Durango to Denver, Colorado | August | bikepacking
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/colorado-trail-race/

The Colorado Trail doesn't care about your FTP when you're pushing your bike through talus fields at 13,000 feet. The CTR is 530 miles of high-altitude punishment disguised as a trail race. You'll spend most of your time above 10,000 feet, where 70% singletrack means constant technical riding interrupted by mandatory hike-a-bikes. This isn't gravel racing—it's Alpine survival with a bike occasionally underneath you. Signature challenge: The altitude never lets up—you'll average over 10,000 feet for 530 miles while navigating technical singletrack that forces constant hike-a-bikes. Most riders don't realize that CTR's difficulty isn't the climbing, it's maintaining any rhythm when you're pushing your bike every few miles at oxygen levels that make sea-level riders dizzy just standing still. This is for riders who've already proven they can suffer for days, not weekends. You need legitimate bikepacking experience, gear dialed for everything from desert heat to alpine storms, and the fitness to hike-a-bike for miles without breaking. If you haven't done multi-day self-supported racing or think 530 miles sounds doable because you can ride centuries, stay home.

### Trans Am Bike Race
Tier 1 | Score: 90/100 | 4233 mi | 175,000 ft | Astoria, Oregon to Yorktown, Virginia | June | bikepacking
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/trans-am-bike-race/

The longest self-supported bike race on earth—4,200+ miles from Pacific to Atlantic, crossing 10 states, no support crews, pure transcontinental adventure. A 4,200+ mile expedition from Pacific to Atlantic following the historic TransAmerica Bicycle Trail. The route passes through 10 states, crossing the Continental Divide in Colorado and the Appalachian Mountains in Virginia. Surface is roughly 90% paved—quiet county roads, bike paths, and low-traffic highways. The character evolves dramatically: Pacific Northwest forests, Rocky Mountain passes, endless Kansas plains, Ozark rollercoasters, Ohio River farmland, and steep Appalachian ridges. This is America condensed into one transcontinental ride. Signature challenge: The complete American experience—4,200 miles coast to coast through every landscape, every climate, every mental state across 2-5 weeks of self-supported suffering. Trans Am Bike Race is the longest self-supported bike race on earth—4,200+ miles from the Pacific to Atlantic, completely self-supported, across 10 states and every American landscape. While Tour Divide claims the crown for toughest mountain bike ultra, Trans Am is the road equivalent: pure expedition cycling at maximum distance. The mostly-paved route is more accessible technically but no less demanding physically—175,000 feet of climbing over 2-5 weeks will humble any cyclist. With the historic TransAmerica Trail as its backbone and 50 years of trail angel culture, this is America's great cycling pilgrimage. Yes if you have serious ultra-endurance experience and can commit 2-5 weeks to crossing America. Yes if you can handle complete self-sufficiency across 4,200 miles. Yes if you want the ultimate American cycling pilgrimage. Skip if you haven't completed multi-day self-supported events. Skip if 200+ mile days for weeks on end sounds impossible. Skip if Kansas wind and Appalachian rollercoasters would break your spirit.

### UCI Gravel World Championships
Tier 1 | Score: 90/100 | 112 mi | 8,202 ft | Rotating (2024: Heerlen, Netherlands) | October | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/uci-gravel-worlds/

The only gravel race where you can actually call yourself World Champion—if you can handle 180km of whatever terrain the UCI throws at you. 180 km elite, 140 km age group. 70% gravel, 30% paved. Varies by location. Technical rating: 4/5. Signature challenge: 180 km elite + World Championship field + October weather. Rainbow jersey. UCI Gravel World Championships is World Championship—180 km elite, 140 km age group. Founded 2022. 70% gravel, 30% paved. Rotating locations (2022-2023 Italy, 2024 Netherlands). 1,000+ riders (elite + age group). October autumn conditions (10-20°C, 40% rain). UCI license required (€100-€150 / $110-$165). If you want World Championship rainbow jersey and fastest field in gravel, this is it. If you need domestic/local race, skip it. International destination, prestige 5. You need a UCI license, the fitness for nearly five hours of World Championship pace racing, and the mental fortitude to suffer in front of 1,000+ of the world's fastest gravel riders. The course changes annually, so you're gambling on terrain, but if you can't handle 2,500m of climbing and October weather in Europe, stay home. This isn't about participation—it's about proving you belong with the global elite.

### Unbound XL
Tier 1 | Score: 90/100 | 350 mi | 14,000 ft | Emporia, Kansas | May | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/unbound-xl/

The longest gravel race that matters, where the Flint Hills strip away everything but what's left of your soul. Unbound XL is a 350-mile commitment to Kansas limestone gravel that slices tires and punishes poor line choices across the rolling Flint Hills. The course feels like prairie purgatory — constant undulations that never let you settle into a rhythm, with long stretches between gas station salvation points. Sharp, chunky gravel demands constant vigilance while the relentless rollers chip away at your soul one hill at a time. Signature challenge: The 75-mile gap between meaningful resupply points turns nutrition into a high-stakes chess match. Most riders crack not from the hills or the gravel, but from running out of food or water in the Kansas void where gas stations close at 10 PM and your mistakes compound in the darkness. You need to finish a 200-miler first, preferably two. The 36-hour cutoff sounds generous until you're grinding limestone in your 16th hour with 150 miles still ahead. Self-supported means what it says—no crew, no checkpoints, just you and whatever gas stations stay open past midnight in rural Kansas.

### Badlands
Tier 1 | Score: 89/100 | 497 mi | 52,493 ft | Granada, Andalusia, Spain | August | bikepacking
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/badlands/

The world's hardest gravel race—800km through Spanish deserts and alpine passes, completely self-supported. An 800km expedition through Andalusia's extremes. Desert badlands that look like Mars, Europe's only true desert (Tabernas), and the 3,396m Pico Veleta climb. Self-supported means you carry what you need between remote villages. The combination of distance, elevation, heat, and self-reliance is unmatched in gravel racing. Signature challenge: The complete package—800km of desert heat, alpine altitude, and multi-day self-supported suffering through some of Europe's most extreme terrain. Badlands is gravel racing taken to its logical extreme. 800km, 16,000m climbing, 40°C+ deserts, 3,400m alpine passes, completely self-supported. It's not really comparable to other gravel races—it's an expedition that happens to be timed. The combination of distance, elevation, heat, and self-reliance creates something uniquely brutal. Finishing Badlands is a lifetime achievement that earns permanent respect in ultra-endurance circles. Yes if you have genuine ultra-endurance experience and want the ultimate test. Yes if you can handle extreme heat and self-support logistics. Skip if you haven't completed multi-day bikepacking events or can't adapt to 40°C+ conditions.

### Crusher in the Tushar
Tier 1 | Score: 89/100 | 69 mi | 10,000 ft | Beaver, Utah | July | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/crusher-in-the-tushar/

The climber's gravel race—where thin air separates pretenders from contenders. Relentless vertical. You climb for 35 miles, then descend for 35 miles. Simple math, brutal execution. Signature challenge: The altitude. 10,000+ feet average means your power is down 10-15%. Plan accordingly. Crusher is what happens when you take a mountain stage of the Tour and add gravel. It's not for everyone—flat-landers and non-climbers will suffer disproportionately. But if you love vertical and want to test yourself against thin air, this is your race. If you love climbing and want a race that rewards it, absolutely. If you're a diesel-engine flat-lander, consider something else. The altitude alone makes this a specialist's race.

### Leadville Trail 100 MTB
Tier 1 | Score: 89/100 | 100 mi | 11,900 ft | Leadville, Colorado | August | mtb
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/leadville-100/

The mountain bike endurance race that defines the category. Bucket list for a reason. Out-and-back with one massive climb (Columbine) and relentless terrain. The altitude is the course. Signature challenge: Columbine. The climb to 12,600 feet breaks people. If you can't hike-a-bike at altitude, you can't Leadville. Leadville isn't the hardest 100-mile race technically. It's not even the most scenic. But it's the most meaningful. The history, the community, the belt buckle—finishing Leadville changes how you see yourself as an endurance athlete. It's a rite of passage. Yes—if you're serious about endurance riding and willing to commit to proper altitude preparation. No—if you can't take time to acclimatize or haven't trained specifically for high-altitude suffering.

### Belgian Waffle Ride
Tier 1 | Score: 87/100 | 120 mi | 10,000 ft | Del Mar, California | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/bwr-california/

The race where bike handling skills outweigh FTP and 40% learn what DNF feels like. Belgian Waffle Ride delivers 10,000+ feet of climbing across 120-135 miles (2025's 106-mile course being an outlier) mixing 70% pavement with 30% 'unroad'—dirt, gravel, single-track, sand, water crossings, rock gardens. The route uses Belgian-themed sector names marking specific suffering zones. Signature challenge: Six-hour cyclocross race where tire choice matters more than FTP. That 23% grade at mile 130 doesn't negotiate. Belgian Waffle Ride earns its 'Hell of the North County' nickname through relentless variety rather than single massive climbs. The course delivers 10,000+ feet of climbing across 120-135 miles mixing 70% pavement with 30% 'unroad'—dirt, gravel, single-track, sand, water crossings, and rock gardens. Founder Michael Marckx designed the route to expose riders who rely on draft in group rides but lack comprehensive bike handling and endurance skills. The 40% DNF rate validates the course's effectiveness at separating technical riders from pure power athletes, maintaining BWR's identity as an 'un-road' race rather than a pure gravel endurance test. Yes, if you have legitimate technical skills from cyclocross or mountain biking, can handle 12+ hour moving time, and want to test yourself against a course designed to expose weaknesses. The race rewards actual bike riding—handling rough descents, choosing correct lines through sand, managing energy across varied terrain. It's expensive and honest about your capabilities. Skip it if you're purely a power athlete with limited technical skills, have never ridden extended single-track or sand, or can't stomach California travel costs ($1,200-1,800 total). Also skip if you need grassroots intimacy—BWR offers professional production and competitive intensity, not community warmth.

### La Marmotte Granfondo Alpes
Tier 1 | Score: 87/100 | 110 mi | 16,990 ft | Bourg d'Oisans, Isere, France | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/la-marmotte/

France's original cyclosportive. Four Tour de France cols. 5,000m of climbing. Finish atop Alpe d'Huez. Pure Alpine suffering. Almost exclusively climbing or descending — there is virtually no flat on this route. The four cols are stacked in ascending psychological impact: the Glandon is honest, the Telegraphe is a warm-up, the Galibier is the crux, and Alpe d'Huez is the cherry on top of 163 km of pain. By the time you reach the 21 hairpins, you've already done more climbing than most sportives contain in total. Signature challenge: The Galibier at 2,642m is the crux — cycling's most famous high pass, with altitude effects, weather exposure, and brutal gradients. But the real signature is the entire package: four legendary cols stacked in a single day, finishing on Alpe d'Huez's 21 hairpins. La Marmotte is the original. Since 1982, it has set the standard for what a mountain cyclosportive should be: four genuine Tour de France cols, 5,000+ meters of climbing, and a finish atop cycling's most famous mountain. It isn't the hardest event in the world — but it might be the most perfectly designed one. The Glandon opens your legs, the Telegraphe-Galibier block breaks them, and Alpe d'Huez asks you to find something you didn't know you had. The Gold/Silver/Bronze diploma system gives you a permanent benchmark, and the knowledge that the route never changes means your time means something forever. Yes if you can sustain 8-12 hours of Alpine climbing and want the definitive mountain cycling experience. Yes if you want a permanent benchmark against one of cycling's most storied courses. Skip if you need technical terrain — this is pure road climbing. Skip if altitude above 2,600m concerns you. Non-negotiables: Long climbing endurance — 4-5 hour sustained climbing sessions in training; Altitude exposure or simulation above 2,000m; Full dress rehearsal — 5+ hours of climbing with nutrition strategy

### Mallorca 312 OK Mobility
Tier 1 | Score: 87/100 | 194 mi | 16,568 ft | Playa de Muro, Mallorca, Spain | April | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/mallorca-312/

The defining European gran fondo — 312km around cycling's most beloved island on fully closed roads. Mallorca 312 is the event that proved a gran fondo could be as culturally significant as any professional race. It is not the hardest 312 kilometers you will ever ride — the roads are perfect, the support is immaculate, and the Mediterranean provides a backdrop that makes suffering feel almost romantic. But make no mistake: 312 kilometers with 5,050 meters of climbing will find every weakness in your preparation. The front-loaded elevation through Serra de Tramuntana is designed to break you before the halfway mark, and the flat eastern sections that follow are not a gift — they are a test of what you have left after Puig Major has taken everything.

What separates Mallorca 312 from every other gran fondo is the combination of scale and intimacy. Eight thousand five hundred riders sounds like chaos, but the closed-road organization and wave starts create an experience that feels personal. You climb alongside riders from 60 countries, share feed stations with people whose names you will never know but whose suffering you understand completely, and cross the finish line on a beach at sunset knowing you just rode around an entire island. Alberto Contador has done this ride. Miguel Indurain has done this ride. And when you finish, you understand exactly why they keep coming back.

The only honest criticism is access. The event sells out in minutes, turning registration day into its own competitive event. If you get an entry, do not waste it. Train for the distance, respect the climbs, pack layers for the mountains, and arrive in Mallorca early enough to shake out your legs on the same roads you will race. This is one of the great single-day cycling experiences on the planet, and it earns that reputation every April. If you have ever dreamed of riding around an entire Mediterranean island in a single day, this is the one. You need a genuine endurance base — 312km with 5,000m of climbing is not a stretch goal you wing on enthusiasm. Train for 6+ hour rides, respect the Serra de Tramuntana climbs, and register the moment entries open because you will not get a second chance. This event is worth traveling for, worth training for, and worth the suffering. Non-negotiables: Multiple 6+ hour endurance rides completed before race day; Sustained climbing preparation with back-to-back mountain efforts; Fueling strategy tested and refined on training rides of 10+ hours

### Torino Nice Rally
Tier 1 | Score: 87/100 | 435 mi | 52,493 ft | Turin, Italy to Nice, France | September | bikepacking
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/torino-nice-rally/

The quintessential European bikepacking pilgrimage - 700km crossing the Alps on legendary military roads for 25 EUR. A 700km pilgrimage across the Maritime Alps from Turin's Piazza Giambattista Bodoni to Nice's Rifugio Massacan. The route weaves between Italy and France across 10+ major cols, alternating between legendary Tour de France climbs and historic military gravel roads. 65% paved alpine roads, 35% gravel including the sublime Gardetta Pass 'Little Peru' and Via del Sale finale. Every day takes riders over passes above 2,000m. The sweet spot between 2,000-3,000m where peaks escape trees and sky becomes more blue. Signature challenge: The complete Alpine crossing - 700km, 16,000m+ climbing, legendary military roads, self-supported through the Maritime Alps from Italy to France. The Torino Nice Rally represents bikepacking at its purest. 700km crossing the Maritime Alps from Italy to France on legendary military roads, fully self-supported, for 25 EUR. It's not a race - it's a pilgrimage. The Gardetta Pass 'Little Peru' section is described as possibly the most mesmerizing gravel ride in the world. Via del Sale's finale is 30km of dream gravel. The refugio culture, the rolling community, the charity mission for Smart Shelter Foundation - this is what the bikepacking ethos looks like when executed with soul. Founded with Tour Divide inspiration, TNR has earned its legendary status. Yes if you want authentic bikepacking adventure without commercial racing pressure. Yes if you appreciate community, charity, and soul over competition. Yes if you're prepared for multi-day Alpine self-sufficiency. Skip if you need race infrastructure and support. Skip if rocky military roads will destroy your pure gravel setup.

### Big Sugar Gravel
Tier 1 | Score: 86/100 | 100 mi | 9,500 ft | Bentonville, Arkansas | October | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/big-sugar/

Fall gravel perfection—stunning scenery, world-class town, legitimately hard course. Relentless punchy climbing with technical descents. The Ozarks don't have long climbs—they have endless short ones. Signature challenge: The accumulated vertical. 9,500 feet sounds manageable until you realize it comes in 50+ individual climbs. Death by a thousand punches. Big Sugar takes everything great about fall gravel—colors, weather, community—and combines it with a legitimately hard course. The Ozarks' endless punchy terrain creates a unique challenge. Add Bentonville's cycling culture and you've got a complete experience. Yes if you love punchy terrain and want a complete cycling destination experience. Maybe not if you're strictly a flat-course diesel.

### Gravel Earth Series
Tier 1 | Score: 86/100 | 208 mi | 12,139 ft | Global (HQ: Girona, Spain) | February | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gravel-earth/

Europe's premier gravel circuit—global adventure racing from Girona to Kenya to Iceland, built on passion over profit. A global circuit that delivers genuine adventure across continents. The Traka offers Mediterranean gravel with sea views and Catalan forest trails. Migration throws riders into Kenya's safari lands with wildlife encounters and Maasai villages. Iceland provides volcanic otherworldliness. The Oregon Trail delivers American multi-day stage racing. Each event is distinct, but all share the GES ethos: adventure over pure competition. Signature challenge: The sheer variety—committing to GES means racing Mediterranean forests, African safari lands, volcanic Iceland, and American mountain ranges. No other series delivers this breadth of adventure. Gravel Earth Series represents the soul of gravel racing—adventure, community, and global exploration rather than pure competition. Where Life Time offers production polish and prize money, GES offers riding with giraffes in Kenya, volcanic rifts in Iceland, and Mediterranean forests in Girona. It's 'more of an experience than a competition,' as founder Cristina Freixes puts it. The 2026 consolidation (from 23 to 12 events) and calendar controversies suggest growing pains, but the core experience remains world-class. Yes if you want adventure beyond typical gravel racing and value experience over prize money. Yes if you're European-based or seeking unique global destinations. Yes if community vibe matters more than production polish. Skip if you need significant prize purses, prefer USA-based racing, or can't handle the travel logistics and expenses.

### Haute Route Alps
Tier 1 | Score: 86/100 | 502 mi | 63,170 ft | Nice to Thonon-les-Bains, French Alps / Italy | August | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/haute-route-alps/

Seven stages. 800 km. 19,000 meters. Cols above 2,700m. The definitive amateur stage race across the Alps. A 7-stage point-to-point crossing of the Alps, alternating between massive road stages (100-182 km with 2,500-4,020m climbing each) and an uphill individual time trial. The route changes annually but consistently features the most iconic and highest cols in the French and Italian Alps. The 2026 edition runs Nice to Thonon-les-Bains, opening with a brutal 182 km / 4,020m stage crossing into Italy via the Col de la Lombarde, and continuing through the Agnel, Izoard, Vars, Bonette, and other legendary passes. Each day is a standalone epic; together they form the most demanding week in amateur cycling. Signature challenge: The Col d'Agnel at 2,744m is the physical peak — one of the highest paved passes in the Alps, where oxygen debt compounds the climbing effort. But the Haute Route's true signature challenge is the cumulative toll: 19,000+ meters of climbing spread across 7 consecutive days, with no rest days. By stage 5, even flat roads feel like climbs. The event tests not just fitness but the ability to recover, refuel, and race again when every fiber demands rest. The Haute Route Alps is the closest an amateur cyclist can get to riding a professional Grand Tour. Seven stages across the Alps, 800 km, 19,000+ meters of climbing, cols above 2,700m, timed stages with a General Classification, professional support from start to finish. It is expensive. It is brutally hard. It will break you at least once during the week. And it will deliver cycling experiences that nothing else in the amateur calendar can match. The Col d'Agnel at 2,744m on stage 2, the Casse Deserte of the Izoard, the Bonette standing alone above 2,700m — these are not just climbs, they are the cathedrals of European cycling, and you ride them consecutively for a week. The Haute Route doesn't apologize for its price or its difficulty. It simply delivers the definitive Alpine cycling experience. Yes if you want the ultimate amateur cycling challenge — 7 days of Alpine stage racing with professional infrastructure. Yes if you can commit the time, training, and budget for a premium event. Skip if the EUR2,099 entry fee (before travel and accommodation) is prohibitive. Skip if you prefer single-day events — the multi-day format is a fundamentally different challenge. Non-negotiables: Multi-day endurance base — consecutive 100+ km mountain days in training; Altitude preparation — exposure or acclimatization above 2,500m; Stage-race recovery protocol — nutrition, sleep, and fueling strategy tested across multi-day efforts

### Paris-Brest-Paris Randonneur
Tier 1 | Score: 86/100 | 757 mi | 38,550 ft | Rambouillet to Brest and back, France | August | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/paris-brest-paris/

The oldest cycling event on earth. 1,200 km from Paris to the Atlantic and back. Since 1891. A 1,219 km journey through the heart of France. The route rolls southwest from Rambouillet through the gentle hills of Normandy and the Perche, then west through Brittany's increasingly rolling interior to the Atlantic coast at Brest. The return follows a similar path. No single climb is memorable. No section is particularly technical. The character emerges from the accumulation — 11,750 meters of relentless undulation over 757 miles, three nights of riding, and the psychological weight of knowing you are doing what cyclists have done since 1891. Signature challenge: Not a single climb or section — it is the totality. 1,219 km, 11,750m of climbing, 90 hours, three nights, sleep deprivation, and the knowledge that 130 years of cyclists have faced the same road. PBP's challenge is existential, not physical. Paris-Brest-Paris is the event that started it all. Not gravel, not trail, not even road racing — just pure, distilled endurance cycling as it has existed since 1891. The concept is beautifully simple: ride from Paris to the Atlantic and back within 90 hours. No rankings. No podium. Just a medal and your name in the Great Book if you make it. What makes PBP extraordinary is not the terrain — it is rolling French countryside, pleasant rather than dramatic. It is the scale: 1,200 km of continuous effort, the sleep deprivation, the hallucinations, the 3 AM moments in Brittany where you question every decision that led you here. And then the finish, where you realize you have joined 26,000 people across 130 years who did the exact same thing. Yes if you are a committed endurance cyclist willing to invest two years in qualification and training. Yes if the history and community of cycling matter to you. Yes if you want to join the most international cycling event in the world. Skip if you want dramatic terrain, technical challenge, or competitive rankings. Skip if you cannot commit to the multi-year qualification pathway. And skip if you cannot handle the idea of riding through the night for three consecutive nights. Non-negotiables: Complete Super Randonneur series (200/300/400/600 km brevets) in qualification year; Night riding proficiency with tested lighting systems; Sleep deprivation rehearsal during a 600 km brevet or equivalent

### Marcha Cicloturista Quebrantahuesos
Tier 1 | Score: 86/100 | 123 mi | 11,483 ft | Sabinanigo, Huesca, Spain | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/quebrantahuesos/

Spain's legendary bone breaker. 198 km. Four Pyrenean cols. Two countries. 11,000 riders. Named after the bird that shatters bones from the sky. Quebrantahuesos is Spain's cycling soul distilled into a single day. Born in 1991 when a handful of riders from Pena Ciclista Edelweiss decided to build their own Marmotte in the Pyrenees, it has grown into an 11,000-rider institution that shuts down roads across two countries and draws cycling royalty — Indurain, Valverde, Zubeldia — to ride alongside club cyclists and weekend warriors. The name says it all: the bearded vulture, the bone breaker, the bird that drops bones from great heights to crack them open. This event does the same to your legs. Yes if you want the definitive Pyrenean cycling experience and can sustain 7-12 hours of mountain climbing. Yes if you want to ride the same cols as Indurain and Valverde alongside 9,000 other cyclists on fully closed roads. Yes if you've done La Marmotte and want its Pyrenean counterpart. Skip if lottery frustration will ruin your planning (backup options exist through tour operators). Skip if you need technical off-road terrain — this is pure road climbing. Non-negotiables: Long climbing endurance — 4-5 hour sustained climbing sessions in training; Steep gradient training — repeated efforts at 10-13% gradients; Full dress rehearsal — 5+ hours of climbing with complete nutrition strategy

### SBT GRVL
Tier 1 | Score: 86/100 | 108 mi | 8,189 ft | Steamboat Springs, Colorado | June | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/steamboat-gravel/

Champagne gravel at altitude with a three-day festival vibe that somehow doesn't ruin the racing. SBT GRVL serves up 108 miles of Colorado's famous 'champagne gravel' - smooth, fast surfaces that seduce you into thinking this will be easy. The 8,189 feet of climbing happens gradually across working ranch land, aspen groves, and sage-covered hills, with 70% gravel that ranges from hero dirt to loose chunky sections that'll humble your bike handling. Signature challenge: The thing nobody tells you about SBT GRVL: champagne gravel is fast until it isn't. The deceptively smooth surfaces let you burn matches early while the altitude and relentless rolling terrain compound every tactical mistake. By mile 80, that silky gravel feels like riding through wet cement, and you've got 28 miles to contemplate your pacing choices. If you can hold 20+ mph for five hours at 7,000 feet, the Black course rewards fitness with buttery gravel and legitimate competition. The 8 mph cutoff sounds generous until you're grinding into headwinds at altitude. Come undertrained and you'll get pulled to a shorter course, which isn't the worst fate given the scenery.

### We Ride Flanders / Ronde van Vlaanderen Cyclo
Tier 1 | Score: 86/100 | 150 mi | 7,415 ft | Antwerp to Oudenaarde, Belgium | April | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/tour-of-flanders-sportive/

Cycling's greatest one-day classic, ridden by 16,000 amateurs on cobblestones and bergs the day before the pros. The Tour of Flanders Sportive is the ultimate expression of Classics cycling culture. 16,000 riders on the cobblestones and bergs that define the sport's most iconic one-day race — the day before the pros do it. The Koppenberg at 22% on narrow cobbles, the Paterberg's vicious kick, the Oude Kwaremont's grinding 2.2 km cobbled drag, and the flat Flemish crosswinds between bergs — it is everything that makes cycling in Belgium unlike anywhere else on Earth. Add Belgian fans offering you beer on the climbs and a proper Flemish celebration at the finish in Oudenaarde, and you have the most complete sportive experience in Northern Europe.

De Ronde van Vlaanderen is not just a bicycle race. It is a cultural institution — a celebration of Flemish identity that dates to 1913, when journalist Karel Van Wijnendaele created a race to run entirely on Flemish soil. The amateur sportive, running since 1999, carries that weight. When you grind up the Koppenberg's narrow cobbled lane with spectators packed in shoulder to shoulder, screaming encouragement in Flemish and pouring beer, you are participating in something larger than a bike ride. You are part of Belgium's national sport at its most visceral.

The logistics are professional, the distances accommodate everyone from casual tourists (80 km) to masochists (242 km from Antwerp), and the post-race celebration is world-class. But the real value proposition is simple: ride the cobblestones on Saturday, then stand on the Kwaremont on Sunday and watch Van der Poel or Pogacar attack on the exact same stones you suffered on 24 hours earlier. No other sporting event offers that connection between amateur and professional. If you call yourself a cyclist and you have not ridden the Ronde, you are missing something fundamental about the sport. Yes if you love Classics cycling and want the most atmospheric sportive in the world. Yes if you can handle cobblestones and bergs in cold, wet conditions. Yes if the idea of riding the same roads as Merckx, Cancellara, and Van der Poel gives you chills. Skip if bad weather ruins your ride — this IS bad-weather cycling. Skip if your bike handling skills on rough surfaces are genuinely weak — wet cobbles punish hesitation and inexperience. Non-negotiables: Cobblestone riding practice — bike handling on rough, uneven surfaces; Short steep climbing power — repeated 1-3 minute all-out efforts; Wet weather gear and bike setup tested in training

### Gravel Worlds
Tier 1 | Score: 84/100 | 150 mi | 10,000 ft | Lincoln, Nebraska (Sandhills Global Event Center) | August | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gravel-worlds/

The OG grassroots gravel race—where the spirit of the sport lives on in Nebraska summer heat. Long, hot, and humbling. NOT flat—'hill after hill after hill' averaging 4-5% grade either up or down the entire 150 miles through iconic cornfields. 10K+ feet of climbing with no single killer climb, just relentless rolling. Mud possible in wet years. Signature challenge: The heat. August in Nebraska is oppressive. Heat adaptation isn't optional—it's survival. Gravel Worlds represents what the sport was before corporate money arrived. It's authentic, community-driven, and legitimately hard. The 'World Championship' name started as a joke but the race earned its reputation through genuine challenge. Yes if you value authenticity, community, and genuine challenge over polish. Yes if you can handle August Midwest heat. Not if you need corporate production value.

### L'Etape du Tour de France
Tier 1 | Score: 84/100 | 106 mi | 17,700 ft | Route changes annually (2026: Bourg d'Oisans to Alpe d'Huez, French Alps) | July | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/letape-du-tour/

The official Tour de France amateur stage. ASO-organized. Closed roads. The Galibier. Alpe d'Huez. 5,400m of climbing. Pure Alpine mountain warfare on the world's most famous cycling roads. The 2026 route is a five-climb monster: Croix de Fer opens the day with 29 km of climbing, Telegraphe and Galibier form the iconic block at mid-course, and Alpe d'Huez finishes the job when you have nothing left. The roads are immaculate — inspected and resurfaced for the professional Tour. The scenery is staggering. The suffering is real. Signature challenge: The Galibier at 2,642m — one of the highest paved passes in the Alps, the single most iconic climb in Tour de France history, and the crux of the 2026 L'Etape. But the real challenge is the accumulation: five climbs, 5,400m of vertical, and a finish on Alpe d'Huez's 21 hairpins when your body is completely spent. L'Etape du Tour is not a ride inspired by the Tour de France. It IS the Tour de France — the amateur edition, run by ASO since 1993. You ride the actual stage route, on fully closed roads, one week before the professional peloton covers the same ground. The 2026 edition delivers a brutal day: Croix de Fer, Telegraphe, Galibier, and a finish on Alpe d'Huez's 21 hairpins. 5,400 meters of climbing. 170 km. And the knowledge that every switchback, every summit, every meter of road will be on television the following week. Yes if you want the most prestigious name in cycling attached to your accomplishment. Yes if 5,400m of Alpine climbing and a finish on Alpe d'Huez is your bucket list. Skip if you want a fixed route to benchmark against year after year — L'Etape changes annually. Skip if you want intimate — 15,000 entries means crowds. Non-negotiables: Sustained climbing endurance — multiple 90+ minute climbs in a single ride; Altitude preparation above 2,000m; Nutrition strategy tested over 6+ hours of climbing

### Dirty Reiver
Tier 1 | Score: 83/100 | 130 mi | 14,100 ft | Kielder, Northumberland, England | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/dirty-reiver/

Britain's original gravel race where 4,300 meters of climbing meets technical terrain and British weather that doesn't negotiate. Classic British suffering. Cold, wet, hilly, and beautiful. Kielder Forest doesn't negotiate. Signature challenge: The combination: 4,300 meters of climbing on technical terrain in British weather. Any one factor is manageable. Together, they break riders. Dirty Reiver is what happens when you take American gravel ambition and drop it in British conditions. The result is a genuinely hard event that tests fitness, skills, and weather resilience. If you want to understand UK gravel culture, you do Dirty Reiver. Yes, if British gravel interests you. Dirty Reiver is non-negotiable for UK gravel credibility. Just be ready for the registration sprint, the weather roulette, and the technical terrain that separates prepared riders from hopeful ones.

### Gravel Locos
Tier 1 | Score: 83/100 | 157 mi | 7,884 ft | Hico, Texas | May | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gravel-locos/

Free 150-mile Unbound tune-up on gorgeous Texas gravel where Texas heat is the great equalizer. 'The scenery around Hico was stunning, adorned with wildflowers, and the gravel paths were as smooth as other riders had mentioned.' Difficulty is about relentless rollers, heat, and length - not technical terrain. Signature challenge: The heat-soaked late race is the defining suffer zone. In normal years, cumulative rollers plus Texas heat near the end are what everyone talks about. In 2022, riders literally ran out of water. Gravel Locos is what happens when you make a 150-mile Unbound tune-up free. Pros use it as their final rehearsal. Amateurs get to ride with them on gorgeous Texas gravel for zero entry fee. The catch? Texas heat that can hit 120°F and aid stations that occasionally run dry. Yes if you want a legitimate ultra-gravel experience for free with international competition and legendary Texas hospitality. Gravel Locos proves you don't need a $250 entry fee to have a world-class gravel race. Just train for heat.

### Haute Route Dolomites
Tier 1 | Score: 83/100 | 280 mi | 47,572 ft | Cortina d'Ampezzo / Bormio, Dolomites, Italy | August | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/haute-route-dolomites/

450 km and 14,500m of climbing through the Stelvio, Gavia, and the full Dolomite pass collection in 5 days — the most demanding amateur stage race in Italy. A multi-stage traverse of the Italian Dolomites and eastern Alps, linking the glamour of Cortina d'Ampezzo to the alpine spa town of Bormio through the most legendary passes in Italian cycling. The route deliberately targets the giants — Stelvio, Gavia, Pordoi, Falzarego — and connects them with lesser-known climbs that are equally demanding but less crowded. Each stage averages 90 km with 2,500-3,500m of climbing, creating a daily rhythm of sustained mountain suffering punctuated by breathtaking descents through UNESCO World Heritage Dolomite scenery. Signature challenge: The Stelvio from Bormio: 21.5 km at 7.1% with 48 switchbacks to 2,758m — arriving on Stage 4 with 10,000+ meters of climbing already in the legs and 12-18% less oxygen at the summit. The highest paved pass in the Eastern Alps, climbed when you are at your most depleted. The Haute Route Dolomites is the most demanding amateur multi-day cycling event in Italy. 450 km. 14,500 meters of climbing. 5 stages through the Stelvio (2,758m), Gavia (2,621m), Pordoi, Falzarego, and the full catalogue of Dolomite passes that define the Giro d'Italia. This is not a tourism ride with mountains in the background — it is a professional-format stage race where the mountains are trying to break you.

The Stelvio is the signature: 21.5 km at 7.1% with 48 switchbacks on the Bormio side, summiting at 2,758m where the air has 12-18% less oxygen. The Gavia is the wild card: 17.3 km at 7.9% on a narrow, rough road through alpine wilderness that feels more like an expedition than a bike race. The Dolomite stages deliver the visual drama — vertical limestone towers, meadow-filled valleys, and the unique pink glow of the enrosadira at sunset. Five days of this creates a complete experience that is equal parts athletic challenge and mountain pilgrimage.

The Haute Route organization makes it possible. Feed stations, mechanical support, timing, luggage transport, and post-stage meals remove the logistical complexity that would otherwise make this route inaccessible to amateur riders. The 200-400 rider field creates genuine stage-race camaraderie — by the final stage, you have shared enough suffering to build real bonds. The price is high, the climbing is relentless, and the altitude is genuine. But the experience of riding the Stelvio and Gavia in a professionally supported stage race through UNESCO World Heritage Dolomite scenery is worth every euro and every meter of vertical gain. Ride this if you are a serious mountain cyclist seeking a multi-day challenge through the most iconic passes in Italian cycling. Skip it if you are on a tight budget, cannot commit to months of altitude-specific climbing preparation, or prefer gravel/mixed terrain. Confirm the event is scheduled before planning — Haute Route's calendar has evolved. Non-negotiables: Multi-day mountain endurance base; Altitude acclimatization strategy; Cold-weather riding capability

### L'Ardéchoise Cyclosportive
Tier 1 | Score: 83/100 | 172 mi | 17,618 ft | Saint-Félicien, Ardèche, France | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/lardechoise/

Europe's largest mountain cycling gathering. 15,000 riders. 160 villages. 9,000 volunteers. The Ardèche becomes a cycling festival. Relentless Massif Central mid-mountain grinding. The cols here are not the Alpine monsters of the Tour de France — they're 8-14 km climbs averaging 5-7%, stacked 16 deep on the marathon route. The cumulative fatigue is the weapon. Each climb alone is manageable; the 16th one, after 240 km, is not. Between the cols, narrow departmental roads wind through chestnut forests, volcanic landscapes, and the Cévennes foothills. But the course character is defined as much by the villages as the terrain: every 10-15 km, you ride into a decorated village square with food, music, and cheering locals. The temptation to stop at every village ravitaillement is real — and is why the marathon route takes 10-14 hours. Signature challenge: The Col de la Barricaude is the flagship climb — 14 km of sustained Cévennes grinding that separates the marathon riders from the tourists. But the real signature challenge is cumulative: 16 cols across 276 km means you never fully recover. Every descent leads to another climb. The mountains are not individually famous, but collectively they are devastating. L'Ardéchoise is not the hardest cyclosportive in Europe. It is not the most prestigious. It does not have the highest mountains or the most famous climbs. What it has — and what no other event on the continent can match — is soul. When 9,000 volunteers from 160 villages collectively decide to throw 15,000 cyclists the greatest party in European cycling, something transcendent happens. The Massif Central cols are relentless, the Cévennes scenery is gorgeous, and the marathon route's 276 km with 16 cols is genuinely brutal. But L'Ardéchoise isn't really about the cols. It's about the grandmother in a village of 200 people who has been making cakes for passing cyclists since 1992. It's about the accordion player at the feed station at kilometer 180. It's about arriving in a decorated village square, exhausted and sun-baked, and being handed a cold drink by someone who has been waiting all day just to see you ride past. This is what cycling is for. Yes if you want the most authentic, community-driven cycling experience in Europe. Yes if you want incredible value and route flexibility from 125 km to 618 km. Yes if you care about atmosphere as much as athleticism. Skip if you want iconic Alpine climbs — the Massif Central is stunning but lacks the Tour de France cachet. Skip if getting to rural Ardèche is logistically prohibitive. Non-negotiables: Long-day endurance — at least two 8+ hour rides in training; Heat acclimatization and hydration strategy for sustained effort in 28-32C; Nutrition plan tested over 200+ km with frequent stops

### The Mid South
Tier 1 | Score: 83/100 | 100 mi | 6,000 ft | Stillwater, Oklahoma | March | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/mid-south/

The early-season gravel race with the biggest heart and the most unpredictable conditions. Fast and tactical when dry. Unrideable mud slog when wet. The weather decides what race you're getting. Signature challenge: The weather lottery. You train for months but Oklahoma decides what race you're riding. The Mid South is what happens when someone builds a gravel race around unreasonable hospitality instead of unreasonable suffering. Bobby Wintle hugs every finisher. The DFL gets the biggest trophy. The community shows up like their lives depend on it. And the weather? The weather does whatever Oklahoma wants. When it's dry, you get a blazing-fast tactical race. When it's wet, you get an unrideable mud nightmare. Either way, you finish to live music, local beer, and a town that genuinely wants you there. This isn't the hardest gravel race. It's not the longest. But it might be the one with the biggest heart. If you value community, celebration, and authentic hospitality over predictable racing conditions—yes, absolutely. If you need to know what you're getting into before you commit—maybe reconsider. The Mid South is chaos wrapped in a Bobby hug. Some years it's fast and perfect. Some years it's a mud disaster. Some years it gets cancelled by wildfires. But the people who come back year after year aren't coming for the course—they're coming for the experience. Show up ready for anything. Leave with stories. Non-negotiables: Weather adaptation training for unpredictable conditions (cold, heat, wind); Red clay handling skills and mud preparation; Wind exposure training on exposed ridgelines

### Taiwan KOM Challenge — The Ascent to the Clouds
Tier 1 | Score: 83/100 | 65 mi | 10,745 ft | Hualien to Wuling Pass, Taroko National Park, Taiwan | October | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/taiwan-kom-challenge/

105 kilometers. Sea level to 3,275 meters. Through Taroko Gorge to the roof of Taiwan. Asia's greatest hill climb. The Taiwan KOM Challenge is a 105 km journey from the Pacific Ocean to the roof of Taiwan — a single, continuous ascent from sea level to 3,275 meters that passes through one of the natural wonders of Asia. The first 18.5 km is a neutral rollout along the coast from Qixingtan Beach to the Taroko Gorge entrance, where the official timing begins. From there, the road enters a different world: Taroko Gorge's marble walls rise hundreds of meters on either side, the Liwu River carves through the canyon floor far below, tunnels pierce the rock, and red bridges span impossible gaps. The gradient through the gorge is steady — 5-7% for roughly 50 km — and the scenery is so extraordinary that first-time riders forget they are in a race. Past Tianxiang, the gorge opens into forested mountain valleys. The road continues climbing through temperate forest and alpine meadow. Then comes Dayuling at 2,565m — the psychological halfway point of the high-altitude suffering. The final 10 km from Dayuling to Wuling Pass delivers the coup de grace: switchbacks averaging 17% with pitches to 27%, at an altitude where your lungs can extract only 70% of the oxygen they are used to. The summit at 3,275m is above the cloud line more often than not. You rode there from the beach. Signature challenge: The final 10 km from Dayuling to Wuling Pass. The combination of extreme gradient (17% average, 27% max) and genuine high altitude (2,565-3,275m) on legs that have already climbed for 3-5 hours creates a physiological crisis that no amount of sea-level training fully prepares you for. The oxygen deficit compounds the muscular fatigue. The cold compounds both. This is where the Taiwan KOM separates itself from every other hill climb on Earth — it does not merely climb high, it climbs brutally to get there. The Taiwan KOM Challenge is one of the most extraordinary single-day efforts in competitive cycling. From the moment you roll off Qixingtan Beach at dawn, the Pacific Ocean still audible behind you, the road points upward and does not relent for 87 timed kilometers. Through Taroko Gorge — marble walls hundreds of meters high, tunnels carved through the mountain by hand, bridges spanning the Liwu River far below — the road climbs steadily, almost gently, lulling you into believing the gradient is manageable. Then the gorge ends, the valley opens, and the switchbacks begin. The final 10 kilometers to Wuling Pass — where gradients average 17% and spike to 27% at an altitude where the air holds 70% of the oxygen your lungs are accustomed to — are among the hardest paved kilometers in world cycling. When you reach 3,275 meters, you are standing on the highest paved road in Taiwan, above the cloud line, and you rode there from the beach. WorldTour Grand Tour winners have raced this course. The record stands at 3:16:09. Most riders take 4-6 hours. All of them remember it forever. Yes if you want one of the world's most spectacular single-day climbing challenges — a ride from the Pacific Ocean to 3,275m through marble canyons and cloud forests. Yes if racing alongside WorldTour professionals in a mass-start field of 40+ nationalities appeals to you. Yes if Taiwan's extraordinary food, culture, and hospitality are as compelling as the climbing. Skip if high altitude (3,275m) intimidates you and you have no acclimatization plan. Skip if the logistical complexity of traveling to eastern Taiwan is a dealbreaker. Skip if the event's vulnerability to weather and earthquake disruption makes you uneasy about booking flights months in advance. Non-negotiables: High-altitude climbing simulation — at least two training sessions at or above 2,500m, or altitude tent acclimatization protocol; Sustained climbing endurance — at least two training rides with 3+ hours of continuous climbing; Gearing for 27% gradients at altitude — test your lowest gear under load

### Belgian Waffle Ride Cedar City
Tier 1 | Score: 81/100 | 127 mi | 6,500 ft | Cedar City, Utah | June | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/bwr-cedar-city/

Half the climbing of San Diego. All the suffering. Plus miles of playground sand and a soul-crushing mile-100 combo. '125 miles. 6k of climbing. 75% dirt. Starting at 5,600 ft. Technical riding and sand. Dry weather at elevation. Demoralizing.' The first 3.5 hours average 23 mph. The last 25 miles average pain. Signature challenge: The mile-100 sandy climb leading straight into rocky/washboarded descending is the signature 'soul-crusher' that repeatedly appears in every race report. BWR Cedar City is what happens when you take the Waffle formula and add playground sand, mile-100 suffer-climbs, and Utah weather roulette. Half the climbing of San Diego but arguably more suffering because of where the difficulty is concentrated. The first 3.5 hours at 23 mph feel sustainable. The last 25 miles feel eternal. Yes if you want a different BWR challenge that tests sand navigation, weather adaptability, and late-race mental fortitude. Cedar City punishes under-prepared riders more than they expect. Come with 40mm+ tires, cold-weather layers, and a pacing plan that saves matches for mile 100.

### Squadron Energy Grafton to Inverell Cycle Classic
Tier 1 | Score: 81/100 | 142 mi | 11,096 ft | Grafton to Inverell, New South Wales, Australia | March | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/grafton-to-inverell-classic/

228 km from the Clarence Valley to the New England Tablelands, climbing 3,382 metres through World Heritage rainforest on Gibraltar Range. Australia's toughest and most historic one-day road race since 1961. A 228 km point-to-point journey from sea level in the subtropical Clarence Valley to the New England Tablelands at 580 m, crossing the Great Dividing Range via the iconic Gibraltar Range. The course follows the Gwydir Highway westward from Grafton — a two-lane highway that serves as the only direct road link between the coast and the northern tablelands. The character shifts dramatically across the race: flat, humid valley roads give way to the sustained ascent of Gibraltar Range through ancient World Heritage rainforest, then open out onto the rolling, exposed grasslands of the New England Highlands. The final 100 km from Glen Innes to Inverell is deceptively demanding — constant rolling terrain with no flat road and the ever-present threat of headwinds across the open tablelands. Wire Gully at 20 km to go is the tactical crux — the last categorised climb where every decisive move in the race's history has been made. The descent into Inverell is fast, the finish on Vivian Street is decisive, and the applause of Inverell's residents is the reward for 228 km of suffering. Signature challenge: Gibraltar Range — 16.3 km at 5.7% average gradient through World Heritage-listed Gondwana Rainforest, cresting at approximately 1,100 m with 140 km still remaining. The longest sustained climb in Australian one-day road racing. You cannot win the race on the Range, but you can certainly lose it. The Grafton to Inverell Cycle Classic is the race that Australian road cyclists measure themselves against. Two hundred and twenty-eight kilometres from the subtropical Clarence Valley to the New England Tablelands, climbing 3,382 metres including the legendary 16.3 km ascent of Gibraltar Range through World Heritage-listed Gondwana Rainforest. Running since 1961 — when 35 riders tackled 110 km of gravel on the newly opened Gwydir Highway — it has spent 65 years earning its reputation as Australia's toughest one-day road race. That reputation is not marketing. It is earned in every pedal stroke of every edition.

The race was a UCI international event in the 1980s, attracting teams from New Zealand, Great Britain, and continental Europe. Paul Curran (GB) set a course record of 6:00:49 in 1985 that stood for 26 years until Mark Jamieson broke it by 28 seconds in 2011. The roll call of winners and participants includes riders who competed in the Olympics and the Tour de France. Today it is a round of the National Road Series and the ProVelo Super League, attracting Australia's best domestic teams and riders. But the Grafton is not defined by its elite racing alone. It is defined by the mountain — Gibraltar Range, rising relentlessly through ancient rainforest for 40 minutes of sustained effort, cresting at 1,100 metres with 140 km still remaining. You cannot win the race on the Range, the saying goes. But you can certainly lose it.

The experience is singular. You ride through three distinct geographic zones in a single day: humid coastal valley, temperate mountain rainforest, and open highland tableland. The temperature drops, the vegetation transforms, and the landscape opens up as you cross from one side of the Great Dividing Range to the other. Wire Gully at 20 km to go is where the race explodes — the launchpad for every decisive attack. The fast descent into Inverell and the finish on Vivian Street are earned, not gifted. This is not a sportive. This is a proper bike race with 65 years of history behind it, and every rider who finishes knows exactly what they have done. Yes if you are a competitive road cyclist who wants to test yourself against Australia's hardest one-day race — 228 km with serious climbing and genuine NRS-level racing. Yes if you want to ride a race with 65 years of unbroken history through some of the most dramatic terrain in New South Wales. Skip if you are not prepared for 228 km and 3,382 m of climbing. Skip if the remote logistics of regional NSW are a dealbreaker. Skip if you need a feed station every 30 km — self-sufficiency is part of the challenge. Non-negotiables: Complete at least two training rides of 180+ km with 2,500+ metres of climbing; Practice race-day nutrition delivering 60-90g carbohydrates per hour for 7+ hours; Recce or study the Gibraltar Range descent — know the road, the corners, and the surface

### Haute Route Pyrenees
Tier 1 | Score: 81/100 | 365 mi | 47,570 ft | Biarritz to Saint-Lary-Soulan (2024) / Girona to Pau (varies by year), French-Spanish Pyrenees | July | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/haute-route-pyrenees/

Five stages. 580 km. 14,500 meters. Tourmalet, Aubisque, Portet. The Haute Route brand meets the most legendary mountain range in cycling. The Haute Route Pyrenees is the Haute Route formula applied to the most storied mountain range in professional cycling. Five stages across the Pyrenees, 580+ km, 14,500 meters of climbing, and every col that ever broke a Tour de France contender — Tourmalet, Aubisque, Portet, Peyresourde. It is expensive. It is punishingly hard. And it delivers a cycling experience that only the Alps edition of the same brand can rival. The Pyrenees have a different character than the Alps — wilder, more remote, steeper and more relentless in gradient, with a raw beauty that the more developed Alpine resorts have lost. The Tourmalet from the east at 7.4% average for 17 km is arguably a harder climb than anything on the Alps route below 2,300m.

The 5-day format is both a strength and a limitation. It is more accessible than the 7-day Alps — fewer vacation days, lower entry fee, marginally less cumulative damage. But it lacks the soul-crushing seventh-day delirium that makes the Alps edition legendary. You finish the Pyrenees edition battered and proud; you finish the Alps edition fundamentally changed. That said, for many riders, the Pyrenees is the better event: the cols carry more Tour de France mythology per kilometer than anywhere else on earth, the route crosses three countries, and the closing ceremony in Pau's gilded Palais Beaumont is a fitting conclusion to an epic mountain crossing. Yes if you want the premium amateur stage-race experience across the Pyrenees — professional infrastructure on Tour de France roads. Yes if the 5-day format suits your schedule better than the 7-day Alps. Skip if the EUR1,399 entry fee (before travel and accommodation) is prohibitive. Skip if you want the absolute pinnacle — the Haute Route Alps is still the flagship. And check availability: the 2025-2026 editions are not currently scheduled. Non-negotiables: Multi-day climbing endurance — back-to-back 100+ km mountain days in training; Steep climbing specificity — sustained 7-9% gradient training; Stage-race recovery protocol — nutrition, sleep, and fueling strategy tested across multi-day efforts

### Jeroboam
Tier 1 | Score: 81/100 | 186 mi | 23,000 ft | Erbusco, Lombardy, Italy | July | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/jeroboam/

Italy's gravel masquerading as a wine tour until 7,000 meters of climbing and mandatory night riding separate the romantics from the masochists. Jeroboam masquerades as gravel but delivers two-thirds asphalt—don't be fooled, this isn't easy road riding. The Lombard Prealps serve up Roman cobbles, vineyard doubletrack, and 30% mountain bike trails that'll have you hiking sections. Wine country never tasted this bitter. Signature challenge: The 4pm start guarantees you'll ride most of this beast in complete darkness, navigating technical terrain with a headlamp while your body shuts down. It's not the 7,000m of climbing that breaks people—it's doing it blind for 14 hours straight. Show up undertrained and the Lombard Prealps will humble you by kilometer 30. The 4pm start means you're grinding technical singletrack in the dark with dead legs, so your bike handling better be dialed. This isn't a gravel race—it's a 24-hour suffer fest through vineyards that thinks it's the Giro.

### L'Eroica
Tier 1 | Score: 81/100 | 130 mi | 12,674 ft | Gaiole in Chianti, Tuscany, Italy | October | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/leroica/

209 km of strade bianche on vintage steel through UNESCO Tuscany — the event that created a movement and remains cycling's most profound cultural experience. A circular route radiating from Gaiole in Chianti through the heart of Tuscany's most celebrated landscapes. The 209 km flagship alternates between paved roads and strade bianche — unpaved white gravel roads of galestro clay and limestone that have been the region's farm tracks for centuries. The terrain is relentlessly rolling — there is no flat section longer than 2 km on the entire route. Every gravel sector features at least one steep, punchy climb. On vintage equipment, the course is an endurance test that demands patience, pacing, and a willingness to suffer beautifully. Signature challenge: The cumulative effect: 112 km of strade bianche on vintage steel bikes with 3,800m of climbing, limited gearing, toe clips, and questionable brakes across 12+ hours of riding through some of the most beautiful terrain on Earth. No single sector breaks you — the totality does. L'Eroica is not a race. It is a religion. Founded in 1997 by Giancarlo Brocci with 92 riders and a romantic idea — relive the golden age of cycling on Tuscany's endangered gravel roads — it has grown into an 8,000-rider cultural phenomenon that sells out within minutes of registration opening. The 209 km flagship route delivers 3,800 meters of climbing across 112 km of strade bianche on mandatory vintage steel bikes with downtube shifters and toe clips. This is cycling stripped to its essence: beauty, fatigue, and conquest, wrapped in wool jerseys and Tuscan autumn light.

The event transcends cycling in ways that no other ride can claim. The ristori — refreshment stops serving Brunello di Montalcino, aged Pecorino, and fresh bread — are worth the entry fee alone. The landscapes through Val d'Orcia and Crete Senesi are UNESCO World Heritage territory. The community, organized through the Ciclo Club Eroica with chapters in 50+ countries, represents the strongest tribal identity in recreational cycling. L'Eroica did not just create an event; it created a movement that saved Tuscany's white roads from being paved and inspired the professional Strade Bianche race.

The 209 km route is no joke. Vintage equipment with limited gearing, heavy frames, and questionable brakes turns a challenging gravel course into a genuine ordeal. Rain years are legendary for their suffering — clay mud that fills tire clearance and turns descents into controlled crashes. But that suffering is exactly the point. Brocci's founding philosophy — 'the beauty of fatigue and the thrill of conquest' — is not marketing copy. It is the lived experience of every rider who finishes the long route as the sun sets over Chianti. Ride this if you care about cycling history, Tuscan culture, and the kind of suffering that comes with beauty attached. Skip it if you need a competitive format with timing and results, cannot handle the registration lottery, or dislike the idea of riding vintage equipment on gravel. If you own a modern bike and want a Tuscan gravel experience, look at Nova Eroica instead. Non-negotiables: Gravel endurance on vintage equipment; Back-to-back climbing on tired legs; Vintage equipment familiarity

### Paris-Roubaix Challenge
Tier 1 | Score: 81/100 | 106 mi | 3,600 ft | Busigny to Roubaix Velodrome, Nord, France | April | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/paris-roubaix-challenge/

The Hell of the North. 30 cobbled sectors. The Arenberg trench. Finish on the Roubaix Velodrome. One of cycling's five Monuments. Flat, exposed northern France farmland punctuated by sectors of centuries-old cobblestones. The contrast is the signature: smooth tarmac at 35 km/h, then suddenly rough pave at 20 km/h with your teeth rattling and your hands losing feeling. Each cobbled sector is rated 1-5 stars for difficulty. The three 5-star sectors — Arenberg, Mons-en-Pevele, Carrefour de l'Arbre — are cycling's most famous stretches of road. The finale: riding through a tunnel into the Roubaix Velodrome and completing a lap on the banked track. Signature challenge: The cobblestones. Not one sector — all of them. 50 km of pave spread across 30 sectors, each one resetting your fatigue and testing your bike handling. The Arenberg is the icon, but the cumulative effect of 170 km with constant surface changes is the real challenge. And then the velodrome: the reward for surviving the Hell. Paris-Roubaix Challenge is not a pleasant ride. It is 170 km of cold, wet, windswept northern France punctuated by 50 km of cobblestones so rough they break bikes, hands, and spirits. The Arenberg trench is a sunken forest road that looks like a supply route from the Western Front. The cobbles of Mons-en-Pevele and Carrefour de l'Arbre are farm tracks that haven't been properly surfaced since Napoleon. And when you finally emerge from the last sector, battered and filthy, you ride into the Roubaix Velodrome — onto the same banked track where Cancellara, Boonen, and Van der Poel have carved their names into cycling history. That moment — entering the velodrome — justifies every jarring, teeth-rattling, hand-numbing kilometer. Yes if you want to experience one of cycling's five Monuments from the saddle. Yes if cobblestones, mud, and cold are your idea of cycling at its most authentic. Skip if you want beautiful scenery — this is flat, grey northern France. Skip if your bike handling skills are weak — wet cobbles punish hesitation. Non-negotiables: Cobblestone riding experience — practice on rough, uneven surfaces; Equipment preparation — wider tires (28-32mm), lowered pressure, spare wheels; Cold-weather bike handling — practice in cold, wet conditions with thick gloves

### Gran Fondo Strade Bianche
Tier 1 | Score: 81/100 | 86 mi | 6,560 ft | Siena, Tuscany, Italy | March | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/strade-bianche-gran-fondo/

The cycling bucket list—Tuscan white roads and Piazza del Campo finish like the WorldTour pros. A mini spring classic through Tuscan countryside. The strade bianche sectors are the stars—compacted clay roads that become dusty highways when dry and grey paste when wet. Between sectors, fast paved roads with constant punchy climbs. The finish on Via Santa Caterina into Piazza del Campo is cycling theater at its finest. Signature challenge: Via Santa Caterina—the 16% cobbled finish into Piazza del Campo where you either crush it or walk in front of thousands. Gran Fondo Strade Bianche is the ride every cycling fan dreams about—the same white roads, same sectors, same Piazza del Campo finish as the WorldTour race. It's not the longest or hardest gravel event, but it might be the most romantic. The Tuscan landscape, medieval villages, and Italian cycling culture create something unique. When you hit Via Santa Caterina's 16% finish and thousands cheer, you understand why cyclists make pilgrimages here. Yes if you love cycling culture and want the iconic experience. Yes if you can handle March weather variability. Skip if you want pure gravel racing—this is Italian road cycling with gravel sectors. Non-negotiables: Gravel/mixed-surface riding comfort on white roads; Climbing endurance for 2,000m of Tuscan rollers; Wet-weather bike handling and layering strategy

### Granfondo La Fausto Coppi
Tier 1 | Score: 80/100 | 107 mi | 14,108 ft | Cuneo, Piedmont, Italy | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gran-fondo-la-fausto-coppi/

The Campionissimo's mountains. Cuneo to the summit of Colle Fauniera at 2,481 meters. Three Piedmont cols. Since 1987. A mountain marathon that begins in the elegant city of Cuneo and ventures deep into the Maritime Alps before returning. The route is defined by three major climbs — Colle di Sampeyre, Colle Fauniera, and Madonna del Colletto — connected by rolling valley roads and demanding descents. The first 20 km are flat, rolling through the Piedmont countryside. Then the road tilts upward and does not genuinely flatten again until the return approach to Cuneo. The Granfondo traverses three distinct alpine valleys — the Varaita, the Maira, and the Grana — each with its own character, before the final sting of Madonna del Colletto delivers the last 7.3 km of climbing. The terrain transitions from refined Piedmont civilization to raw Alpine wilderness and back again over 172 km. Signature challenge: Colle Fauniera: 22.3 km of climbing to 2,481 meters, with the upper half averaging 9.2% on deteriorating road surfaces through increasingly remote and wild Alpine terrain. The combination of sustained gradient, altitude, rough roads, and the psychological weight of the Pantani memorial at the summit makes the Fauniera the defining challenge of the event — and one of the most demanding single climbs accessible by gran fondo anywhere in Europe. The Gran Fondo La Fausto Coppi is one of the hardest and most historically resonant one-day cycling events in Italy. Named after the man who defined Italian cycling — five Giri, two Tours, the first to win both in the same year — the event sends 2,800 riders from Cuneo's grand Piazza Galimberti into the Maritime Alps for 172 km and 4,300m of climbing across three named cols. The centerpiece is Colle Fauniera: 22.3 km of relentless climbing to 2,481 meters, one of Italy's highest and most dramatic road passes, where the Pantani memorial at the summit bridges two eras of Italian cycling legend. This is not a tourist sportive. The climbing is Alpine in scale, the road surfaces on the Fauniera are raw and demanding, and the 10-hour time limit is generous but not a gift. Cuneo to the clouds and back, since 1987. Yes if you want genuine Alpine climbing — 4,300m of vertical including a summit at 2,481m — in a historically significant Italian granfondo. Yes if the names Fausto Coppi and Marco Pantani mean something to you and you want to ride the mountain where their legacies intersect. Yes if you are a strong climber looking for a demanding one-day event that rivals the Dolomite granfondos in vertical gain. Skip if you need polished road surfaces — the upper Fauniera is rough and remote. Skip if the limited airport access to Cuneo is a dealbreaker. Skip if 4,300m of climbing in 172 km exceeds your current fitness. Non-negotiables: Sustained steep gradient climbing — repeated 20-40 minute efforts at 8-10%; Multi-climb mountain day — minimum three major climbs totaling 3,000m+ vertical in a single ride; Full distance simulation — 160-170 km with 3,500m+ climbing, including nutrition and pacing strategy

### Maratona dles Dolomites - Enel
Tier 1 | Score: 80/100 | 86 mi | 13,878 ft | La Villa / Corvara, Alta Badia, South Tyrol, Italy | July | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/maratona-dles-dolomites/

Seven Dolomite passes. UNESCO World Heritage scenery. 9,000 lottery-selected riders. The most oversubscribed cycling event on Earth. Seven Dolomite passes strung together in a 138 km loop that starts and finishes in Corvara. The route is a continuous series of climbs and descents — there is virtually no flat. Each pass reveals a completely different Dolomite panorama: the Sella massif, the Marmolada glacier, the Cinque Torri, the Tofane. The Passo di Giau is the crux — 9.9 km at 9.3% with pitches of 14% — a genuine wall that comes at km 100 when your legs are already deep in debt. Signature challenge: The Passo di Giau: 9.9 km at 9.3%, arriving at km 100. The hardest individual climb of the day, on depleted legs, with no place to hide from the gradient. But the real signature is the cumulative effect of seven passes — 4,230m of climbing through the most beautiful mountains in the world. The Maratona dles Dolomites is not just a bike ride. It is a pilgrimage to the most beautiful mountains on Earth, earned through the uncertainty of a lottery and rewarded with seven passes through UNESCO World Heritage scenery that no photograph can capture. Founded in 1987 with 166 riders, it has become the most oversubscribed cycling event in the world — and it deserves every ounce of that demand. The climbs are honest: the Giau will break you, the Pordoi will humble you, and the descent into Corvara will make you forget every moment of suffering. Yes if you can win the lottery and want cycling's most visually spectacular day. Yes if seven Dolomite passes through scenery that defies description is your idea of paradise.
