Skip if you need guaranteed entry — the lottery is genuine. Skip if remote Italian mountain villages are logistically challenging. Non-negotiables: Repeated climbing endurance — multiple 30-45 minute climbs in a single ride; Steep gradient training — sustained 8-10% climbing; Full race simulation — 4,000m+ of climbing with nutrition plan

### Rebecca's Private Idaho
Tier 1 | Score: 80/100 | 103 mi | 8,500 ft | Ketchum, Idaho | September | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/rebeccas-private-idaho/

The gravel pilgrimage that sells itself through pure experience. Big mountain beauty with sustained but manageable climbing. Not the steepest grades, but relentless. Signature challenge: The combination of distance, altitude, and relentless terrain. Nothing is crazy steep—but nothing is flat either. RPI is what happens when a legendary athlete shares her passion with the community. It's not the hardest race, not the longest, not the most technical—but it might be the most soulful. The combination of world-class scenery, genuine community, and Rebecca's personal involvement makes this special. Yes—if you can afford the trip and want to experience gravel at its most soulful. This isn't just a race; it's a community gathering hosted by royalty.

### Sea Otter Classic Gravel
Tier 1 | Score: 80/100 | 89 mi | 8,100 ft | Monterey, California | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/sea-otter-gravel/

A gravel race wrapped in North America's biggest bike festival. Experience > competition. Fast fire roads with coastal character. More about the experience than pure gravel suffering. Signature challenge: The distraction. There's so much happening at Sea Otter that the race can feel secondary. Stay focused or get caught up in expo madness. Sea Otter gravel is best understood as part of the larger festival experience. If you want a pure gravel race, look elsewhere. If you want to race gravel AND attend North America's biggest bike expo, this is your event. Yes if you want the full Sea Otter experience and gravel is your discipline. No if you're looking for a pure gravel bucket-list race.

### Unbound Gravel 100
Tier 1 | Score: 80/100 | 104 mi | 4,000 ft | Emporia, Kansas | June | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/unbound-100/

All the Unbound prestige, all the Kansas suffering, half the distance—still not easy. A 100-mile test through the Kansas Flint Hills. Leaves Emporia traveling north through Wabaunsee and Morris Counties. One checkpoint at Council Grove (~mile 50). Same brutal flint rock roads, rolling terrain, and open prairie as the 200—just compressed into a faster, more intense package. Signature challenge: The combination of tire-destroying flint, Kansas heat, and self-sufficiency with only one checkpoint. Simple but relentless. The 100 is designed as a launchpad to the 200—and it delivers. All the Flint Hills suffering, the Unbound atmosphere, the iconic finish, but in a (slightly) more digestible package. You'll still get humbled by Kansas. You'll still earn your finisher glass. And you'll probably start eyeing the 200 the moment you cross Commercial Street. Yes if you want the world-class Unbound experience in a (relatively) more accessible package. Yes if you're building toward the 200. Skip if lottery entry or Kansas weather are deal-breakers.

### Belgian Waffle Ride San Diego
Tier 1 | Score: 79/100 | 131 mi | 12,000 ft | Del Mar, California | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/bwr-san-diego/

Belgian suffering translated to sunny San Diego, where Double Peak turns roadies into believers and flatlanders into casualties. BWR San Diego masquerades as an American Belgian classic, trading cobblestones for bone-rattling dirt sectors and sandy washes across 23 off-road segments. You'll roll through 80 miles of pavement and 52 miles of 'unroad' — everything from rocky single-track at Lake Hodges to the infamous sandy traps of Bandy Canyon. The course saves its cruelest joke for last: Double Peak, a 4.5-mile climb that turns savage in the final mile when your legs are already cooked. Signature challenge: The Muur van Dubbelberg — Double Peak's final mile averages 11% with 20-23% pitches when you've already climbed 10,500 feet. The last 800 feet of elevation come in that brutal final kilometer, forcing riders to 'paperboy' diagonally across the road just to keep moving. Your FTP means nothing here. You need legitimate climbing legs and the patience to navigate technical single-track when you're already cooked. This isn't a pure gravel race—it's a Spring Classic with dirt sectors that'll shred tires and egos equally. Show up undertrained for Double Peak's 20% final pitches and you'll be walking in front of 2,800 witnesses.

### L'Etape du Tour de France Femmes avec Zwift
Tier 1 | Score: 79/100 | 75 mi | 9,843 ft | Route changes annually (2026: Vaison-la-Romaine to Mont Ventoux, Provence) | August | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/letape-du-tour-femmes/

The official Tour de France Femmes amateur stage. ASO-organized. Closed roads. Mont Ventoux via Bedoin. 3,000m of climbing through Provence. A Provencal masterpiece that builds from gentle beauty to savage finality. The route from Vaison-la-Romaine winds through the sun-baked vineyards and lavender fields of the Comtat Venaissin, with the limestone Dentelles de Montmirail and the white dome of Mont Ventoux dominating the horizon throughout. The approach is rolling but manageable — this is the calm before the storm. Then Bedoin arrives, and the real race begins. Mont Ventoux via Bedoin is 21.8 km of relentless climbing: gentle through the village, savage through the dark forest at 9%, then the barren exposed moonscape above Chalet Reynard where the wind howls and the summit observatory beckons. One climb. 1,617 meters of vertical. No hiding. Signature challenge: Mont Ventoux via Bedoin — 21.8 km at 7.4% average. The three-phase structure is what makes Ventoux unique and devastating: gentle village approach that lulls you, the savage forest section at 9% that breaks you, and the barren exposed moonscape that finishes you. One climb. No recovery. No hiding. This is the climb that killed Tom Simpson and terrified Eddy Merckx. It is cycling's most feared mountain for a reason. L'Etape du Tour de France Femmes is not just another sportive — it is a statement. When ASO launched the Tour de France Femmes in 2022, it corrected a century-long omission in cycling's most important race. The amateur L'Etape edition, inaugurated in 2025 with 7,000 riders, extends that revolution to anyone willing to suffer up the same mountains as the professional women. The 2026 route delivers cycling's ultimate single-climb challenge: 120 km from ancient Vaison-la-Romaine through Provencal vineyards and lavender fields, building toward 21.8 km of Mont Ventoux via Bedoin. The forest section averages 9%. The barren moonscape above Chalet Reynard offers no shade, no shelter, and no mercy. And the next day, Demi Vollering and Katarzyna Niewiadoma will ride the same road. Yes if you want to be part of the most important development in women's cycling — the Tour de France Femmes brand, in amateur form. Yes if Mont Ventoux via Bedoin is on your lifetime cycling list. Yes if you want the prestige of L'Etape du Tour with a more accessible distance. Skip if you want a mature, established event with decades of history — this is only its 2nd/3rd edition. Skip if extreme heat is a dealbreaker. Non-negotiables: Single-climb sustained effort of 90+ minutes at threshold; Heat acclimatization and hydration strategy for 30-38C riding; Pacing strategy tested for a back-loaded course

### Liège-Bastogne-Liège Challenge
Tier 1 | Score: 79/100 | 160 mi | 14,764 ft | Banneux, Belgium | April | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/liege-bastogne-liege-challenge/

The oldest Monument in cycling opens its roads to 8,000 amateurs — and the Ardennes côtes don't care about your amateur status. The Ardennes don't do flat. From the moment you leave Banneux, the road undulates through dense forest, drops into river valleys, and then kicks up into côtes that have been breaking professional cyclists since before your grandparents were born. The route heads south to Bastogne through increasingly remote Ardennes terrain, turns around, and then delivers the iconic finale sequence — La Redoute, Roche-aux-Faucons, and Saint-Nicolas — when your legs are most destroyed. Every côte is short but savage. The recovery between them is never enough. The total elevation accumulates like interest on a debt you can't repay. Signature challenge: La Redoute is the signature — the most iconic côte in Monument cycling. But the true challenge is the accumulation: 11+ classified climbs across 257km, each one steeper and more painful than your legs can handle. The Ardennes don't have one killer climb. They have a dozen that work together to destroy you systematically. The Liège-Bastogne-Liège Challenge is the amateur version of the oldest Monument in professional cycling — 257km through the Belgian Ardennes with 4,500m of climbing across the most famous côtes in the sport. You ride La Redoute, Roche-aux-Faucons, and Saint-Nicolas the day before Pogačar and the WorldTour peloton do the same. Eight thousand riders tackle three distance options on roads that have defined one-day racing since 1892. This is not a gravel race — it is a road cycling pilgrimage through the Ardennes, and it carries the full weight of La Doyenne's 130-year legacy. You need the fitness for 257km and 4,500m of climbing on roads that never stay flat for long. The côtes are short but vicious — La Redoute's 20% ramps and Stockeu's 11.9% average will expose any weakness in your climbing. Late April Belgian weather demands full wet-weather gear and the mental toughness to ride 8+ hours in conditions that might be cold, wet, or both. If you've done L'Étape du Tour or Paris-Roubaix Challenge, you know the format. If this is your first Monument sportive, consider the 147km option — it still includes the iconic finale climbs without the 257km death march through the Ardennes. Non-negotiables: Build to 180km+ ride with 3,000m+ climbing; Train steep intervals (10-15% grade, 2-5 min efforts); Cold/wet weather riding experience

### Race Across America (RAAM)
Tier 1 | Score: 79/100 | 3000 mi | 175,000 ft | Oceanside, CA to Atlantic City, NJ | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/race-across-america/

3,000 miles. Coast to coast. 12 days. The most extreme endurance cycling race on Earth. A 3,000-mile transcontinental odyssey across the full diversity of the American landscape. Desert, mountains, plains, ridgelines, and coastline — all in a single continuous effort. Signature challenge: 3,000 miles across the entire continental United States — desert heat, Rocky Mountain passes, Great Plains wind, Appalachian climbs, and 175,000 feet of elevation gain — all in a single continuous, nonstop effort under 12 days. Race Across America is not a bike race in any conventional sense. It is a 3,000-mile, coast-to-coast war of attrition against sleep deprivation, extreme climate, and the fundamental limits of the human body. Running since 1982, with fewer than 300 solo finishers in history and a 50% DNF rate, RAAM occupies a category of one. The logistics are staggering — you need a crew, vehicles, and a budget that rivals a small expedition. The experience is transformative. The prestige is unmatched in ultra-cycling. This is not an event most people should attempt. It is an event everyone should know about. If you are an elite ultra-endurance cyclist with $15,000-$100,000 to spend, a crew of 8-12 people willing to sacrifice two weeks, qualifying results from approved ultra events, and the physical and mental capacity to ride 20+ hours a day for 8-12 consecutive days across the entire continental United States — yes. For everyone else, RAAM is best appreciated from the crew van, the documentary couch, or the dot-watching screen. Non-negotiables: Qualifying ultra-distance results; Crew recruitment and training; Support vehicle acquisition and preparation

### Little Sugar MTB
Tier 1 | Score: 77/100 | 30 mi | 3,500 ft | Bentonville, Arkansas | April | mtb
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/little-sugar-mtb/

The Ozarks' limestone will shred your tires and ego in equal measure across 60 miles of relentless technical punishment. 30 miles, 3,500 ft. 70% singletrack, 30% gravel/dirt. Bentonville MTB trails (Slaughter Pen). Technical Ozarks terrain. Technical rating: 4/5. Signature challenge: Technical Ozarks singletrack + punchy climbs. MTB skills required. Little Sugar MTB is new LTGP race (founded 2025). Pairs with Big Sugar Gravel (same weekend—MTB Saturday, gravel Sunday). 30 miles, 3,500 ft. 70% singletrack, 30% gravel/dirt. Bentonville MTB trails (Slaughter Pen)—technical Ozarks terrain. Life Time Grand Prix race (pro field: Keegan Swenson, Sofia Gomez Villafane). 1,000+ riders. Bentonville is MTB mecca (Walmart headquarters, world-class trails). Early April spring conditions (50-70°F). If you want LTGP MTB race in world-class trails, this is it. If you want gravel or dropbar, skip it (or do Big Sugar Sunday). MTB required. You need advanced MTB skills and bulletproof tires—this isn't gravel cosplaying as mountain biking, it's legitimate XC suffering on sugar-sharp limestone that sounds like riding on glass. The Ledges at mile 39 will humble anyone who hasn't spent serious time on technical singletrack. Skip it if you can't handle extended conga lines or if your idea of technical is a gravel road with potholes.

### Raid Pyreneen Randonneur
Tier 1 | Score: 76/100 | 447 mi | 36,100 ft | Hendaye (Atlantic) to Cerbere (Mediterranean), France | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/raid-pyreneen/

Ocean to ocean. 18 cols. 100 hours. The Pyrenees crossing that's been humbling cyclists since 1950. Epic. Point-to-point, ocean to ocean, 18 mountain passes over 3-5 days. The Pyrenees from west to east, starting in the green, rain-prone Basque country and finishing on the sun-baked Mediterranean coast. Each day brings a new set of cols, a new landscape, and a new challenge. The format — self-navigated with a stamped logbook — strips cycling back to its essentials: you, the mountains, and the road. Signature challenge: The Tourmalet at 2,115m is the symbolic peak, but the real challenge is the cumulative toll of 18 passes over 3-5 days with no support. The Raid tests self-reliance as much as fitness. The Raid Pyreneen is not an event. It's a tradition. Since 1950, cyclists have collected stamps in a logbook as they cross the Pyrenees from ocean to ocean — no timing chips, no aid stations, no spectators. Just you, 18 mountain passes, and 100 hours. The format hasn't changed because it doesn't need to. In an era of sold-out sportives with Instagram hashtags and finisher's beer gardens, the Raid stands apart as a declaration of what cycling adventure was always meant to be: self-reliant, mountain-focused, and earned. Yes if you want cycling's most authentic mountain challenge — no frills, no crowds, pure self-supported adventure. Yes if you value tradition over Instagram. Skip if you need organized support — this is fully on you. Skip if you want a single-day event. Non-negotiables: Multi-day endurance base — back-to-back 200+ km mountain days; Self-sufficiency skills — navigation, nutrition, mechanical, sleep strategy; Weather readiness — full Alpine kit for summit and valley conditions

### Seven
Tier 1 | Score: 76/100 | 78 mi | 10,500 ft | Nannup, Western Australia | May | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/seven/

Australia's crown jewel—hosting 2026 Worlds with brutal climbs, wildlife hazards, and Western Australian wilderness. A relentless climbing race through Western Australian pine plantations and state forests. The 'parallel singletrack' roads are often just two tire tracks wide with grass in the middle. Sharp quartz grit and mica create constant flat tire risk. 12-13 categorized climbs with 3,200m total elevation in 125km—one of the highest climbing ratios in gravel racing. Signature challenge: The Crucible—the 20%+ grade climb on loose surface where 'I went to hell and back... gradients frequently touching 20%' is the common refrain. SEVEN is Australia's answer to the world's great gravel races—and in 2026, it will literally host the World Championships. The 125km course through Western Australian forests is brutally technical with 3,200m of climbing on sharp quartz gravel. The Crucible alone, with 20%+ grades on loose surface, separates this from smooth US gravel. It's a genuine adventure race in a location that surprises everyone who expected red desert. Yes if you want a bucket-list Australian adventure and handle technical terrain. Yes if you're chasing UCI Gravel World Series. Skip if the travel commitment to Western Australia is prohibitive.

### BWR Utah (Belgian Waffle Ride Utah)
Tier 2 | Score: 79/100 | 84 mi | 9,843 ft | Cedar City, Utah | May | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/bwr-utah/

Cedar City's altitude strips away your excuses while loose rock strips away your skin—Utah's answer to European suffering with American scenery. 135 km, 3,000 m. 70% gravel, 30% paved. Utah red rock—high desert, technical sections. Technical rating: 4/5. Signature challenge: 135 km + 3,000 m elevation + May heat (20-32°C). Utah red rock. BWR Utah is red rock adventure—135 km, 3,000 m elevation. Founded ~2021. 70% gravel, 30% paved. Utah red rock—high desert, technical sections. 500-800 riders. Cedar City, UT. May spring conditions (15-30°C, hot). BWR series entry ($180-$250). If you want Utah red rock scenery with technical climbs and waffles, this is it. If you need cool weather, skip it. May heat is the challenge. Show up if you can handle bike handling at 6,000 feet where thin air meets technical terrain. The 92% unroad isn't just marketing speak—it's sand, rock, and singletrack that punishes hesitant front wheels and rewards desert racing experience. Skip it if you think gravel means smooth fire roads or you've never raced above 4,000 feet.

### Dreiländergiro
Tier 2 | Score: 79/100 | 104 mi | 11,811 ft | Nauders am Reschenpass, Tyrol, Austria | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/dreilander-giro/

Three countries. The Stelvio at 2,757m. 48 hairpins. Since 1994. Europe's tri-border Alpine classic. A geographic masterpiece disguised as a bike ride. Route A (Stelvio Engadin) is the complete experience: 168 km through Austria, Italy, and Switzerland, with the Stelvio as the centerpiece and the Engadin Valley as the reward. Route B (Stelvio Vinschgau) delivers the Stelvio and the Umbrail in a more compact 120 km format. Both routes start and finish in Nauders at 1,394m. The signature challenge is obvious — the Stelvio's 48 hairpins from Prad, 24.9 km at 7.4% average, ascending to 2,757m. But the route's genius is everything around the Stelvio: the approach through the Italian Vinschgau, the Umbrail descent into Switzerland, and (on Route A) the long Engadin traverse before the final push over the Norbertshöhe. Signature challenge: The Stelvio from Prad. 48 hairpins. 24.9 km at 7.4% average. 2,757 meters above sea level. This is the climb that has decided Giro d'Italia stages, broken professional cyclists, and inspired a million cycling photographs. On the Dreiländergiro, you ride it in a mass-start event with 1,500 others, counting down the numbered hairpins from 48 to 1. The upper switchbacks above treeline — exposed to weather, short of oxygen, spectacular in every direction — are the most dramatic kilometers in European sportive cycling. The Dreiländergiro is what happens when a small Austrian cycling club in a village of 1,500 people decides to build one of Europe's greatest road cycling events — and succeeds for 30 years running. Three countries. The Stelvio. 3,000 riders from 30 nations. Sells out in hours. The route from Nauders crosses into Italy at the Reschenpass, climbs the legendary 48 hairpins of the Stelvio to 2,757m, drops into Switzerland over the Umbrail Pass, and returns through the Engadin and over the Norbertshöhe. It is not the longest Alpine marathon (the Otztaler is longer), not the most famous (La Marmotte and L'Etape are bigger names globally), but the Dreiländergiro may be the most complete Alpine cycling experience available in a single day: three countries, three languages, five major passes, and the single most iconic road in professional cycling. Yes if the Stelvio is on your bucket list and you want to ride it in a fully supported, mass-start event through three countries. Yes if you thrive on major Alpine climbing and want 3,600m of vertical gain in a single day. Skip if you cannot commit to the October 1 registration scramble. Skip if remote Austrian Alpine logistics are a dealbreaker. Non-negotiables: Alpine climbing endurance — multiple rides of 120+ km with 2,500+ meters of climbing; Stelvio-specific preparation — sustained efforts of 60-90 minutes at threshold on climbs of 1,500m+; Altitude acclimatization and cold-weather layering strategy

### Iron Horse Bicycle Classic
Tier 2 | Score: 79/100 | 47 mi | 5,700 ft | Durango, Colorado | — | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/iron-horse-bicycle-classic/

Fifty-three years of man versus machine through the San Juan Mountains — Colorado's definitive cycling tradition. A point-to-point mountain road race on closed US Highway 550 through the heart of the San Juan Mountains. Two passes above 10,000 feet, a net elevation gain of 2,806 feet, and the narrow gauge train running alongside for the first 10 miles. Simple concept, demanding execution. Signature challenge: The double pass crossing — Coal Bank (10,640 ft) and Molas (10,910 ft) — with no recovery between them. At altitude, your power drops 10-15% from sea level values, and the thin air makes the descents feel faster than they are. The Iron Horse Bicycle Classic is not just a road race — it's an American cycling institution. Since 1972, the concept of racing a steam train through the San Juan Mountains has created something genuinely irreplaceable. Two 10,000-foot passes on a closed US highway, a point-to-point finish in Silverton, and more than half a century of tradition make this Colorado's definitive cycling event. The road race itself is demanding without being destructive, and the surrounding festival has grown into a weekend celebration of everything on two wheels. If you love mountain road racing and want an event steeped in history with a genuinely unique concept, absolutely. If you need flat-course speed or a pure criterium experience, this is not your race. The altitude and climbing demand respect — prepare accordingly. Non-negotiables: Sustained climbing fitness for two 10,000+ ft passes; Altitude acclimatization plan; Cold-weather descending capability

### Migration Gravel Race
Tier 2 | Score: 79/100 | 404 mi | 26,000 ft | Maasai Mara, Kenya | June | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/migration-gravel-race/

Four days through Kenya's Maasai Mara where heat, altitude, dust, and wildlife create the ultimate gravel adventure. Four days through the Maasai Mara ecosystem. Wildlife crossings. Cultural immersion. Volcanic terrain. It's a safari with a timing chip. Signature challenge: It's not one thing—it's everything. Heat. Altitude. Dust. Distance. Wildlife. Four days of sensory overload. Migration isn't a race you do for a time or placing. It's a race you do for the experience. Four days through Kenya's Maasai Mara with full support, wildlife encounters, and landscapes that rewire your brain. If bucket-list gravel exists, this is it. Yes, if you can afford it and can get a spot. Migration is the gravel adventure of a lifetime. No other race combines this level of support, experience, and pure adventure. Just be ready for the price tag and the cumulative stress of four consecutive days.

### Otztaler Radmarathon
Tier 2 | Score: 79/100 | 141 mi | 18,045 ft | Solden, Otztal Valley, Tyrol, Austria | August | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/otztaler-radmarathon/

Europe's hardest single-day sportive. 227 km. 5,500m climbing. The Timmelsjoch at 2,474m. Since 1982. An ultra-distance Alpine odyssey. The 227 km route is a loop from Solden that crosses the main Alpine ridge twice via four major passes. The first half — Kuhtai and the descent to Innsbruck, then south over the Brenner — builds gradually. The Jaufenpass introduces the Italian side with its tight switchbacks and South Tyrolean villages. Then comes the Timmelsjoch: 30+ km of climbing from the Passeiertal valley to 2,474m, the final 10 km above treeline in barren, exposed Alpine terrain. The Timmelsjoch is the race. Signature challenge: The Timmelsjoch. 30+ km of climbing to 2,474m after 180+ km already in the legs. The final 10 km above treeline — exposed, cold, barren, with no shelter and no respite — is where 227 km of accumulated fatigue meets altitude and weather. This is the moment the entire ride has been building toward. The Otztaler Radmarathon is not a ride for people who want a nice day out on their bike. It is 227 km and 5,500 meters of climbing across four Alpine passes, finishing with a 30+ km ascent to the Timmelsjoch at 2,474m. It crosses the Austrian-Italian border twice. It starts in pre-dawn cold and ends — if you're lucky — before the afternoon thunderstorms hit the summit. Since 1982, the Otztaler has been the benchmark for ultra-distance road cycling in Europe. It is not the most beautiful event (the Maratona wins that), not the most prestigious (L'Etape owns that), and not the most famous (La Marmotte predates it). It is simply, relentlessly, the hardest. Yes if you want the ultimate single-day cycling challenge and can sustain 9-13 hours of Alpine riding. Yes if the Timmelsjoch at 2,474m after 180 km is your idea of a good time. Skip if 227 km exceeds your distance capability — there is no shorter route option. Skip if remote Austrian valley logistics are a dealbreaker. Non-negotiables: Ultra-distance training — multiple rides of 180+ km with significant climbing; Altitude preparation — sustained climbing above 2,000m; Cold-weather riding capability and layering strategy

### Ride the Rockies
Tier 2 | Score: 79/100 | 450 mi | 35,000 ft | Colorado Rocky Mountains (route changed annually) | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/ride-the-rockies/

37 years of crossing Colorado's highest mountain passes. A Denver Post institution — now a memory. Relentless vertical. Six to seven consecutive days of sustained climbing on Colorado's highest paved mountain passes, with 30,000-40,000 feet of total elevation gain over 400-500 miles. The route changed annually — some years favoring the San Juan Mountains of southwest Colorado, others crossing Rocky Mountain National Park or the central Sawatch Range. Valley sections between passes offered recovery, but the altitude never let up. Every day included at least one major pass above 10,000 feet. Signature challenge: Sustained altitude. Not one killer day, but six or seven consecutive days of climbing at 8,000-12,000 feet where oxygen is scarce, weather is volatile, and there is no flat. The cumulative altitude exposure is what breaks riders — you cannot fake acclimatization. Ride the Rockies was Colorado cycling's crown jewel for 37 years — a week-long pilgrimage through the highest paved passes in North America, organized by the Denver Post and drawing 2,000 riders from 49 states and nine countries. Then the Denver Post sold it in 2021, new management cratered the experience, and by 2024 it was dead. The riding was always spectacular — cresting Independence Pass, descending into Aspen, climbing Trail Ridge Road through Rocky Mountain National Park. But the institution that made it possible is gone. Colorado's Ride carries the spiritual torch with 350 riders. The mountains remain. You cannot ride Ride the Rockies. It no longer exists. The spiritual successor, Colorado's Ride, runs annually in August with 350 riders through similarly spectacular Colorado mountain terrain. If multi-day supported mountain cycling through the Rockies is your goal, Colorado's Ride at coloradosride.com is the current option. Non-negotiables: Altitude acclimatization — arrive in Colorado 3-5 days before start; Multi-day climbing endurance — back-to-back 4,000-6,000 ft climbing days; Descending skills and cold-weather gear for mountain passes

### Sportful Dolomiti Race
Tier 2 | Score: 79/100 | 130 mi | 16,400 ft | Feltre, Belluno, Veneto, Italy | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/sportful-dolomiti-race/

Five Dolomite passes. 5,000m of climbing. The Manghen at 2,047m. UNESCO stone cathedrals. Italy's toughest granfondo. Relentless Dolomite climbing through the most dramatic mountain scenery in European cycling. Five major passes in 210 km — the route is essentially a linked series of climbs and descents with minimal flat. The Manghen is the queen stage: 18 km of sustained 7.5% with ramps above 12% through increasingly wild terrain to a 2,047m summit. The other four passes — Rolle, Valles, Cereda, Croce d'Aune — each deliver 600-1,100m of climbing through UNESCO World Heritage landscape. The character is distinctly Italian: suffering, beauty, and history intertwined. Signature challenge: The Passo Manghen defines this event: 18 km at 7.5% average with ramps above 12%, through progressively wilder terrain to a 2,047m summit with panoramic Dolomite views. Combined with four more passes — including two others above 1,900m — the total 5,000m of climbing across the Dolomite landscape makes this one of cycling's great mountain days. The Sportful Dolomiti Race is the Dolomites at their most honest: five passes, 5,000 meters of climbing, and no hiding behind a famous name. The Maratona dles Dolomites gets the headlines and the 30,000-person lottery. The Sportful gets the riders who want the harder route and don't need a lottery to feel validated. The Passo Manghen — 18 km at 7.5% with ramps above 12% — is the kind of climb that separates cyclists who ride mountains from cyclists who ride hills. And the scenery? The Dolomites are not mountains in the normal sense. They are vertical limestone cathedrals — UNESCO gave them World Heritage status for a reason. Riding through the Pale di San Martino from Passo Rolle is one of cycling's great visual moments. The Croce d'Aune, where Campagnolo was so frustrated by frozen wing nuts in 1927 that he invented the quick-release skewer, is a reminder that cycling history was written in these mountains. Yes if you want the hardest Dolomite granfondo with fewer riders and no lottery. Yes if the Passo Manghen's 12%+ ramps and UNESCO scenery speak to your cycling soul. Skip if you want the spectacle and lottery drama of the Maratona. Skip if mid-June Dolomite weather volatility concerns you. Non-negotiables: Mountain climbing endurance: multiple 3-4 hour climbing sessions per week; Steep gradient preparation: repeated efforts at 8-12% for 15+ minutes; Full dress rehearsal: 160+ km with 3,000m+ climbing and tested nutrition

### The Rift
Tier 2 | Score: 79/100 | 124 mi | 6,400 ft | Hvolsvöllur, Southern Iceland | July | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/the-rift/

Iceland's volcanic gravel race where you literally cross between continents and experience weather that doesn't negotiate. Otherworldly. Black volcanic sand, lava fields, glacier views. It looks like Mars with better air. Signature challenge: The weather. Iceland doesn't care about your race plans. You can experience four seasons in four hours. The Rift is gravel racing as geological adventure. The course crosses the Mid-Atlantic Ridge—you literally ride between continents. The terrain is volcanic, the weather is unpredictable, and the landscapes are unlike anywhere else on Earth. This is cycling adventure at its most otherworldly. Yes, if bucket-list adventure matters more than racing. The Rift delivers geological pilgrimage, otherworldly terrain, and a story you'll tell forever. Just be ready for Iceland's weather and the expense of getting there.

### Triple Bypass — Colorado's Three-Pass Challenge
Tier 2 | Score: 79/100 | 118 mi | 10,800 ft | Evergreen to Avon, Colorado | July | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/triple-bypass/

3 passes. 11,990 feet at the summit. Colorado's defining cycling challenge since 1988. A three-act mountain epic. The ride builds from forested foothills through the alpine tundra of the Continental Divide and into the heart of Colorado ski country. Each pass has its own personality — Juniper is the opener, Loveland is the crux, Vail is the closer. The final 28-mile descent from Vail Pass to Avon on the dedicated bike path is one of the great finishing stretches in American cycling. You earn every foot of that descent. Signature challenge: Loveland Pass — the switchbacks above treeline to 11,990 ft, crossing the Continental Divide. The thin air, the exposed alpine tundra, the endless turns — this is the moment that defines the Triple Bypass and the image that stays with every rider who completes it. The Triple Bypass is Colorado's cycling identity made manifest. Three mountain passes. Nearly 12,000 feet at the summit of Loveland. The Continental Divide crossed on two wheels. Since 1988, this ride has been the measuring stick for Front Range cyclists — the annual question: can I get over all three? It's not a race, and it doesn't pretend to be. It's a 118-mile conversation between you and the Rocky Mountains, starting in the dark foothill pines of Evergreen and ending with a 28-mile victory lap descent into the Vail Valley. Over $4.6 million donated to community nonprofits. 37 years without interruption. This is what cycling for something looks like. Yes if you want to cross the Continental Divide on a bicycle and experience the full spectrum of Colorado mountain riding in a single day. Yes if sustained high-altitude climbing on paved roads is your definition of a good time. Skip if you need race competition or can't tolerate riding above 10,000 feet. Non-negotiables: Sustained climbing endurance — back-to-back pass simulations of 45-90 minutes each; Altitude acclimatization or preparation for riding above 10,000 ft; Early start pacing plan that clears Loveland Pass before 1:00 PM

### Belgian Waffle Ride Asheville
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 129 mi | 10,000 ft | Asheville, North Carolina | May | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/bwr-asheville/

BWR's Blue Ridge cousin serves up Belgian-style punishment with Southern humidity and 10,000 feet of Appalachian reality checks. 129 miles, 10,000 ft. Belgian-style: 60% gravel, 30% pavement, 10% dirt. Blue Ridge Mountains. Technical gravel sectors. Technical rating: 4/5. Signature challenge: 129 miles + 10,000 ft + humid heat. Blue Ridge brutality. BWR Asheville is Blue Ridge brutality—129 miles, 10,000 ft elevation, Belgian-style mixed terrain (60% gravel, 30% pavement, 10% dirt). Founded 2018 as BWR series expansion. Technical gravel sectors (BWR signature—rough, chunky, steep). 800+ riders. Asheville tourist city. Late May/early June = 60-85°F + humid. 15-25% DNF. If you want Blue Ridge suffering and BWR challenge, this is it. If you want pure gravel or easy terrain, skip it. You need solid climbing legs and comfort on technical gravel—those steep, chunky sectors don't forgive weakness. The 129-mile distance isn't the killer; it's the relentless elevation combined with late May humidity that breaks people. Show up undertrained and you'll join the 25% DNF rate somewhere around mile 80 when the Blue Ridge climbs start compounding.

### BWR Montana (Belgian Waffle Ride Montana)
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 87 mi | 9,186 ft | Missoula, Montana | August | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/bwr-montana/

The Belgian Waffle Ride that actually lives up to its Belgian difficulty rating, where Montana's mountains separate the climbers from the pretenders. 140 km, 2,800 m. 75% gravel, 25% paved. Montana Rockies—mountain terrain, technical sections. Technical rating: 4/5. Signature challenge: 140 km + 2,800 m elevation + Montana Rockies mountain terrain. BWR Montana is Montana Rockies adventure—140 km, 2,800 m elevation. Founded ~2022. 75% gravel, 25% paved. Montana Rockies—mountain terrain, technical sections. 500-800 riders. Missoula, MT (great town, good airport access). August summer conditions (15-30°C). BWR series entry ($180-$250). If you want Montana Rockies scenery with technical mountain gravel and waffles, this is it. If you need flat terrain, skip it. Mountain terrain is the challenge. Show up if you can sustain power at 7,000 feet and don't mind walking your bike through Hell's Kitchen when the grades hit double digits. This isn't about gravel handling—it's about whether your legs can push a big gear up endless climbs while your lungs remember what thin air feels like. Skip it if you think 9,000 feet of elevation sounds fun on paper.

### Chasing Cancellara
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 171 mi | 18,045 ft | Switzerland (multiple routes) | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/chasing-cancellara/

Chase an Olympic champion through the Swiss Alps on ultra-distance routes finishing in Zermatt or Andermatt. An ultra-distance point-to-point road race through the Swiss Alps. The Bern-Zermatt flagship begins at 2:00 AM in Ittigen near Bern, threading through the Gantrisch Natural Park in darkness before crossing the Mittelberg and Col du Pillon passes. The route winds through the lower Valais valley before the brutal final climb from Visp to Zermatt (1,620m) — a relentless drag after 250km in the saddle. The Zurich-Andermatt variant follows Lake Zurich through Glarnerland before tackling the Klausen Pass (1,948m) and descending to Andermatt. Both routes are fully paved on excellent Swiss roads. Signature challenge: The final climb from Visp to Zermatt — 35km of sustained climbing to 1,620m after 250km already in the legs, with the Matterhorn watching your suffering. Chasing Cancellara is what happens when a cycling legend with two Olympic golds and four Paris-Roubaix wins decides to create the event he'd want to ride. Ultra-distance point-to-point routes through the Swiss Alps, a 2:00 AM start in darkness, and the genuine possibility of beating Cancellara's time. The Bern-Zermatt flagship — 275km, 5,500m of climbing, finishing beneath the Matterhorn — is one of the most demanding one-day cycling events in Europe. It's not cheap (this is Switzerland) and the field is small (~400), but the experience is irreplaceable. Yes if you want an ultra-endurance Alpine road challenge with genuine star power. Yes if 275km and 5,500m of climbing in one day excites rather than terrifies you. Skip if you want gravel or mixed-surface riding. Skip if Swiss pricing is a dealbreaker. Non-negotiables: Ultra-distance endurance base (250+ km road rides); Night riding proficiency and equipment; Alpine climbing endurance with sustained efforts above 1,500m

### Fred Whitton Challenge — The Daddy of Them All
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 112 mi | 12,795 ft | Grasmere, Lake District, Cumbria, England, UK | May | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/fred-whitton-challenge/

112 miles. 3,900 metres. Seven passes. Hardknott at 33% on mile 96. Britain's hardest sportive, run for charity since 1999. There is no harder single-day cycling event in the British Isles. The Fred Whitton Challenge was the first sportive ever held in the UK, and after 27 editions nothing has surpassed it. The route strings together every major Lake District pass — Kirkstone, Honister, Newlands, Whinlatter, Cold Fell, Hardknott, Wrynose, and Blea Tarn — into 112 miles and 3,900 metres of climbing through a UNESCO World Heritage landscape. The numbers alone are formidable. What makes the Fred legendary is the sequencing. The first 90 miles soften you up. Then at mile 96, with your glycogen reserves exhausted and your legs carrying the accumulated damage of 3,000+ metres of climbing, you arrive at the foot of Hardknott Pass. The road tilts to 33%. Your rear wheel slips on the tarmac. Riders walk. The strong survive. Over the top, Wrynose and Blea Tarn deliver the double and triple blows that break whatever the first 96 miles left intact.

But the Fred is not just a hard ride — it is a memorial, a charity, and a community. Fred Whitton was a popular member of the Lakes Road Club who died in 1998 at fifty years old. His clubmates rode this route in his honour the following year and raised money for Macmillan Cancer Support. Twenty-seven years and £2,000,000 later, the event is still powered by over 350 volunteers, and every penny of the £200 charity donation goes directly to cancer nurses, air ambulances, mountain rescue, and youth cycling. The ballot entry system means places are coveted. The atmosphere at Grasmere on the morning of the Fred is unlike any other UK cycling event. This is not a brand activation with a sportive attached. This is a cycling club honouring a dead friend by running the hardest ride they could imagine, year after year, for charity. Yes if you want the defining climbing challenge in British cycling — the one every serious UK cyclist measures themselves against. Yes if 3,900 metres of climbing through the Lake District's most savage passes appeals to you. Yes if you value charity, community, and memorial over timing chips and podiums. Yes if you can handle Hardknott's 33% gradient on legs with 96 miles already in them. Skip if you need competitive structure, guaranteed entry, reliable weather, or closed roads. Non-negotiables: Low gearing: 34/34 or lower — a standard road cassette will not save you on Hardknott; Three training rides of 80+ miles with 2,000+ metres of climbing — at least one with a hard climb after mile 60; Wet-weather descending confidence on narrow, steep roads

### Granfondo Gavia e Mortirolo
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 84 mi | 13,123 ft | Ponte di Legno, Brescia, Lombardy, Italy | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gf-gavia-e-mortirolo/

The Mortirolo at 18%. The Gavia at 2,618m. Pantani's mountains compressed into 135 km of pure Italian Alpine brutality. Compact and devastating. At 135 km, this is not a test of endurance — it's a test of climbing power and altitude tolerance. The route wastes no time: the Mortirolo hits early with its 18% ramps, the Gavia climbs to 2,618m through thinning air, and the Tonale provides one final sustained effort before the return to Ponte di Legno. There is almost no flat. The climbing density (4,000m in 135 km) is among the highest of any sportive on Earth. Signature challenge: The Mortirolo at 18% followed by the Gavia at 2,618m. These two climbs — one defined by gradient, the other by altitude — create a combined challenge unlike anything else in amateur cycling. The Mortirolo destroys your legs; the Gavia destroys your lungs. The GF Gavia e Mortirolo is not the longest gran fondo, not the highest, and not the biggest. It is, kilometer for kilometer, the most brutal. The Mortirolo's 18% gradients reduce strong riders to survival mode — there is no 'riding tempo' on this climb, only suffering and stubbornness. The Gavia at 2,618m takes whatever the Mortirolo left and dissolves it in thin air. These are the mountains where Marco Pantani became il Pirata, where Andy Hampsten rode through a snowstorm in 1988. You don't ride this event for a medal or a time. You ride it to discover what's left of you after the Mortirolo takes everything you thought you had. Yes if you want the steepest sustained gradients in any amateur cycling event. Yes if the Giro d'Italia legends of Mortirolo and Gavia mean something to you. Skip if 18% gradients terrify you or your gearing doesn't go below 34x30. Skip if you need a large international field and polished logistics. Non-negotiables: Steep climbing specificity — repeated efforts at 10-15% gradient for 30+ minutes; Gearing check: 34x32 minimum, 34x34 recommended; Altitude preparation or acclimatization for 2,618m

### Grinduro
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 60 mi | 7,800 ft | Multi-Location Global Series | — | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/grinduro/

Four timed segments scattered across 60 miles of Sierra Nevada dirt — race hard when it counts, cruise with friends when it doesn't. Grinduro flips gravel racing on its head - four brutal timed segments scattered across 60 miles of Sierra Nevada terrain, with social cruise sections between the suffering. You'll ride conversationally through spectacular mountain scenery, then absolutely torch yourself for 5-15 minutes when the clock starts ticking. Signature challenge: The format itself is the killer - you can't pace conservatively because only four segments count, but you can't go full gas because there are three more stages coming. It rewards complete riders who can climb, descend, and suffer repeatedly while managing effort across 60 miles of Sierra Nevada punishment. You need legitimate climbing legs for 7,800 feet and technical handling skills for those singletrack descents. The format saves you from pacing disasters but won't save you from being undertrained. Show up fit enough to go anaerobic for 10 minutes at a time across varied terrain, or you'll get shelled on every timed segment.

### Haute Route Ventoux
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 168 mi | 24,606 ft | Bedoin, Provence, France | October | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/haute-route-ventoux/

Three summits of cycling's most legendary mountain in three days — the definitive Ventoux experience with professional stage-race support. A 3-day stage race centered entirely on Mont Ventoux — cycling's most legendary standalone mountain. Each stage radiates from Bedoin through the surrounding Provencal countryside before converging on the summit at 1,912m via a different ascent route. The course design is elegant in its simplicity: three days, three sides, one mountain. The climbing is relentless, the exposure above treeline is savage, and the cumulative fatigue of three consecutive summit finishes separates those who trained from those who hoped. Signature challenge: The cumulative summit assault: three ascents of Mont Ventoux (1,912m) in three days from three different sides. Each approach has distinct character, but all converge on the same exposed, wind-battered final 6 km of moonscape. By Stage 3, the climb you know is waiting breaks you mentally before the gradient breaks you physically. Mont Ventoux is cycling's most mythological mountain — a barren, windswept colossus that has broken professionals in the Tour de France and claimed the life of Tom Simpson in 1967. The Haute Route Ventoux takes this legendary climb and demands you do it three times in three days, each from a different side. 270 km. 7,500 meters of climbing. Three summit finishes at 1,912m on the exposed moonscape where the Mistral can gust past 90 km/h.

The Bedoin ascent is the classic: 21.5 km at 7.6% average, with a murderous 10 km stretch through the forest averaging 9-10%. The Malaucene side hits you immediately with 12% gradients before a brief mid-climb reprieve. The Sault approach lulls you with 26 km of gentle lavender-country climbing before the exposed final kilometers deliver the same savage summit as the other two sides. By Stage 3, your legs have climbed to 1,912m twice already. The third ascent is where character is revealed.

The Haute Route organization is world-class — feed stations, mechanical support, timing, post-stage meals, and daily rankings make this feel like a professional stage race for amateur riders. The field of 300-500 creates genuine camaraderie forged through shared suffering. Provence as a backdrop — vineyards, gorges, medieval villages — adds culture and cuisine to complement the climbing. This is not a race for beginners. It is a pilgrimage for serious cyclists who understand that Ventoux is not just a mountain — it is a test of everything you are. Ride this if Mont Ventoux is your cycling grail and you want all three ascents in a professionally supported format. Skip it if you are on a budget (EUR 600-900 entry), prefer flat or gravel terrain, or cannot commit to 3 consecutive days of serious climbing. If you just want to climb Ventoux once, ride it independently — it is a public road. Non-negotiables: Sustained climbing endurance for 60-90 minute efforts; Multi-day recovery capacity; Headwind and exposure strategy

### LoToJa Classic — Logan to Jackson
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 203 mi | 9,800 ft | Logan, Utah to Teton Village, Wyoming | — | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/lotoja-classic/

America's longest one-day road race. Three states. Three passes. One unforgettable day. An odyssey across three states and three mountain passes. The front half punishes you with climbing; the back half tests whether anything remains. At 203 miles, LoToJa is not about any single climb or feature — it is about the accumulation of everything across an entire day. Signature challenge: Three mountain passes in the first 110 miles — then 90+ miles on depleted legs. The front-loaded climbing ensures that the 'flat' back half of the course is anything but easy. At mile 150, every false flat feels like a climb. At mile 180, you still have a marathon of cycling remaining. The signature challenge is not any single climb — it is the relentless accumulation of 203 miles. LoToJa is the American one-day classic that European cycling traditions inspired but the Mountain West made its own. Two hundred three miles from a Utah college town to the foot of the Tetons, across three states and three mountain passes, in a single day. It is not the hardest race in America. It is not the most famous. But it might be the most audacious — a race conceived in 1983 when two Logan cyclists decided that Americans deserved their own Paris-Roubaix and built it through sheer mileage. LoToJa is for riders who want more than a century ride — who want a genuine odyssey. If you can sustain moderate effort for 10-13 hours, have a reliable crew, and want a finishing story that few other events can match, this is your race. But be honest with yourself: 203 miles is a very long day, and the three mountain passes in the first half ensure you will face the final 90 miles on tired legs. Non-negotiables: Long ride progression to 7+ hour sustained efforts; Crew logistics planned and rehearsed; Mountain climbing block with sustained efforts at threshold

### Bob Cook Memorial Mt. Evans Hill Climb (Blue Sky Hill Climb)
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 27.4 mi | 6,590 ft | Idaho Springs, Colorado | July | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/mt-evans-hill-climb/

27 miles. 6,590 feet. Summit at 14,130 ft. The highest paved cycling race in North America since 1962. One direction: up. The Mt. Evans Hill Climb is 27.4 miles of continuous ascending from the town of Idaho Springs at 7,540 feet to the summit parking area at 14,130 feet. The gradient is never savage — averaging 4.5% with a maximum around 10% — but the relentlessness is the point. There are no descents, no flat recovery sections (save a brief respite at Echo Lake), and no escape from the altitude that builds with every pedal stroke. The course passes through three distinct life zones: montane forest, subalpine forest with ancient bristlecone pines, and finally barren alpine tundra where nothing grows and mountain goats outnumber cyclists. Above treeline at 11,500 feet, the landscape transforms into an exposed, wind-blasted moonscape. The final miles above 13,000 feet are ridden in a hypoxic fog where your body is operating on roughly 60% of sea-level oxygen. Signature challenge: The final 5 miles above Summit Lake. From 12,830 feet to 14,130 feet, exposed switchbacks climb through alpine nothing. The oxygen is gone. The trees are gone. The gradient is honest (5.7% average) but your body has been robbed of its ability to process it. This is where the Mt. Evans Hill Climb separates itself from every other cycling event on the continent. There is no higher paved road you can race a bicycle on in North America. That sentence is the entire sales pitch, and it is enough. Since 1962, cyclists have lined up in Idaho Springs to ride 27 miles straight up into oxygen debt, hypothermia risk, and the company of mountain goats. The race honors Bob Cook, a five-time winner who died at 23 — a reminder that the mountain does not care about your credentials. At the summit, you will stand at 14,130 feet with 40% less oxygen and 100% more perspective. The Mt. Evans Hill Climb is not the hardest race. It is not the longest. It is not the most prestigious. But it is the highest, and nothing else comes close. Yes if you want the most extreme altitude experience available in competitive cycling on paved roads. Yes if you have trained for altitude or can arrive early enough to acclimatize. Skip if you have altitude sickness history, need technical terrain variety, or want a mass-participation atmosphere. Non-negotiables: Altitude acclimatization — arrive in Colorado at least 3-5 days before the race; Sustained climbing endurance — 2-3 hour continuous climbing efforts in training; Cold-weather gear for July — arm warmers, vest, full-finger gloves, ear cover

### RAMROD — Ride Around Mount Rainier in One Day
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 154 mi | 10,000 ft | Enumclaw, Washington, USA | July | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/ramrod/

154 miles. 10,000 feet. One active volcano. The Pacific Northwest's defining cycling challenge since 1984. RAMROD is a circumnavigation. You ride all the way around an active volcano and come back to where you started. That simple premise — one loop, one mountain, one day — produces one of the most varied and rewarding days you will ever spend on a bicycle. The ride begins in the agricultural lowlands east of Tacoma, where Enumclaw sits at the Cascade foothills. Depending on the route edition, you may head counterclockwise toward the Nisqually Entrance and Paradise (the classic route) or clockwise toward Crystal Mountain and Cayuse Pass (the modern bypass). Either way, the terrain transitions dramatically: flat farmland gives way to rolling foothills, which give way to towering old-growth forest, which gives way to alpine passes where the air cools and the mountain dominates the sky. Stevens Canyon Road — when permitted — is the emotional heart of the ride: a 1930s-era park road through a glacial valley with stone bridges and tunnels. Cayuse Pass at mile 100+ is the physical heart: 8 miles at 6% when your legs have nothing left. The long descent back to Enumclaw in the Pacific Northwest's extended summer twilight is the reward. The mountain was always there. You rode all the way around it. Signature challenge: Cayuse Pass at mile 100. Eight miles at a constant 6% grade in afternoon sun with no shade, on legs that have already climbed 6,000-7,000 feet over 100 miles. Every RAMROD veteran knows the name. The rest stop halfway up Cayuse is the most grateful you will ever be for a cup of water and a folding chair. The climb is not technically difficult — it is 6% and relentless — but arriving at its base after 100 miles of riding around a volcano transforms a moderate climb into a test of will. Cayuse Pass is where RAMROD separates the people who trained for 150 miles from the people who trained for 100. Forty years ago, a cyclist named John Dixon convinced 51 other people to ride their bicycles all the way around Mount Rainier in a single day. Forty-one editions later, 800 people win a lottery each year for the privilege of doing the same thing. RAMROD is the Pacific Northwest condensed into a single day on a bicycle: old-growth forest, alpine passes, glacial rivers, quiet foothills towns, and a 14,411-foot active volcano that is always there — above the treeline, above the clouds, above everything. The ride starts in the dark of Enumclaw at 5 AM with breakfast cooked by the local Rotary club. It climbs to Paradise through cathedral forests with the mountain overhead. It crosses Stevens Canyon on 1930s stone bridges through a glacial valley. It grinds up Cayuse Pass at mile 100 on legs that stopped cooperating 20 miles ago. And it returns to Enumclaw 150 miles later, in the long Pacific Northwest twilight, with the mountain still watching. RAMROD is not the hardest ride in America. It is not the longest. But it may be the most complete — a full orbit of something genuinely magnificent, earned one pedal stroke at a time. Yes if you want to circumnavigate an active volcano in a single day on one of America's most storied cycling routes. Yes if 150+ miles through national park alpine passes, old-growth forest, and glacial valleys is your idea of a perfect day. Yes if you value community tradition over timing chips and age-group podiums. Enter the lottery every year and volunteer when you can. Skip if you need competitive structure, technical terrain, or a ride shorter than 150 miles. Skip if you cannot handle uncertainty — the route changes annually and the event's future depends on National Park Service permitting decisions. Non-negotiables: At least two training rides of 120+ miles with 6,000+ feet of climbing in a single day; Fueling strategy validated for 10-15 hours of sustained riding — 250-300 calories per hour minimum; Layering system for 30-degree temperature swings: arm warmers, vest, light gloves packed and accessible

### Giro Sardegna Gravel
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 81 mi | 8,530 ft | Siniscola, Sardinia, Italy | May | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/giro-sardegna-gravel/

Mediterranean gravel with UCI points that actually feels like a vacation until the dirt sections remind you why your watts matter. 130 km, 2,600 m. 75% gravel, 25% paved. Sardinian mountains—rolling terrain, coastal views. Technical rating: 4/5. Signature challenge: 130 km + 2,600 m elevation + May warmth. Sardinian mountains Mediterranean views. Giro Sardegna Gravel is Sardinian mountains—130 km, 2,600 m elevation. Founded ~2020. 75% gravel, 25% paved. Sardinian mountains—rolling terrain, Mediterranean coastal views. 500-700 riders. Siniscola, Sardinia (coastal town). May spring conditions (18-28°C). Affordable entry (€90-€130 / $100-$145). If you want Sardinian island gravel with Mediterranean views and affordable entry, this is it. If you need local/domestic, skip it. International destination race. If you can hold threshold power on loose surfaces and don't mind mixing beach starts with proper gravel circuits, this delivers. The 50km circuit format means your pacing mistakes get amplified—show up undertrained for those dirt sections and the second lap becomes survival mode. Solid choice if you want UCI World Series points without the full sufferfest of most qualifiers.

### La Stelvio Santini (Granfondo Stelvio Santini)
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 81 mi | 14,009 ft | Bormio, Alta Valtellina, Lombardy, Italy | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/granfondo-stelvio-santini/

The Mortirolo breaks you. The Stelvio finishes you. 4,270 meters of climbing to the highest paved pass in Italy, where 48 hairpins and 2,758 meters of altitude separate the riders from the tourists. A tale of three climbs — or one, depending on your ambition. Every route finishes at the summit of the Passo dello Stelvio at 2,758m, making this one of the highest-finishing cycling events in Europe. The short route is pure Stelvio: 64 km from Bormio with the entire second half spent climbing 21.5 km to the summit. The medium route descends the valley first, adds the Teglio climb, then returns for the Stelvio. The long route adds the Mortirolo between Teglio and the Stelvio — a detour of only 13 km but an additional 1,300m of the most punishing climbing in European cycling. The rhythm of the long route is descent-climb-descent-climb-CLIMB: valley roads give way to Teglio's steep walls, a recovery descent leads to the Mortirolo's relentless 10.5% forest road, another descent returns you to the valley, and then the Stelvio begins. By the time you hit the first Stelvio switchback, you have already climbed over 2,700m. The final 21.5 km to the summit is a war of attrition against altitude, fatigue, and the mountain itself. Signature challenge: The Stelvio from Bormio: 21.5 km, 1,533m of vertical gain, 40 switchbacks, summit at 2,758m. It is the event's centerpiece, the reason every route exists, and the reason the event exists at all. On the long route, you arrive at the base of the Stelvio having already climbed the Teglio and the Mortirolo — with over 2,700m of ascending in your legs before the main event begins. The Stelvio does not care what you have already done. It presents 21.5 km of 7.1% average gradient, thinning air, dropping temperatures, and the psychological weight of 40 numbered switchbacks. The final 5 km above the tree line, in the barren Alpine wasteland at 2,400-2,758m, is where the event reveals who came prepared and who came optimistic. The Granfondo Stelvio Santini asks one question: can you climb to the top of the second-highest paved pass in the Alps? The long route adds a darker question first — can you survive the Mortirolo, 12.4 km of 10.5% average gradient through silent forest, and then ride another 80 km to the Stelvio summit at 2,758m? This is not a gran fondo that hides its intentions. The Mortirolo was introduced to the Giro in 1990 and immediately became cycling's benchmark of suffering. The Stelvio has been the Giro's defining climb since Fausto Coppi made it his personal theater in the 1950s. The event itself is young — founded in 2011, it is still the new kid on Italy's crowded gran fondo calendar. But the mountains are ancient, and the mountains are the point. Santini puts a jersey on your back. The Stelvio puts you in your place. Yes if the Stelvio is on your bucket list — and it should be. Yes if you want to ride two of the most famous climbs in cycling in a single day. Yes if you have the fitness for 4,000+ meters of climbing at altitude. Yes if you want a Santini jersey earned at 2,758m. Skip if you need convenient logistics — Bormio is remote and the access costs add up. Skip if you lack sustained climbing fitness — the Mortirolo's 10.5% average gradient punishes the underprepared. Skip if altitude is a concern — 2,758m is high enough to meaningfully impact performance without acclimatization. Non-negotiables: Sustained climbing at altitude — ability to maintain output for 60-90 minutes above 2,000m elevation; Mortirolo simulation — repeated efforts at 10%+ gradient for 30-60 minutes; Multi-climb endurance — 3,000m+ of climbing in a single training ride

### Grinduro France
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 62 mi | 6,562 ft | Xonrupt-Longemer, Vosges, France | June | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/grinduro-france/

Grinduro's inaugural European outing trades California dust for Vosges forests, keeping the party-to-pain ratio intact. Enduro format: 4 timed descent stages (technical singletrack), untimed climbs. 62 miles, 6,562 ft. Vosges mountains: steep, forested, technical. Rewards MTB descending, allows social climbing. Signature challenge: Technical singletrack. MTB skills required. Crashes possible. Grinduro France is the French edition of the international festival-race series. Same format as Germany (untimed climbs, 4 timed descents), same party atmosphere (live music, camping, expo), but French: wine instead of beer, Vosges instead of Eifel, French food instead of German. New venue in 2024 (moved to Vosges from Monts du Lyonnais) brings stunning mountain scenery. Most riders prioritize experience over results. Technical descents reward MTB skills but are optional—untimed climbs allow social riding. If you loved Grinduro Germany but prefer French wine and mountain views, this is it. If you can handle 100 kilometers of varied terrain without crying about gravel bike handling, this works. The 2,000 meters of climbing spread across French forest roads won't destroy you, but the mix of surfaces rewards riders who can adapt rather than specialists. Show up for the weekend festival vibe, not pure racing—Grinduro's DNA is equal parts suffering and beer.

### Grinduro Germany
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 62 mi | 6,562 ft | Hellenthal, Eifel, Germany | May | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/grinduro-germany/

A weekend festival disguised as a gravel race where your descending skills matter more than your FTP. Unique enduro format: 4 timed descent stages (technical singletrack/doubletrack), all climbs untimed. Rewards MTB descending skills, allows social climbing. 62 miles, 6,562 ft elevation. Eifel volcanic landscape—German forest roads and technical descents. Signature challenge: First technical test. Sets the tone. MTB skills required. Loose, rooty, rocky. Grinduro Germany is what happens when you cross a gravel race with a music festival and MTB enduro. The format is unique: only 4 descent stages are timed (technical singletrack), while all climbs are untimed transport stages. You can climb at social pace, chat, stop for photos—then pin it on the timed descents if you want. Most riders are here for the festival: live music, beer garden, camping, expo. The racing is secondary. The Eifel region is stunning—volcanic landscape, German forest roads, near the Belgian border. If you want a chill weekend with friends, amazing scenery, and some optional racing—Grinduro delivers. If you want a serious gravel race, look elsewhere. If you can handle technical singletrack descents and care more about German beer than race times, this is perfect. The untimed climbs mean fitness matters less than bike handling—bring 42mm tires, a dropper post, and zero ego. Skip it if you want pure gravel racing or hate crowds partying while you're trying to suffer.

### Mammoth Tuff
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 100 mi | 7,500 ft | Mammoth Lakes, California | June | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/mammoth-tuff/

Altitude meets volcanic attitude in California's most honest sufferfest. 100 miles of High Sierra alpine gravel. 7,500 ft elevation, 7,880-8,000 ft altitude. 70% gravel, 30% paved. Smooth fast roads. Technical rating: 2/5. Altitude is the defining challenge. Signature challenge: Altitude at 8,000 ft. Smooth roads mean high speeds burn energy faster. Sea-level riders suffer. Mammoth Tuff is a UCI Gravel World Series qualifier at 8,000 feet. 100 miles of smooth High Sierra gravel. Altitude is the challenge—7,880-8,000 ft throughout means 30% less oxygen. If you're sea-level, arrive 3-5 days early or suffer. Course isn't technical (2/5), it's fast and smooth. Mammoth Lakes is expensive resort town. June Lake Loop (mile 40-60) is stunning. UCI status brings competitive field (Keegan Swenson, Lauren De Crescenzo). If you want High Sierra scenery with altitude suffering, this is it. Show up fit or the 8,000-foot starting elevation will expose every training shortcut you took. The 7,500 feet of climbing over 105 miles isn't negotiable, and volcanic gravel punishes both your legs and your equipment. This isn't a scenic tour—it's a legitimate training ground that's produced Unbound winners for a reason.

### Nordic Chase Gravel Edition
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 373 mi | 26,247 ft | Copenhagen, Denmark to Oslo, Norway | June | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/nordic-chase-gravel/

Three countries, 100 hours, and enough Scandinavian gravel to remind you why self-supported ultras don't negotiate. ~600 km, ~8,000 m. 70% gravel, 30% paved. Multi-day stage race (4 stages). Copenhagen to Oslo. Technical rating: 3/5. Signature challenge: Multi-day fatigue (4 days, 600 km). Supported but cumulative. Nordic Chase Gravel Edition is multi-day Scandinavian adventure—Copenhagen to Oslo, ~600 km over 4 stages. Founded ~2019. 70% gravel, 30% paved. Supported stage race (hotels, meals included in €400-€600 entry). 200-300 riders. 4 stages ~150km each. Scandinavian countryside—rolling terrain, coastal sections. June summer conditions (15-25°C). If you want multi-day European adventure with support, this is it. If you want single-day or budget race, skip it. Expensive but all-inclusive. You need serious self-supported experience and the mental fortitude for 100-hour efforts across three countries. The 60-80% gravel through Nordic forests isn't technical, but the distance and isolation will expose every weakness in your preparation and gear. Show up undertrained and you'll be arranging your own expensive extraction from rural Sweden.

### Oregon Trail Gravel Grinder
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 375 mi | 35,000 ft | Central Oregon (multiple towns) | July | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/oregon-trail-gravel/

The Grand Tour of American gravel—five days of Oregon's best, compressed into one transformative experience. Five distinct stages showcasing Oregon's diversity—high desert, forests, mountains, and everything between. Signature challenge: The cumulative nature. Any single stage is manageable. Five consecutive days of 60-80+ miles with major climbing is a different animal. Oregon Trail is for those who find single-day races limiting. The stage format creates different demands—recovery, consistency, pacing across days. It's closer to bikepacking than racing, with timing chips. Yes if you're ready for multi-day racing and want to experience gravel at its deepest. Not if you can't commit the time or prefer the intensity of one-day events.

### TORTOUR
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 621 mi | 45,932 ft | Schaffhausen, Switzerland | August | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/tortour/

1,000 km non-stop across Switzerland. Alpine passes by starlight. The world's ultimate ultra-endurance cycling test. The TORTOUR 1000 route traverses Switzerland in a massive loop, changing year to year but always incorporating multiple Alpine passes, Swiss lowland roads, and urban transitions. The character is defined not by any single climb or descent but by the accumulation: 1,000 km of terrain changes, day-to-night-to-day temperature cycles, the psychological battle of sustained effort, and the Swiss Alps appearing on the horizon when your body is already finished. The route takes riders through multiple cantons, language regions, and climate zones. It is Switzerland distilled into a 48-hour continuous experience. Signature challenge: The second night. After 24+ hours and 500+ km of continuous riding, darkness falls again and the body demands sleep. Pushing through the second night — often on Alpine terrain — is the defining challenge of the TORTOUR 1000. Everyone can ride the first 500 km. The TORTOUR is decided in the dark hours. The TORTOUR is not a bike race. It is a 1,000 km argument with yourself about whether you can keep pedaling through the night, over Alpine passes, across an entire country, for 48 straight hours. The world's largest multi-day non-stop ultra-endurance cycling event takes everything Switzerland throws at you — 12,000m of climbing, 30-degree temperature swings, complete darkness, Alpine dawn — and compresses it into a single continuous effort.

Solo unsupported is the purest form: you, your bike, and whatever you can carry for 1,000 km. Solo with support gives you a crew car and checkpoint assistance. Teams of 2-6 relay the distance. Every format is punishing. The TORTOUR 1000 solo finisher has earned something that no one-day sportive can replicate.

The 2026 edition returns to Schaffhausen, the event's birthplace, after six years in the Zurich canton. The prologue, pasta party, and finisher's brunch return to the original format. This is a homecoming for an event that has grown into the defining ultra-cycling challenge in Europe. Yes if ultra-endurance cycling is your discipline and you want to test yourself against 1,000 km of Swiss terrain in 48 hours. Yes if the idea of Alpine passes at 3 AM excites rather than terrifies you. Skip if you have never ridden through the night or covered more than 300 km in a single effort. Skip if the budget (CHF 3,000-5,000 all-in) is prohibitive. Non-negotiables: Multiple 400+ km training rides with night riding segments — minimum 20 hours continuous; Support crew logistics finalized — vehicle, driver rotation, checkpoint strategy; Night riding equipment tested — lights, reflective gear, warm layers for Alpine passes at 3 AM

### Tour du Mont-Blanc Cyclo
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 205 mi | 26,247 ft | Megeve, Haute-Savoie, France | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/tour-du-mont-blanc-cyclo/

Circumnavigate Mont-Blanc through France, Switzerland, and Italy. 330 km. 8,000m of climbing. Dawn to dusk. One mountain. Three countries. Epic in scale, relentless in execution. The 330 km route is a circumnavigation — you are literally riding around a mountain massif, and the sense of circling Mont-Blanc drives the entire experience. The six major cols are not stacked as brutally as the Marmotte or Gavia events — there are valley recovery sections between climbs — but the sheer accumulation of climbing over 15+ hours creates a unique kind of attrition. The middle section through Switzerland and Italy, with the Great St. Bernard as the high point, is the psychological crux. The Cormet de Roselend as the final major col, when 260 km are in the legs, is the physical crux. Signature challenge: The 330 km distance with 8,000m of climbing through three countries creates a unique attrition challenge. No single climb is as brutal as the Mortirolo or as iconic as Alpe d'Huez, but the accumulated effect of 6+ cols over 15+ hours, with the Great St. Bernard at 2,469m as the high point after 130+ km, is the defining challenge. The Tour du Mont-Blanc Cyclo is not for the majority of cyclists. It is for the minority who have looked at the map, seen that you can ride around Western Europe's highest mountain through three countries in a single day, and thought: yes. 330 km. 8,000 meters. Six major cols. Dawn to dusk. France, Switzerland, Italy, and back to France. The Great St. Bernard Pass at 2,469m is a crossing that Napoleon used to invade Italy — you're doing it on a road bike with 200 km already in your legs. This is not a sportive. This is a geographical statement. Yes if you have deep ultra-endurance fitness and want the most epic single-day cycling adventure in Europe. Yes if riding through three countries around Western Europe's highest peak speaks to your sense of journey. Skip if you haven't completed at least one 200+ km mountain sportive. Skip if the logistics of a 15-hour day across three countries overwhelm rather than excite you. Non-negotiables: Ultra-endurance base: at least three 200+ km rides with 3,000m+ climbing; 15-hour nutrition strategy tested and refined; Clothing strategy for 5C-30C range including fast transitions

### Tour of the Gila
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 170 mi | 15,000 ft | Silver City, Grant County, New Mexico, USA | April | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/tour-of-the-gila/

America's only UCI stage race — open to amateurs. The Gila Monster at 7,400 ft is waiting. Multi-day stage racing at altitude through the Gila Wilderness. Each stage is a distinct test — the ITT sets the opening gaps, the road stages include genuine mountain climbs on narrow wilderness roads, and the criterium rewards tactical racing. The Gila Monster road race is the queen stage: 69 miles for amateurs with 5,200 ft of climbing, finishing at 7,436 ft on a narrow, winding mountain road through ponderosa pine forest. Signature challenge: The Gila Monster — 10.3 miles of narrow wilderness mountain road finishing at 7,436 ft. The only summit finish in American amateur stage racing that rivals European HC climbs for difficulty and atmosphere. Tour of the Gila is the most unique amateur road racing opportunity in the United States. It's the only place an amateur can pin on a number and race a UCI stage race course — the same climbs, the same roads, the same New Mexico wilderness that the professional peloton tackles. The Gila Monster climb is a genuine mountain summit finish that rivals anything in European professional cycling for atmosphere and difficulty. Silver City is remote, rugged, and authentically Western. This is not a corporate gran fondo with a finish-line festival — it's real stage racing in real wilderness. Yes if you want the unique experience of racing a UCI stage race as an amateur. Yes if you can handle altitude and remote logistics. Skip if you want a mass-participation gran fondo atmosphere. Non-negotiables: Stage race fitness — ability to recover between racing days; Altitude acclimatization or pre-adaptation strategy; USA Cycling license and category classification

### Whiskey Off-Road
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 50 mi | 6,523 ft | Prescott, Arizona | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/whiskey-off-road/

The Southwest's 20-year MTB institution where Skull Valley tests your suffering and Whiskey Row rewards your survival. A greatest-hits tour of Prescott National Forest combining technical singletrack, flowing trails, exposed fire road grinding, and tactical racing. You start on historic Whiskey Row, climb through granite formations, suffer through Skull Valley, flow through Spence Basin, and navigate technical descents when your legs are cooked. Every type of MTB terrain represented. Signature challenge: Skull Valley—a 12-mile exposed fire road climb at the halfway mark. 2,500-2,700ft of gain when you're already depleted. Documented 102F readings in hot years. Where cramping becomes universal and mental fortitude separates finishers from DNFs. The Whiskey Off-Road is the Southwest's premier mountain bike festival—a 20-year institution that combines genuine physical challenge with exceptional event production. Skull Valley will humble you. The technical sections demand MTB skills. But the weekend package—historic Whiskey Row, live music, expo, community—elevates it beyond just a race. It's what happens when an event organizer spends two decades perfecting their craft. Yes if you want a world-class MTB festival experience with genuine physical challenge. Yes if you appreciate 20 years of event refinement. No if you're a gravel purist uncomfortable on technical terrain. No if you need budget-friendly entry.

### Chequamegon MTB
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 40 mi | 4,000 ft | Cable, Wisconsin (Chequamegon National Forest) | September | mtb
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/chequamegon-mtb/

Forty miles of Northwoods power suffering disguised as a mountain bike race that somehow became a gravel institution. 40 miles, 4,000 ft. 60% forest roads, 40% singletrack. Technical MTB terrain. Mass start chaos. Technical rating: 4/5. Signature challenge: Mass start chaos (3,000 riders) + OO section technical singletrack. Chequamegon MTB is legendary—40+ years (founded 1983), 3,000+ riders, mass start chaos. Life Time Grand Prix race (pro field: Keegan Swenson, Payson McElveen). 40 miles, 4,000 ft. 60% forest roads, 40% singletrack. Technical MTB terrain—roots, rocks, OO section legendary. Cable small Wisconsin town. Mid-September fall conditions (50-70°F). If you want MTB history, mass start chaos, and LTGP glory, this is it. If you want gravel or dropbar, skip it. MTB required. Show up fit or don't show up at all—the Seeley Fire Tower climb doesn't care about your weekend warrior training plan. You need sustained power for constant rollers and the mental fortitude to handle September weather that swings from dust storms to mud festivals.
