If you can hold 250+ watts for three hours while navigating lottery systems and tourist crowds, this delivers old-school racing credibility.

### UCI Gravel Suisse
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 68 mi | 11,300 ft | Aigle / Villars-sur-Ollon, Switzerland | July | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gravel-fondo-switzerland/

The UCI's backyard playground where 3,300 meters of climbing separates tourists from gravel racers. Starting at cycling's global headquarters in Aigle, this course lulls you through 30 miles of Rhone Valley false confidence before unleashing 3,300 meters of vertical punishment. The second half abandons civilization for alpine forest tracks and 20% wooden ladders disguised as bike paths. It's less gravel race, more high-altitude survival exam with a fancy UCI stamp. Signature challenge: The Route des Chaux section after mile 49 strips away any illusion that this is a normal gravel race. Twenty percent pitches on technical forest tracks at 5,000+ feet elevation turn bikes into anchors and legs into concrete. The thing nobody tells you: it's not just steep, it's steep when you're already cooked from the altitude and previous 2,000 meters of climbing. You need legitimate climbing legs and a gravel bike that can handle 20% grades on loose surfaces. The second half destroys weekend warriors—if you can't sustain power on steep off-road climbs above 1,500 meters, the Route des Chaux will expose you. Come for the prestige and alpine scenery, but expect to earn every meter of that Col de la Croix finish.

### Gunni Grinder
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 120 mi | 10,000 ft | Gunnison, Colorado | September | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gunni-grinder/

High-altitude gravel that separates your actual fitness from your sea-level delusions. High-altitude mountain gravel with sustained climbs and big descents. 10,000 ft elevation gain over 120 miles at 7,700-10,000 ft base. Altitude is the defining challenge—oxygen is 25-30% thinner than sea level. Cochetopa Pass (miles 30-45) is a 15-mile sustained grind. High alpine sections (miles 50-70) max out altitude. Taylor Canyon rollers (miles 85-105) finish with relentless punchy climbs. This is a legitimate mountain challenge, not rolling gravel. Signature challenge: 15 miles of sustained climbing at 8,000+ ft elevation. Not steep, but relentless. Altitude makes every pedal stroke harder. Just keep spinning. Gunni Grinder is high-altitude mountain gravel done right. This isn't a flat power race—it's a climbing test at thin oxygen. You start at 7,700 ft (already higher than most people live) and max out near 10,000 ft. If you're from low elevation, your FTP will drop 10-15%. Your breathing will be labored. Your bonk will come faster. The Cochetopa Pass Road climb (miles 30-45) is a 15-mile grind that just keeps going—not steep, but relentless at altitude. The high alpine sections (miles 50-70) are exposed, windy, and stunning—above treeline with panoramic Rocky Mountain views. The Razor Creek descent is technical and loose—confidence-testing. Gunnison is a hidden gem: small town, big mountains, zero crowds. The post-race BBQ in the park is low-key awesome—burgers, local beer, live bluegrass. It's not a corporate expo. It's a community celebration. If you can sustain power above 8,000 feet and have low gears for Cochetopa Pass, this is honest mountain suffering without the Leadville circus. Sea-level riders thinking they'll adapt on race day will discover what oxygen debt feels like around mile 40. The terrain isn't technical, but the altitude will expose every fitness gap you've been hiding behind at home.

### Cycle to the Sun — Haleakala
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 36 mi | 10,023 ft | Pa'ia, Maui, Hawaii | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/haleakala-cycle-to-the-sun/

Sea level to 10,023 feet. One climb. One volcano. The House of the Sun. Pure uphill. There is no flat. There is no descent. The road leaves Pa'ia at sea level and climbs continuously for 36 miles through four distinct climate zones to the summit of Haleakala at 10,023 feet. The average gradient is 5%, but sections through Makawao hit 10% and the final summit approach reaches 10-18% in oxygen-depleted air. The landscape transforms completely as you climb: tropical vegetation gives way to eucalyptus, then pine, then barren volcanic rock above the treeline. You will ride above the clouds. You will arrive at the rim of a volcanic crater. Every foot of elevation is earned. Signature challenge: The final 8 miles above the park entrance. Above the cloud line, through barren volcanic landscape, with 70% oxygen and gradients hitting 18%, you grind to the summit crater of Haleakala at 10,023 feet. It is the closest thing to riding on another planet. Cycle to the Sun is not the hardest race in the world, or the most prestigious, or the most competitive. It is, however, one of the most extraordinary cycling experiences on the planet. You start at sea level on a tropical island and ride continuously uphill for 36 miles until you're standing at 10,023 feet on the rim of a volcanic crater, above the clouds, in 40-degree air that was 80 degrees when you started. You traverse every climate zone Hawaii has to offer — tropical, temperate, alpine, volcanic — in a single effort. The name Haleakala means 'House of the Sun,' and when you reach the summit, you understand why. This is not a race you enter to win. This is a ride you do to remind yourself that cycling can take you to places that feel like the edge of the Earth. Yes if you want one of cycling's most extraordinary single-climb experiences. Yes if riding through four climate zones to the summit of a Hawaiian volcano sounds like a lifetime memory. Skip if you need competitive depth or can't justify Hawaii travel costs for a single 36-mile uphill ride. Non-negotiables: Sustained climbing endurance — 3-5 hour continuous uphill efforts in training; Altitude awareness and preparation for riding above 8,000 ft; Layering strategy tested and rehearsed for 40-degree temperature swing

### Lauf True Grit Gravel Epic
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 84 mi | 9,000 ft | Santa Clara, Utah | March | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/lauf-true-grit/

Utah's technical playground where your handling skills matter more than your FTP and the Rollers of Doom separate pretenders from the genuinely prepared. True Grit drops you into the geological chaos where three deserts collide—Mojave, Great Basin, and Colorado Plateau. Eighty percent off-road through sandstone mazes, volcanic remnants, and sandy washes that chew up bikes and spit out undertrained riders. This isn't gravel racing, it's technical mountain biking disguised as a gravel event. Signature challenge: Lines through the rocks that riders practice a dozen times pre-race and still freeze up on race day. The technical sections aren't just difficult—they're bike-destroying, confidence-crushing puzzles that punish hesitation and reward local knowledge you don't have. Show up if you're comfortable dismounting on steep rock faces and practicing technical lines a dozen times before race day. The 9,000 feet of climbing rides like a century because of the constant technical demands—your bike handling needs to be sharp when you're deep into hour six. Skip this if you prefer smooth gravel roads or think a dropper post is overkill for gravel racing.

### Le Grand du Nord
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 110 mi | 5,000 ft | Grand Marais, Minnesota | May | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/le-grand-du-nord/

The most civilized way to suffer through 110 miles of Minnesota Northwoods while pretending Lake Superior views make your legs hurt less. Rolling terrain through Minnesota Northwoods. 5,000 ft over 110 miles = constant ups and downs, no sustained climbs. Mix of smooth gravel forest roads (70%), paved sections (20%), and technical doubletrack (10%). Smoother than mountain gravel but never flat. Signature challenge: Steep, scenic climb with Lake Superior views. Pack may still be together here—surges happen. Le Grand du Nord is Minnesota's entry into the UCI Gravel World Series—2,000+ riders racing through the stunning North Shore of Lake Superior at peak spring green. The course rolls through dense Northwoods, climbs for Lake Superior overlooks, and finishes in the tourist town of Grand Marais. It's not the hardest course (5,000 ft over 110 miles = rolling, not mountainous), but the field is international and fast. The scenery is world-class: dense Minnesota woods, Lake Superior vistas, and the iconic Gunflint Trail. Grand Marais is the perfect race town—hotels, restaurants, breweries, and post-race tourism. The large field means pack racing early and potential bottlenecks, but it thins out. This is a big-event, well-organized UCI race with all the amenities. You need the fitness for 110 miles and 5,000 feet—that's six hours minimum of rolling punishment through forest roads. The course isn't technically brutal, but it's relentlessly rolling, and those doubletrack sections at mile 80 will test your focus when you're cooked. If you can't handle pack racing with 2,000 riders or the possibility of 45-degree rain, stay home.

### Marmotte Granfondo Pyrénées
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 110 mi | 18,701 ft | Argelès-Gazost / Luz-Saint-Sauveur, Hautes-Pyrénées, France | August | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/marmotte-pyrenees/

Five Pyrenean cols. Double Tourmalet. 5,700m of climbing. Summit finish. The Marmotte's Pyrenean sister hits harder per kilometer than the Alpine original. The Marmotte Pyrénées is the event that cycling insiders know but the broader world has not fully discovered yet. On paper, it is the younger sister of the legendary Alpine Marmotte. In practice, it might be the harder ride. The numbers do not lie: 5,700m of climbing in 177 km gives it a higher elevation-per-kilometer ratio than the Alps. The double Tourmalet — climbing the most storied mountain in the Pyrenees from both sides in a single day — is an act of organized cruelty that, after 120 km, will have you questioning the sanity of the route designers. And then, when the Tourmalet has taken everything, they send you up Hautacam with its irregular gradients that refuse to let you find a rhythm.

The Pyrenees are darker, wilder, and less forgiving than the Alps, and this event embraces that character completely. Where the Alpine Marmotte finishes on the iconic 21 hairpins of Alpe d'Huez with crowds four deep, the Pyrénées edition sends you up a ski station road where the only witnesses are sheep and your own suffering. The Tourmalet at dawn, with mist rolling off the Pic du Midi and the road rising endlessly above the treeline, is one of cycling's great sensory experiences. Doing it a second time, 100 km later on legs that have already climbed three other mountain passes, is one of cycling's great tests of character.

If the Alpine Marmotte is the bucket-list mountain sportive, the Pyrénées edition is the insider's version — harder per kilometer, less crowded, and set against the wild, dark drama of the Tour de France's spiritual home. Give it ten more years and this event will be spoken about in the same breath as its Alpine sibling. For now, it remains the connoisseur's choice. Yes if you want the intensity of the Alpine Marmotte with more climbing per kilometer and fewer crowds. Yes if the Tourmalet means something to you — doing it twice in one day is a career cycling memory. Skip if you want the prestige and global atmosphere of the Alpine original. Skip if you need technical terrain — this is pure road climbing on tarmac. Non-negotiables: Sustained climbing endurance — 4+ hour sessions with 2,500m+ of climbing in training; Back-to-back climbing simulation — two major climbs on pre-fatigued legs; Full nutrition strategy tested over 6+ hours of climbing

### Gran Fondo Milano-Sanremo
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 184 mi | 14,474 ft | Milan to Sanremo, Liguria, Italy | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/milano-sanremo-gran-fondo/

La Classicissima — 296 km from Milan to the Ligurian Riviera. The Turchino, the Capi, Cipressa, Poggio, Via Roma. The longest day in amateur cycling. A race of three acts across three regions of Italy. Act One: the Lombardy and Piedmont plains — 100+ km of flat, fast, relentless agricultural roads where the peloton moves at 40+ km/h and the only challenge is staying in the group and out of the wind. Act Two: the Turchino — the first real climb, the passage through the Apennines where Lombardy becomes Liguria and the grey plains give way to the blue Mediterranean. Act Three: the Ligurian coast — 100 km of increasingly dramatic riding as the three Capi, the Cipressa, and the Poggio pile suffering onto legs that have already ridden 200+ km. The course is deceptive in its simplicity. No individual climb is fearsome. The Poggio averages 3.7%. The Cipressa averages 4.1%. But at kilometer 270 and kilometer 280, those modest percentages become the hardest climbs of your life. The pro peloton calls this phenomenon 'the course that bites you at the end.' For amateurs riding 2-3 hours longer than the professionals, the bite is sharper. Signature challenge: The distance. 296 kilometers. Not the Turchino, not the Cipressa, not the Poggio — those are just climbs. The signature challenge of the Gran Fondo Milano-Sanremo is the distance itself. It is the longest one-day amateur cycling event in the world, and there is no way to simulate it in training. You can ride 200 km. You can ride 250 km. But the zone between 250 km and 296 km is uncharted territory for nearly every rider who lines up. The legs stop working normally. The nutrition strategy that seemed bulletproof starts to fail. The mind begins its negotiations with the body. The Poggio at kilometer 280 is the cruelest joke in cycling — a climb that would be trivial at kilometer 80, transformed by distance into a monument of suffering. There are five Monuments in professional cycling. Milan-Sanremo is the longest, the oldest continuously run, and — for many — the most beautiful. La Classicissima. The Spring Classic. 296 kilometers from the industrial flatlands of Lombardy across the Apennines to the flower-scented Riviera di Ponente. The gran fondo version has been running since 1970, making it older than most of the riders who line up for it. You leave Settala at dawn and ride through the flat agricultural plains, the Turchino pass, the Ligurian coast, the three Capi, and then — with nearly 280 km in your legs — you climb the Cipressa and the Poggio. Two climbs that are not objectively hard. Two climbs that, after 280 km, will feel like the steepest roads you have ever ridden. Then you descend to the Via Roma in Sanremo and you are done. You have ridden La Classicissima. The same road Merckx rode seven times to victory. The same Poggio where Cancellara attacked from the front. The same Via Roma where van der Poel sprinted. No other amateur event in the world offers 296 km of Monument history in a single day. Yes if you are an endurance cyclist who wants the ultimate one-day road challenge — 296 km is a threshold experience that changes how you think about distance. Yes if you care about cycling history and want to ride every meter of the oldest, longest Monument. Yes if you have the discipline to train specifically for ultra-endurance and the self-sufficiency to manage nutrition for 10-13 hours with minimal feed station support. Skip if 296 km genuinely exceeds your ambition or your available training time. Skip if you want a well-serviced, festival-atmosphere sportive with aid stations every 30 km. Skip if you are looking for technical terrain or off-road riding — this is road cycling, pure and relentless. Non-negotiables: Ultra-endurance base — at least three rides of 200+ km (125+ miles) in the final 8 weeks of preparation; Race-day nutrition plan tested to failure — 80-100g carbohydrates per hour for 10+ hours; Climbing on pre-fatigued legs — practice riding climbs of 3-6% gradient after 150+ km of riding

### Monaco Gravel Race
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 68 mi | 8,202 ft | Monaco | October | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/monaco-gravel-race/

A UCI qualifier that trades the usual gravel brutality for Alpine scenery and climbing legs that'll make you question your gear choices. 110 km, 2,500 m. 70% gravel, 30% paved. Monaco/French Alps—steep climbs, technical. Technical rating: 5/5. Signature challenge: 110 km + 2,500 m elevation = very steep. Monaco/French Alps. 20-25% DNF. Monaco Gravel Race is Monaco/French Alps—110 km, 2,500 m elevation. Founded ~2020. 70% gravel, 30% paved. Steep climbs, technical. 300-500 riders. Monaco start/finish (iconic location). October autumn conditions (12-22°C). Expensive entry (€150-€200 / $165-$220). If you want Monaco/French Alps iconic location with brutal steep climbs, this is it. If you need manageable elevation or budget race, skip it. 20-25% DNF. If you can handle 121 feet of climbing per mile on short, punchy ascents, this is your European gravel vacation with World Championship qualification upside. The Mercantour park setting means you're climbing through postcard scenery instead of Kansas cornfields. Skip it if you prefer sustained efforts over constant gear changes, or if you think 68 miles sounds easy because the math doesn't lie on those repeated kicks.

### Nova Eroica
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 84 mi | 9,186 ft | Buonconvento, Tuscany, Italy | October | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/nova-eroica/

Tuscany's gravel answer to L'Eroica that trades vintage wool for modern suffering on the same unforgiving strade bianche. 135 km, 2,800 m. 70% gravel (strade bianche), 30% paved. Tuscan rolling hills. Technical rating: 3/5. Signature challenge: 135 km + 2,800 m cumulative elevation. Tuscan strade bianche rolling hills. Nova Eroica is Tuscan strade bianche—135 km, 2,800 m elevation. Founded ~2015. 70% gravel (strade bianche), 30% paved. Rolling Tuscan hills, iconic white roads. 1,000-1,500 riders. Buonconvento medieval town. October autumn conditions (12-22°C). Affordable entry (€80-€120 / $85-$130). If you want Italian gravel with Tuscan scenery and strade bianche, this is it. If you want local/domestic race, skip it. International destination race. You need legitimate climbing legs for 9,186 feet of Tuscan punishment and the bike handling skills to descend white gravel roads that turn sketchy fast. This isn't a beginner's intro to European gravel—it's proper suffering in postcard scenery. Show up undertrained and those rolling hills will expose every fitness gap you've been ignoring.

### Ouray Gravel Grinder
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 80 mi | 7,800 ft | Ouray, Colorado | September | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/ouray-gravel-grinder/

The Ouray Gravel Grinder doesn't exist as an organized race, but the San Juan Mountains will humble your FTP and test your ability to push a bike up 13,000-foot passes. Ouray serves up 80 miles of high-altitude punishment across mining roads that were never meant for bicycles. You'll grind through San Juan valleys on County Road 23 before the real show begins—12,000-foot alpine passes where your 34x42 becomes a prayer. This isn't gravel racing, it's controlled mountaineering on two wheels. Signature challenge: Engineer and Cinnamon passes back-to-back above 12,000 feet, where even Tour de France climbers end up walking sections. The altitude turns your FTP into a cruel joke, and the loose rock surfaces make every pedal stroke a negotiation with gravity. You can't race what isn't there, but if you're drawn to self-supported suffering in Colorado's most brutal terrain, bring a mountain bike and your hiking legs. The Alpine Loop and Imogene Pass will have you walking more than riding above 12,000 feet, and afternoon thunderstorms don't negotiate with your Strava segment dreams. This is bikepacking country masquerading as a gravel destination.

### Pirinexus 360
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 211 mi | 11,150 ft | Girona, Catalonia, Spain | September | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/pirinexus-360/

The thinking rider's 340k—half the distance of the big ultras but twice as beautiful, with a border-crossing climb that sends half the field home early. The Pirinexus is half smooth greenway cruise, half bike-destroying nightmare. You'll roll 55 kilometers on the silky Via Verde del Carrilet thinking this is easy, then spend the next 70 kilometers climbing volcanic hills and technical gravel that'll have you questioning your tire choice. The route crosses into France where the road literally disappears into rocky descents that punish anything under 40mm tires. Signature challenge: Col d'Ares is the course executioner—a 17.9-kilometer climb to the French border that peaks at 1,513 meters. It's not just the elevation; it's arriving there after 80 miles of volcanic punishment, knowing that technical descents await on the other side. Most riders call it quits here, and the ones who don't spend the descent walking their bikes down rocky trails that shouldn't exist on a cycling route. You need 200+ mile legs and 40mm+ tires minimum—the technical border descent after Col d'Ares will put narrower rubber on foot. This isn't a watts contest; it's navigation, bike handling, and knowing when to push through the Pyrenean climbs versus when to survive them. Show up undertrained and that 18-hour cutoff becomes very real very fast.

### Sellaronda Bike Day
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 33 mi | 5,413 ft | Sella Group, Dolomites, Italy | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/sellaronda-bike-day/

Four car-free Dolomite passes, 20,000 cyclists, zero entry fee — the purest cycling experience in the mountains, twice a year. A counterclockwise circuit around the Sella Group — the massive limestone massif at the junction of four South Tyrolean valleys. The route links four Dolomite passes in a continuous loop of climbing and descending through some of the most dramatic mountain scenery on Earth. On Sellaronda Bike Day, the absence of motor traffic transforms this circuit from a challenging road ride into a transcendent cycling experience. The terrain is constantly undulating — you are either climbing a pass or descending one, with minimal flat valley transitions. Signature challenge: Not a single climb but the totality: four passes in 55 km with 1,780m of climbing, experienced in silence on car-free roads through the most dramatic mountain landscape in Europe. The physical challenge is modest; the experiential impact is extraordinary. Sellaronda Bike Day is the simplest concept in cycling events: close the roads around the Sella Group to cars for one day and let cyclists ride. No registration, no timing, no fees, no bibs. Just 55 km of four legendary Dolomite passes — Sella (2,240m), Pordoi (2,239m), Gardena (2,136m), and Campolongo (1,875m) — in complete silence. The result is one of the most magical cycling experiences on Earth.

The numbers are modest — 55 km and 1,780m of climbing is a half-day ride for any fit cyclist. But the numbers miss the point entirely. This is about the silence. On any other day, these roads carry cars, buses, and motorcycles through some of the most dramatic mountain scenery in Europe. On Sellaronda Bike Day, 20,000+ cyclists of every age and ability share these roads without a single engine noise. The only sounds are tires on tarmac, wind through the Dolomite spires, and the spontaneous cheering at pass summits.

The event runs twice annually — June and September — offering two different Dolomite experiences. June delivers wildflower meadows, long daylight, and occasionally lingering snow at the highest elevations. September brings autumn colors, stable weather, and a gentler light. Both are extraordinary. The 20th edition in 2026 is a celebration of a concept so pure it has not needed to change since its inception: close the roads, open the mountains, and let bicycles be bicycles. Ride this if you love cycling as pure experience — car-free Dolomite passes in UNESCO World Heritage scenery with zero bureaucracy. Skip it if you need competitive structure, timing, results, or a challenging distance (55 km is short). If you want a competitive Dolomite event, enter the Maratona dles Dolomites lottery instead. Non-negotiables: Basic climbing fitness for 4 mountain passes; Self-sufficiency with nutrition and hydration; Weather preparedness

### Amstel Gold Race Toerversie
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 149 mi | 10,410 ft | Valkenburg, South Limburg, Netherlands | April | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/amstel-gold-race-toerversie/

Thirty Limburg climbs including the steepest road in the Netherlands, ridden by 15,000 amateurs the day before the Ardennes Monument. Relentless accumulation. The Limburg hills are not long — the longest climb is under 2 km — but they are steep, frequent, and merciless in their succession. The 240 km route packs 30 classified climbs, with 25 of them concentrated in the final 165 km. Between climbs, the terrain rolls through agricultural plateaus and wooded valleys at deceptive gradients that never truly flatten. The rhythm is: climb hard, descend fast, roll through a village, climb hard again. By climb 20, every subsequent 15% pitch feels like 25%. The Cauberg, ridden multiple times, gets steeper with each repetition — not because the gradient changes, but because your legs remember. Signature challenge: The Keutenberg. 22% gradient on narrow tarmac through the Limburg countryside. The steepest paved climb in the Netherlands, and a moment where the gap between reading a gradient number and riding one becomes vividly, painfully clear. The Amstel Gold Race Toerversie puts 15,000 riders on the steepest roads in the Netherlands, riding the same Limburg hills that define cycling's Ardennes classic. Thirty climbs on the 240 km route — none longer than 2 km, many steeper than 15%, the Keutenberg touching 22%. It's not about altitude or cobblestones; it's about the relentless accumulation of short, savage efforts through a countryside that looks nothing like the flat Netherlands you imagined. Start from the Shimano Experience Center, finish near the Cauberg, watch the pros do it Sunday. Yes if you want to experience a Monument course without cobblestones or extreme distance. Yes if short, steep climbs are your kind of suffering. Skip if you need competitive racing — this is a sportive with no timing. Skip if lottery logistics frustrate you. Non-negotiables: Short steep climbing power — repeated 1-3 minute maximal efforts; Endurance base for 240 km (or chosen distance) with cumulative climbing fatigue; Gearing check — compact or sub-compact crankset with 32-34t cassette

### The Assault on Mt. Mitchell — Beast of the East
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 102.7 mi | 11,000 ft | Spartanburg, South Carolina to Mt. Mitchell, North Carolina | May | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/assault-on-mt-mitchell/

102.7 miles. 11,000 feet. The highest summit east of the Mississippi. The Beast of the East since 1975. The Assault on Mt. Mitchell is a study in patience and delayed devastation. The first 80 miles lull you into thinking this is a pleasant century ride — rolling Piedmont roads through South Carolina and the North Carolina foothills, gaining barely 500 feet of net elevation as you pass through rural towns between Spartanburg and Marion. The road is flat enough to make you forget what you signed up for. Then mile 85 arrives, and the road tilts upward, and it does not stop tilting for the next 18 miles. The climb to the Blue Ridge Parkway gains over 1,000 feet in under 2 miles. The Parkway itself is a sustained grind at 3,000-5,000 feet with no flat recovery. At mile 98, you turn off the Parkway toward Mt. Mitchell State Park, and those final 5 miles to the summit are the cruelest on the course — steep, relentless grades through ancient spruce-fir forest to the summit at 6,684 feet. The air is cooler. The trees are different. You are standing at the highest point east of the Mississippi River, and your legs stopped working two miles ago. Signature challenge: The final 18 miles from Marion to the summit. After 80+ miles of rolling Piedmont riding, the course demands 5,000+ feet of climbing in the final stretch — a backloaded elevation profile that punishes anyone who did not respect the first 80 miles. The last 5 miles up the Mt. Mitchell summit road, on legs that have been working for 7-10 hours, through gradients that would be hard on fresh legs, is where the Beast of the East earns every letter of its name. There is no higher point east of the Mississippi River. There is no older cycling challenge to reach it. Since 1975, when John Bryan and a handful of Freewheelers pedaled from Spartanburg into the clouds above western North Carolina, the Assault on Mt. Mitchell has been the defining endurance cycling event east of the Rockies. The Beast of the East does not care about your FTP, your Strava KOMs, or your deep-section carbon wheels. It cares about whether you can ride 102 miles through the Carolina Piedmont, turn upward at mile 85 when your legs are already spent, and then climb 5,000 feet to the summit of a mountain that has no business being this tall on the East Coast. The first 80 miles are a long, hot warm-up. The last 20 are the ride. At the top, you stand at 6,684 feet in a spruce-fir forest that looks like it belongs in Canada, not North Carolina. You rode there from South Carolina. That fact will matter to you for a very long time. Yes if you want the defining climbing challenge east of the Rockies. Yes if a century ride with 11,000 feet of backloaded climbing and a geographic superlative finish appeals to you. Yes if you value tradition and community over timing chips and podiums. Skip if you need competitive structure, technical terrain, or cannot handle 80 miles of rolling warm-up before the real climbing begins. Non-negotiables: Century-distance climbing endurance — at least two training rides of 80+ miles with 5,000+ ft of climbing; Heat management strategy for the Piedmont section (miles 0-80); Summit layers packed and accessible — vest, arm warmers, light gloves

### Big Horn Gravel
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 85 mi | 10,000 ft | Gypsum, Colorado | June | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/big-horn-gravel/

Colorado's rowdiest gravel race where the 3,000 ft opening wall and rutted Jeep roads make pros switch to hardtails. 'A rowdy race with nearly 10,000 feet of climbing at elevation and a few miles of singletrack.' Rugged Jeep roads where 'you really need to keep your focus or you could crash pretty badly.' Signature challenge: The opening 3,000 ft in 13 miles is the signature gut-punch. Nearly every long-course discussion references that initial climb and how it sets up the rest of your day. Big Horn Gravel is what happens when you build a gravel race for people who think gravel races aren't hard enough. The 3,000 ft opening wall, the rutted Jeep roads, the technical singletrack - this is 'aggressive Colorado gravel' that makes pros switch to hardtails. Dylan Johnson got his biggest check here. The Vegan Cyclist calls it the best gravel race you've never heard of. It won't stay unknown for long. Yes if you want genuinely hard gravel racing that tests your technical skills, altitude tolerance, and pacing discipline. Big Horn is 'the best gravel race you've never heard of' - but it won't stay unknown for long. Come prepared for aggressive terrain and you'll have an unforgettable day.

### GFNY Bogota
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 63 mi | 7,614 ft | Zipaquira, Cundinamarca, Colombia | April | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gfny-colombia/

Start inside a salt cathedral. Climb into the thin Andean air above 3,000 meters. Race where Egan Bernal learned that oxygen is optional. A high-altitude road race that uses moderate gradients and paved Colombian mountain roads to deliver a physiological challenge that the course profile alone cannot explain. The route starts inside the Salt Cathedral of Zipaquira at 2,650m — an underground cavern carved from a salt mountain — before climbing through the Cundinamarca highlands to summits above 3,000m. The terrain is not technically demanding and the gradients are not extreme by European standards, but every effort is amplified by the 27% reduction in available oxygen. A 3.3% average gradient at 3,000m delivers the suffering of a 6% climb at sea level. The course rewards acclimatized climbers, punishes sea-level sprinters, and humbles everyone who arrives thinking fitness alone will suffice. Signature challenge: Altitude. Not the climbing gradient, not the distance, not the technical terrain — the air. GFNY Bogota starts at 2,650m and races above 3,000m. At these elevations, atmospheric oxygen is 25-30% lower than sea level, your VO2max drops 15-25%, and physiological systems that function flawlessly at sea level begin to fail. Heart rate creeps higher at the same power output. Lactate accumulates faster. Recovery between efforts takes longer. A 3.3% gradient that you'd spin up at sea level becomes a gasping, vision-narrowing ordeal. The course doesn't need steep grades or long distances to destroy you — it has physics. No amount of sea-level fitness compensates for unacclimatized lungs at 3,000 meters. GFNY Bogota is what happens when you take a franchise gran fondo template and drop it into one of cycling's spiritual homelands at an altitude that reduces your VO2max by 20% before you even clip in. The course itself is straightforward — paved roads, moderate gradients, 100 km — but the 2,650m starting elevation transforms every watt into an existential negotiation with the atmosphere. You warm up inside a salt cathedral carved from a mountain, emerge into the Andean dawn in Egan Bernal's hometown, and climb into mountains where the air gets noticeably thin. The Colombian cycling culture does the rest: crowds that actually care, a nation that treats cyclists as heroes, and a post-race atmosphere that feels earned in a way that manufactured expo-village celebrations never do. The franchise format is the ceiling — this is still a GFNY satellite, not a standalone institution — but the Colombian setting is the floor, and that floor is higher than most events' ceilings. Yes if you want to experience the world's highest gran fondo and test yourself at extreme altitude. Yes if Colombian cycling culture — the passionate crowds, the heritage of Bernal and Quintana and Forero — means something to you. Yes if you want a destination gran fondo that costs half of what European events charge. Skip if you haven't acclimatized to altitude and don't plan to arrive early — sea-level fitness is worthless above 3,000m. Skip if you want technical terrain, gravel, or a course that challenges handling. Skip if you need a massive international field. Non-negotiables: Altitude acclimatization protocol — arrive minimum 3-5 days early; Altitude-adjusted pacing strategy — plan for 15-25% power reduction; Layered clothing system for temperature swings

### Granfondo Gottardo
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 71 mi | 10,656 ft | Ambri, Ticino, Switzerland | August | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/granfondo-gottardo/

115 km, 3,248m climbing, three HC passes, 1832 cobblestones. Switzerland's most beautiful suffering. The course is a clockwise loop from Ambri through three distinct Alpine worlds. It opens with the Tremola — the cobblestone ascent of the Gotthard Pass through 24 hairpins of 1832 granite, a road monument unlike anything in cycling. After summiting at 2,106m, a fast descent drops into the Urseren valley and the base of the Furka Pass. The 12.2 km Furka climb at 7.3% average takes you past the famous Goldfinger filming location and the Rhône Glacier viewpoint to the summit at 2,429m. The descent into the Valais is fast and exposed. Then comes the Nufenen — 13.7 km at 8.3% average, the hardest climb of the day, saved for when your legs have nothing left. The summit at 2,478m is the second-highest paved road in Switzerland. The reward is a 26 km descent all the way back to the finish in Ambri. Signature challenge: The Nufenen Pass. At 13.7 km and 8.3% average, it is the steepest and highest of the three passes — and it comes last, after 80+ km and two HC climbs have already emptied your legs. The Tremola is the spectacle. The Nufenen is the reckoning. The Granfondo San Gottardo packs three of Switzerland's most legendary Alpine passes into 115 km of pure, concentrated suffering and beauty. The Tremola cobblestones — 4 km of 1832 granite through 24 hairpins — are cycling's most photogenic climb. The Furka Pass descent past the Rhône Glacier is James Bond's literal road. The Nufenen, at 2,478m and averaging 8.3% over 13.7 km, is the silent assassin that breaks you when you thought the hard work was done.

Cycling Plus voted this the best 100 km loop in Europe. It is hard to argue. The variety is extraordinary — cobblestones to smooth tarmac, Italian-speaking Ticino to German-speaking Uri, valley warmth to Alpine cold, history to cinema. And all of it compressed into a day's ride that starts and finishes in a small Ticino village.

The event itself is Swiss-organized and appropriately precise. Three feeding zones, mechanical support, professional timing, finisher meal. No unnecessary frills, no corporate bloat. Just an honest sportive on extraordinary roads. Yes if you want concentrated Alpine climbing on three legendary passes in a single day. Yes if the Tremola cobblestones and Goldfinger's Furka road are on your cycling bucket list. Skip if you need a large, competitive field or global prestige. Skip if Switzerland's prices and the Nufenen's 8.3% average after two prior passes sound like punishment rather than purpose. Non-negotiables: Back-to-back long climb sessions — minimum 3 hours of sustained climbing in a single ride; High-altitude ride above 2,000m to gauge personal altitude response; Cobblestone descending practice — confident handling on rough surfaces at speed

### Grasshopper Adventure Series
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 70 mi | — | Northern California | January | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/grasshopper-series/

The granddaddy of gravel serves up 70 miles of Northern California's hidden roads with enough climbing to remind you why it started this whole movement. The Grasshopper Adventure Series throws 70 miles of Northern California's most eclectic terrain at you—think wine country roads that suddenly dump you onto logging roads, then back to pavement without warning. This isn't just gravel grinding or road racing; it's bike handling boot camp where your line choice and gear selection get tested every few miles. The elevation gain sits somewhere between 4,500-9,116 feet depending on which Hopper you're riding, but the real challenge is the constant terrain switching that turns your legs into confused mush. Signature challenge: The Grasshopper doesn't just test your fitness—it tests your equipment choices and bike handling across terrain that changes every few miles. You'll climb 4,500+ feet while switching between pavement, packed gravel, loose dirt, and sometimes singletrack, making this less of a race and more of a northern California terrain survival course. Show up on the wrong bike or without solid handling skills, and you'll spend 70 miles getting schooled. If you can handle 4,500-9,116 feet of elevation on mixed terrain and navigate without hand-holding, this is your history lesson in what gravel racing actually is. Bring a gravel bike with 38-42c tires and the fitness for sustained climbing — the self-sufficiency requirement isn't posturing. Skip it if you need constant aid stations or can't fix a flat solo.

### Hotter'N Hell Hundred
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 100 mi | 1,700 ft | Wichita Falls, Texas, USA | August | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/hottern-hell-hundred/

100 miles. 100 degrees. 10,000 riders. The largest and most hostile century ride in America, where the heat is the course and survival is the victory. One hundred miles of flat, exposed North Texas prairie in conditions that redefine the word 'hot.' The course loops through Wichita and Clay counties on paved farm-to-market roads, starting and finishing in downtown Wichita Falls. There are no climbs worth naming, no technical features, no scenic distractions — just relentless sun, wind, and heat radiating off pavement. The challenge is entirely atmospheric. The route's most famous feature is Hell's Gate at mile 61, where riders must arrive by 12:30 PM or be diverted to the shorter 75-mile route. Everything about this course is designed to test whether you can manage your body temperature, hydration, and pacing across 100 miles of hostile conditions. Signature challenge: The heat. Full stop. One hundred miles on flat roads is a standard century. One hundred miles on flat roads at 100-108F with a heat index over 111F and relentless wind is a survival exercise that tests hydration strategy, heat acclimatization, and mental resilience in ways that climbing and technical terrain cannot. CamelBak was invented here because the conditions demanded a solution that did not yet exist. That tells you everything. Hotter'N Hell Hundred is not the hardest century in America. It is not the most scenic. It is not the most technical. It is the most hostile — and that hostility is the entire point. Every late August, 10,000+ cyclists assemble in downtown Wichita Falls, Texas, and voluntarily ride 100 miles through conditions that would make a sensible person stay indoors with the air conditioning on full blast. Temperatures crack 100F by mid-morning. The heat index exceeds 111F. The wind blows hot and gritty across flat, shadeless prairie. And yet they come — year after year, decade after decade, since 1982 — because surviving HHH is a cycling rite of passage that no other event replicates. This is the ride that made a paramedic duct-tape an IV bag to his jersey and accidentally invent CamelBak. If that does not tell you everything you need to know about the conditions, nothing will. If you live in Texas or the southern plains, this is a mandatory bucket-list century. If you are flying in from out of state, it is worth the trip exactly once — to prove to yourself that you can ride 100 miles when the thermometer says you should not. Train your heat tolerance for at least 6 weeks beforehand. Arrive hydrated, start early, respect Hell's Gate at mile 61, and do not underestimate the Texas wind. The multi-day festival and Friday criterium make it a worthy cycling weekend even beyond the century ride. Non-negotiables: Heat acclimatization — minimum 6 weeks of progressive heat training before the event; Hydration strategy tested and locked — practice race-day fluid and electrolyte intake on long training rides in heat; Century distance completed in training — at least one 80-100 mile ride, preferably in warm conditions

### La Leggendaria Charly Gaul
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 88 mi | 13,123 ft | Trento, Trentino-Alto Adige, Italy | July | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/la-leggendaria-charly-gaul/

141 km and 4,000m of climbing to the summit where Charly Gaul collapsed into legend in the 1956 Giro — UCI-caliber racing at a regional gran fondo price. A gran fondo that builds toward a single, defining moment: the summit finish on Monte Bondone at 1,650m. The granfondo route from Trento loops through the Valle dei Laghi and Cembra Valley before climbing Monte Bondone twice — once as an intermediate test, once as the grand finale. The course design is classical Italian: a long day through varied terrain, building toward a decisive summit finish that rewards the strongest climbers. The emotional weight of finishing at the exact spot where Charly Gaul collapsed in 1956 elevates the physical challenge into something more meaningful. Signature challenge: The second ascent of Monte Bondone: 22 km at 7.6% average after 120 km of riding and 3,000m of prior climbing. The same climb you did 60 km ago, but on legs that have nothing left. Summit finish at 1,650m at the exact spot where Charly Gaul became a legend. La Leggendaria Charly Gaul exists because of one day in cycling history: June 8, 1956, when Luxembourg's Charly Gaul — the 'Angel of the Mountains' — rode alone through fog and ice to the summit of Monte Bondone, collapsing at the finish and securing the Giro d'Italia. Seventy years later, 2,000-5,000 riders retrace his path from Trento's Cathedral Square to the same summit at 1,650m, now in the July heat rather than June snow.

The granfondo is not gentle about it. 141 km and 4,000 meters of climbing, including Monte Bondone done twice — once as a warm-up that ends with a descent back to the valley, and once as the final 22 km summit finish that decides the race. The climb averages 7.6% for 22 km with sections punching to 15%. On the second ascent, after 120+ km of riding in July heat through the Valle dei Laghi and Cembra Valley, those 15% sections feel like walls. The mediofondo at 57 km with 2,000m of climbing offers a more direct path to the emotional summit finish.

UCI Gran Fondo World Series status gives the event competitive teeth. Age-group riders are genuinely racing for World Championship qualification slots, and the field reflects this — La Leggendaria hosted the actual UCI Worlds in 2013 and 2022. The Luxembourg connection (the ACC Contern club co-organizes) adds a unique cross-border dimension. Trento as a host city provides excellent infrastructure, cultural depth, and accessibility. This is not the most dramatic landscape in the Italian Alps — the Dolomites are 50 km north — but the Monte Bondone summit finish, at the exact spot where Gaul collapsed into legend, delivers an emotional punch that no amount of scenery can replicate. Ride this if you want a UCI Gran Fondo World Series qualifier with genuine competitive depth, historical significance, and a summit finish that connects you to one of cycling's greatest mountain performances. Skip it if you need spectacular Dolomite scenery (go north to the Maratona), cannot handle 30C+ valley heat in July, or are looking for a non-competitive experience. Non-negotiables: Sustained climbing ability for 60-90 minute efforts; Heat management strategy; Pacing discipline for double-climb format

### Pisgah Monster Cross
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 50 mi | 8,000 ft | Brevard, North Carolina (Pisgah National Forest) | October | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/pisgah-monster-cross/

Pisgah's gravel roads will remind you that 8,777 feet of climbing doesn't negotiate, even when wrapped in Appalachian eye candy. 50 miles, 8,000 ft. 60% gravel, 40% singletrack/dirt. Pisgah National Forest. Technical Appalachian terrain. Technical rating: 5/5. Signature challenge: Technical Appalachian terrain (5/5) + steep (8,000 ft / 50mi). Pisgah Monster Cross is Southeast gravel classic—50 miles, 8,000 ft of technical Appalachian terrain. Founded ~2012. Pisgah National Forest. 60% gravel, 40% singletrack/dirt. Technical rating: 5/5—rocks, roots, steep descents. 300-400 riders. Brevard small mountain town. Late October fall conditions (40-65°F). If you want technical Appalachian gravel, this is it. If you want smooth roads or beginner-friendly, skip it. Dropbar MTB or gravel with MTB capability required. 15-20% DNF. You need legitimate climbing legs and comfort grinding for 14 hours through National Forest terrain that rewards bike handling over pure watts. The three aid stations mean you're mostly self-sufficient between long stretches of Pisgah's relentless rollers. Show up undertrained to this one and you'll discover new definitions of suffering before that 10PM cutoff.

### RAGBRAI (Register's Annual Great Bicycle Ride Across Iowa)
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 391 mi | 16,027 ft | Across Iowa, USA (Missouri River to Mississippi River, west to east) | July | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/ragbrai/

America's oldest, largest, and longest multi-day bicycle ride. 53 years of Iowa towns turning themselves inside out for 10,000+ riders. RAGBRAI is not a race. It is not even really a ride, in the way most cyclists understand rides. It is a 400-mile rolling block party across the entire state of Iowa, powered by pedals and sustained by the most extraordinary host community in sports. You will ride through towns where the entire population has turned out to feed you pie, spray you with garden hoses, and blast classic rock from a flatbed trailer. You will camp in church yards, drink beer at breakfast, and discover that Iowa is not flat. You will dip your tire in the Missouri River on Saturday and the Mississippi River the following Saturday and wonder where the week went.

Since 1973, RAGBRAI has defined American recreational cycling. It inspired over 200 similar events worldwide. It has been visited upon all 99 Iowa counties. Nearly half a million riders have completed it. The oldest, largest, and longest multi-day bicycle ride in the world is not a race — and that is precisely why it matters. RAGBRAI proves that the best reason to ride a bicycle is to ride a bicycle, preferably through a small town that has been waiting all year for you to arrive.

Do not come to RAGBRAI expecting a challenge. Come expecting a transformation. Come expecting the farmer playing saxophone on the hilltop at sunrise, the 90-year-old woman handing out cookies from her porch, and the moment on Day 5 when you realize you have been smiling for five straight days and you cannot remember the last time that happened. That is RAGBRAI. That is Iowa. That is cycling at its most fundamentally, unapologetically joyful. You do not race RAGBRAI. You ride it. You experience it. You eat pie at every stop and drink beer before noon and sleep in a tent in a stranger's yard and discover that Iowa is hillier, friendlier, and more beautiful than you imagined. If you have ever wanted to know what happens when an entire state decides to throw a week-long party for cyclists, register for RAGBRAI. Registration opens November each year and fills steadily. $250 for the week. Bring a bike, a tent, sunscreen, and an appetite. Leave the power meter at home. Non-negotiables: Multi-day endurance base — build to consecutive 60-80 mile days on the bike; Heat acclimation — train in warm conditions or layer up to simulate July Iowa humidity; Saddle and bike fit dialed — test your exact setup on 3+ consecutive riding days

### Rasputitsa
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 46 mi | 5,000 ft | Burke, Vermont | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/rasputitsa/

Vermont's 10-year-old mud lottery where 5,000 feet of climbing meets April's bipolar weather and your cross training actually matters. 46 miles of Vermont Class 4 roads (unmaintained dirt). 5,000 ft elevation. Self-supported (NO aid). Mud guaranteed (April). Technical rating: 4/5. Signature challenge: April mud + self-supported + Class 4 technical roads. Mud is guaranteed, aid is not. Rasputitsa is Vermont's early season mud fest—46 miles of Class 4 roads (unmaintained dirt), 5,000 ft elevation, self-supported (NO aid stations), and guaranteed mud. Founded 2016, 10th anniversary 2026. 'Rasputitsa' is Russian for 'mud season'. April in Vermont = mud. Class 4 roads = technical. Self-supported = hardcore. 600+ riders. Burke Northeast Kingdom. If you want Vermont hardcore suffering, this is it. If you want smooth gravel and aid stations, run away. If you can handle 46 miles of sustained climbing in conditions that range from perfect to apocalyptic, Rasputitsa delivers authentic Northeast Kingdom suffering without the ego. The 5,000 feet of elevation isn't negotiable, and April weather means you might get snow, mud, or sunshine—sometimes all three. Skip this if you need predictable conditions or hate climbing.

### Styrkeprøven
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 335 mi | 11,800 ft | Trondheim to Oslo, Norway | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/styrkeproven/

540 km from Trondheim to Oslo over Dovrefjell. The Strength Test since 1967. A 540 km point-to-point journey from Norway's third city across the Dovrefjell mountain plateau to the capital. Front-loaded climbing over the first 160 km, followed by a long descent and valley run to Oslo. The Dovrefjell crossing is the crux — exposed mountain plateau at 1,000+ meters with unpredictable weather. The second half from Lillehammer to Oslo is predominantly flat along Lake Mjøsa. Signature challenge: The Dovrefjell crossing. Eighty kilometers of exposed mountain plateau at 1,000+ meters where the weather can shift from calm to hostile in minutes. Snow in June. Headwinds of 15 m/s. Near-freezing temperatures when you left Trondheim in 18C. This is where Styrkeprøven separates those who brought enough layers from those who did not. Styrkeprøven is 540 km from Trondheim to Oslo across the Dovrefjell mountain plateau in the midsummer night. It is not technically demanding, it is not a race, and it will never be cheap. What it is: one of the most transformative point-to-point cycling experiences on earth. The name means 'The Strength Test' and 57 years of history confirm it earns that name every June. If ultra-distance road cycling through wild Norwegian terrain in the midsummer night sounds like your idea of a life experience — absolutely yes. Budget for Norway's costs and respect Dovrefjell's weather. If you want a competitive race with a large international field, look elsewhere. Non-negotiables: Progressive long rides reaching 300+ km in training, with at least one 400+ km ride; Cold-weather riding kit tested in 5C or below, including windproof and waterproof layers; Nutrition plan for 18-24 hours including hot food and overnight fueling strategy

### The Rad Dirt Fest
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 113 mi | 10,613 ft | Trinidad, Colorado | September | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/the-rad/

High-desert climbing race with Spanish Peaks views and 10,000+ feet of vertical. Long sustained climbs through high-desert Colorado. Spanish Peaks views and 10,000+ feet of vertical define the challenge. Signature challenge: The 18-mile climb to 9,000 ft. Long, steep, at altitude. This is where races are won and lost. The Rad is a climbing race set against stunning Spanish Peaks scenery. The 18-mile climb to 9,000 ft with grades up to 18% defines the event. Well-organized Life Time production with challenging terrain. Yes if you love climbing and want a challenging altitude race in stunning Colorado scenery. The 18-mile climb is the real deal.

### UCI Gran Fondo World Championships
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 87 mi | 7,776 ft | Venue rotates annually (2026: Niseko, Hokkaido, Japan) | August | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/uci-gran-fondo-world-championships/

The only UCI World Championship for amateur road cyclists. Qualify through 36 global events. Race 2,000 riders from 58 countries. Win the rainbow jersey. Course character changes completely with each host venue, but the UCI imposes design principles that create consistency: the Gran Fondo road race must challenge climbing ability, reward tactical racing, and culminate in a decisive finale. The 2026 Niseko course epitomizes this: 140 km through Hokkaido's volcanic mountain landscape, starting near sea level, building to an 840m main climb at the halfway mark, and finishing with a steep final kilometer that rewards explosive power on tired legs. The Medio Fondo at 80 km delivers the same decisive finish with 1,572m of climbing. The individual time trial on a flat 16.3 km course tests pure power. Three disciplines, three different demands, one championship. Signature challenge: The qualification system IS the signature challenge. Before you even reach the start line, you must race a UCI-sanctioned qualifier event and finish in the top 25% of your age group. Then you must travel to the championship venue — a different country each year — and race against 2,000 of the best amateur cyclists from 58 nations. The course varies, but the depth of competition does not. Winning a rainbow jersey requires not just fitness, but a year-long competitive commitment. The UCI Gran Fondo World Championships are the only event in amateur road cycling where you can earn the rainbow jersey — the single most recognizable symbol of cycling supremacy. This is not a participation sportive. It is a qualified-entry world championship with anti-doping, commissaire panels, and age-group classifications that attract the strongest amateur road cycling field assembled anywhere on the planet. You earn your start by finishing in the top 25% of your age group at one of 36 qualifying events worldwide. Then you race 2,000 riders from 58 countries on a course that changes every year — from Italian mountains to Danish flatlands to Japanese volcanoes. The 2026 edition in Niseko, Hokkaido is the first ever held in Asia: 140 km with 2,370m of climbing through volcanic mountain terrain, 1.5 hours from Tokyo. The rainbow jersey awaits at the finish. One winner per age group. For the rest, there is the knowledge that you raced the deepest amateur field in the world. Yes if you are a competitive age-group cyclist who wants the ultimate test against your global peers. Yes if the rainbow jersey means something to you. Yes if you want a multi-year global cycling adventure — different country, different course, every year. Skip if you want a fixed, repeatable course to benchmark against. Skip if the qualification requirement and international travel expense are dealbreakers. Skip if you want a participation experience — this is a race. Non-negotiables: Qualify at a UCI Gran Fondo World Series event — top 25% in your age group; Race-specific fitness for the championship course profile; Peloton tactics and race craft for a competitive field

### 947 Ride Joburg
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 60 mi | 3,937 ft | Johannesburg, South Africa | October | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/947-ride-joburg/

Africa's greatest mass-participation cycling event. Own the streets of Joburg. A rolling 97km urban road race at altitude through Johannesburg's streets, highways, and suburbs. The course is deceptively challenging - the constant altitude above 1,500m and rolling M1 highway sections wear riders down more than the elevation numbers suggest. Signature challenge: The M1 highway. Seventeen kilometers of rolling highway that car drivers barely notice but cyclists feel in their legs. At 1,500m+ altitude, with 60km already in the legs, the false flats and headwinds of the M1 South section separate those who paced well from those who went out too hard on the Kyalami circuit. The 947 Ride Joburg is not the hardest race you will ever do. It is not the most technical, the longest, or the most remote. But it might be the most electric. Twenty thousand riders taking over the M1 highway, lapping Kyalami, and finishing at FNB Stadium in Soweto creates an atmosphere that makes your chest tight before you even clip in. The race is Johannesburg itself - ambitious, energetic, slightly chaotic, and completely unforgettable. If you want to understand South African cycling culture, this is where you start. If you want to experience the energy of 20,000+ riders owning the streets of one of Africa's great cities, this race delivers. It is not an extreme endurance test or a technical challenge. It is a celebration of cycling in Johannesburg, and the atmosphere alone makes it worth the entry fee. For international visitors, the favorable exchange rate and the unique experience of riding the M1 highway and Kyalami circuit make it a compelling bucket-list event. Non-negotiables: Altitude acclimatization for sea-level visitors; Group riding skills for large peloton; Pacing strategy for M1 highway section

### AIDS/LifeCycle
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 545 mi | 25,000 ft | San Francisco to Los Angeles, California, USA | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/aids-lifecycle/

The most emotionally powerful cycling event ever created. 545 miles of purpose, community, and remembrance down the California coast. Seven days of California coastal beauty and emotional intensity. The route traverses the full diversity of the state's Central Coast — from San Francisco fog through redwood forests, over mountain ridges, through agricultural valleys, past wine country, over coastal passes, and along the Pacific Coast Highway to a Santa Monica finish. The terrain is varied but always paved, with significant climbing concentrated on Days 1, 3, 4, and 6. The real challenge is cumulative: 545 miles over 7 consecutive days in the saddle, compounded by camping, emotional weight, and California's variable June weather. Signature challenge: The cumulative toll of 545 miles over 7 days. No single day is impossible, but the relentless accumulation of miles, climbing, weather, camping, and emotional weight creates an endurance challenge that transforms riders. The body adapts; the spirit grows. By Day 7, riding into Santa Monica feels like crossing into a different version of your life. AIDS/LifeCycle is not a race. It is not a gran fondo. It is not a charity ride in the way most people understand charity rides. It is 545 miles of collective grief, joy, defiance, and love, pedaled down the California coast by thousands of people who refuse to let a disease define or defeat their community. You will climb the Quadbuster with disco music blasting. You will cry at camp listening to names of the dead read aloud. You will finish in Santa Monica having raised thousands of dollars and having been fundamentally changed by the experience. Over 30 years, this event raised more than $300 million and created a community that transcends cycling. The 2025 final ride ended an era — but the legacy is permanent. AIDS/LifeCycle ended in 2025 after 30 years and $300 million raised. If you rode it, you know what it meant. If you never did, the successor event Cycle to Zero (3-day, SFAF-produced) launches in May 2026. The 545-mile journey from San Francisco to Los Angeles is gone, but the community and mission endure. This was never about the miles — it was about why you rode them. Non-negotiables: Multi-day endurance base — build to back-to-back 80-mile days in training; Saddle time and bike fit dialed — test your setup on 3+ consecutive riding days; Fundraising complete — $3,500 minimum raised before ride week

### Belgian Waffle Ride North Carolina
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 131 mi | 13,000 ft | Hendersonville, North Carolina | October | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/bwr-north-carolina/

The Appalachian meat grinder that puts 29% fire roads and hike-a-bike sections in your face whether you're ready or not. BWR NC is the hell spawn of European classics and Appalachian brutality—40% unroad surfaces that mock your bike choice and 13,000 feet of climbing that laughs at your FTP. This isn't gravel racing, it's survival cycling through Blue Ridge punishment chambers where 29% fire roads meet creek crossings and your legs meet reality. The course dips into South Carolina just to add interstate humiliation to your suffering. Signature challenge: The hike-a-bike rate approaches 100% on certain sections—not because you can't ride them, but because the course design ensures traffic jams on unrideable singletrack climbs. BWR NC weaponizes bottlenecks, turning technical skill into patience training while your heart rate spikes from walking your bike up 25% grades. Show up if you've got a 30-tooth chainring, legitimate mountain bike handling skills, and the ego to walk your bike in front of strangers. The 13,000 feet of climbing through Carolina mountains will expose fitness gaps fast, and those singletrack bottlenecks mean pack position matters more than watts. Skip this if you think gravel racing should be mostly rideable.

### Cape Town Cycle Tour
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 67.7 mi | 4,003 ft | Cape Town, Western Cape, South Africa | March | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/cape-town-cycle-tour/

The world's largest individually timed cycling event. 35,000 riders. Chapman's Peak. The entire Cape Peninsula on closed roads. The Cape Town Cycle Tour is not the hardest cycling event you will ever do. At 109 km with 1,220m of climbing, it is shorter and flatter than dozens of European sportives. What it is, unambiguously, is the most spectacular urban cycling event on Earth. The route circles the entire Cape Peninsula — Table Mountain, Chapman's Peak, Cape Point, two oceans, fishing villages, and the Atlantic Seaboard — on closed roads with tens of thousands of other riders. Chapman's Peak Drive, a cliff road carved into the mountainside 200 meters above the Atlantic, is the single most visually stunning segment of any mass-participation cycling event anywhere.

Born from a 1977 protest ride advocating for cycling infrastructure — when Bill Mylrea and John Stegmann organized the 'Big Ride In' — it has grown from 525 riders in 1978 to the world's largest individually timed event and a Cape Town civic institution. The 2026 addition to the UCI Gran Fondo World Series adds competitive teeth to what was already a bucket-list ride. Tyler Lange shattered the 17-year-old course record in 2025 with a 2:25:48 finish, while Pia Grunewald's 50 km solo breakaway in the women's race demonstrated that serious racing lives at the sharp end of this massive field.

The Southeast 'Cape Doctor' wind is the variable you cannot control — it has cancelled the event outright (2017), stopped it mid-race (2009), and shortened it to 47 km (2015 fire). But when conditions cooperate, and the Cape Peninsula delivers one of its flawless March days, there is simply nothing else like it in cycling. You do not come here for suffering. You come here for Chapman's Peak. Yes if you want the most spectacular coastal cycling route on Earth and the energy of 28,000-35,000 riders sharing closed roads around the Cape Peninsula. Yes if you want UCI Gran Fondo World Championship qualification in a stunning setting at an unbeatable price point. Skip if you need serious climbing or endurance challenge — 109 km with 1,220m of climbing is moderate. Skip if the Southeast wind gamble (potential event cancellation) is a dealbreaker for a long-haul trip. Non-negotiables: Group riding and crosswind skills — confident in a peloton of 200+ riders in variable wind; Wind riding experience — ability to sustain power in headwinds and crosswinds for 3+ hours; Heat and hydration strategy tested in warm conditions

### Death Ride — Tour of the California Alps
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 103 mi | 14,000 ft | Markleeville, Alpine County, California | July | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/death-ride/

5 passes. 14,000 feet. The California Alps' ultimate climbing challenge since 1978. Relentless climbing. The up-and-back format on each pass means you're constantly ascending or descending. There is almost no flat riding. The passes come in waves — each summit brings the descent that takes you to the base of the next climb. By pass 4, the cumulative altitude exposure and climbing fatigue make every gradient feel steeper. Signature challenge: Ebbetts Pass — the steepest, most dramatic, and highest summit. Climbing to 8,732 ft on tired legs in the Sierra Nevada is the Death Ride's defining moment. The Death Ride is American road cycling's ultimate climbing challenge. 5 passes. 14,000 feet. One of the least-populated counties in California. No prize money, no timing chips, no Instagram-ready finish line — just you and the Sierra Nevada. Since 1978, serious cyclists have made the pilgrimage to Markleeville to test themselves against the California Alps. The name isn't hyperbole — it's a warning and a promise. If you finish all 5 passes, you've earned something that can't be bought. Yes if you want the defining high-altitude climbing experience in the American West. Yes if you can handle sustained riding above 8,000 ft. Skip if you need race infrastructure or a competitive field. Non-negotiables: Mountain climbing endurance — back-to-back pass simulations; Altitude exposure or preparation for riding above 8,000 ft; Pacing plan with 3:00 PM cutoff reverse-engineered

### Dragon Ride L'Etape Wales
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 143 mi | 11,483 ft | Margam Park, Port Talbot, South Wales, UK | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/dragon-ride/

The UK's oldest and hardest sportive. 230 km through the Brecon Beacons. Devil's Elbow at 10.3%. L'Etape by Tour de France branding earned through 22 years of Welsh mountain suffering. The Dragon Ride is the event that invented the UK sportive scene. Before it existed, British cyclists went to France to suffer on mountain roads. Lou Lusardi looked at the Brecon Beacons in 2004, saw Italian gran fondo terrain hiding in plain sight, and built an event that has drawn 4,700+ riders annually for over two decades. The Gran Fondo at 230 km with 3,500 m of climbing is a legitimate monster — longer and harder than most L'Etape du Tour editions. The Brecon Beacons deliver climbing that riders describe as the closest the UK gets to the Alps: Rhigos grinds on for 11 km, Black Mountain feels like the end of the world, and Devil's Elbow hits you with 10.3% through hairpins that would not look out of place on the Stelvio. Welsh weather adds a dimension that Italian gran fondos never deliver — you might get baked in June sunshine or hammered by horizontal rain, and the organisers tell you to pack for both.

The 2016 ASO partnership brought L'Etape by Tour de France branding and international credibility, but the Dragon Ride had already earned its reputation the hard way: 22 years of riders crawling up Welsh mountains and declaring it the hardest thing they have ever done on a bike. Starting from the gothic grandeur of Margam Park's Tudor mansion, climbing through increasingly wild terrain until you are alone on a windswept mountain with sheep for company, then descending through valleys that feel like they belong in Middle-earth — this is an experience that no amount of branding can manufacture. It is authentically, brutally, beautifully Welsh. The 2027 Tour de France Grand Depart will include a stage cresting Rhigos mountain, bringing international attention to climbs Welsh cyclists have known for decades. Yes if you want to ride Britain's most iconic sportive on roads that rival Alpine passes for scenery and suffering. Yes if a 230 km day with 3,500 m of climbing through Welsh mountain weather sounds like your definition of a good time. Skip if you need competitive racing — this is participatory, not a race. Skip if you cannot handle unpredictable weather — Welsh mountains do not care about your forecast. Skip if you demand continental European sportive prestige — this is the biggest fish in the UK pond. Non-negotiables: Sustained climbing endurance — back-to-back 30-60 minute threshold efforts on 5-10% gradients; 200+ km endurance base with 2,500+ m of climbing in a single ride; Wet weather descending confidence and full foul-weather kit

### Gravel Brazil
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 62 mi | 6,562 ft | Balneário Camboriú, Santa Catarina, Brazil | September | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gravel-brazil/

Brazil's first UCI gravel race arrives with World Championship stakes and zero track record—you're betting on potential over proof. 100 km, 2,000 m. 80% gravel, 20% dirt. Brazilian coastal mountains. Steep climbs, technical descents. Technical rating: 4/5. Signature challenge: Steep Brazilian coastal mountains (2,000 m / 100 km) + technical descents + heat/humidity. Gravel Brazil is Brazilian coastal mountains—100 km, 2,000 m elevation. Founded ~2019. 80% gravel, 20% dirt. Steep climbs, technical descents (loose gravel). 800-1,000 riders. Balneário Camboriú beach town. September spring conditions (18-28°C). Affordable entry (R$400-R$600 / $80-$120). If you want Brazilian gravel adventure with beach town vibes, this is it. If you want local/domestic race, skip it. International destination race. If you need UCI points or want to say you raced Brazil's inaugural gravel event, this makes sense. The 6,562 feet of climbing over 62 miles suggests sustained suffering, not punchy attacks. Show up ready for humidity, unknown surfaces, and the chaos that comes with first-year events where nothing goes according to plan.

### Heck of the North
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 100 mi | 4,500 ft | Two Harbors, Minnesota | September | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/heck-of-the-north/

Minnesota's sneakiest technical test disguised as a scenic fall colors ride. Constant rolling terrain with no extended flats. 4,500 ft over 100 miles = lots of short, punchy climbs. No sustained mountain-style climbs, but relentless ups and downs. 60% snowmobile trails (roots, rocks, narrow paths) make this a technical skills test as much as a fitness test. Think XC mountain biking on a gravel bike. Signature challenge: First extended technical test. Roots, rocks, narrow paths. If you lack MTB skills, this will be slow and scary. Sets the tone for the race. Heck of the North is what happens when you route a gravel race through Minnesota snowmobile trails at peak fall foliage. This is NOT smooth gravel—it's technical, rooty, rocky, narrow-path riding that rewards MTB skills, not just power. If you're coming from flat Midwest gravel races, this will surprise you. 60% of the course is on snowmobile trails: roots, rocks, tight turns, line-choice decisions. You're not just pedaling—you're bunnyhopping roots, picking lines over rocks, and keeping weight centered on loose descents. The fall foliage is stunning—peak color in late September means riding through tunnels of gold, orange, and red along Lake Superior's North Shore. The post-race vibe is classic small-town Minnesota: hot soup, sandwiches, and volunteers who genuinely care. It's a community center potluck, not a corporate expo. If you've got mountain bike skills and don't mind wrestling roots and rocks for 100 miles, this is your jam. The snowmobile trails demand 40mm+ tires and actual bike handling—your road racing background won't save you here. Skip it if you're looking for fast gravel or hate technical terrain that slows you to 8 mph.

### Mt. Washington Auto Road Bicycle Hillclimb (MWARBH)
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 7.6 mi | 4,678 ft | Gorham, New Hampshire | August | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/mt-washington-hillclimb/

7.6 miles. 4,678 feet. 12% average grade. Summit finish on the mountain with the worst weather in America. Since 1973. There is no subtlety to the Mt. Washington Auto Road Bicycle Hillclimb. It is 7.6 miles of going uphill at 12% average gradient until you reach the summit of the highest peak in the Northeastern United States, a mountain so hostile that it holds the world record for the highest wind gust ever directly measured on Earth's surface. Since 1973, cyclists have lined up at the base of the Auto Road to race the clock, the gradient, and whatever weather the mountain decides to deliver. Tom Danielson did it in 49:24. Tyler Hamilton did it four times (though his records were vacated). Ian Boswell set the current mark at 50:18. Most riders take 60-90 minutes. All of them suffer.

The Mt. Washington Hillclimb is not a long race. It is not a technical race. It is not an altitude race. It is a gradient race on a mountain with a personality disorder — a 6,288-foot peak in New Hampshire that produces weather systems normally reserved for peaks three times its height. The 2022 completion of the road paving project reset the records and the conversation: every time set on the fully paved road is a modern mark. The 50th edition that year was a line in the sand. Everything before it was a different race on a different surface. Everything after is the pure, paved truth: how fast can you ride uphill at 12% for 7.6 miles into the worst weather in America?

This is not a race you enter casually. The $350 entry fee, the remote New Hampshire location, and the single-digit mileage all conspire to make this a deliberate choice. You come to Mt. Washington because you want to test yourself against the steepest sustained road climb in the Eastern United States. You stay because the summit finish — fleece blanket in hand, legs destroyed, staring out across the Presidential Range — is one of the most earned moments in American cycling. Yes if you are a hill climb specialist, power-to-weight obsessive, or simply need to know if you can ride up the steepest sustained paved climb in the Eastern US. Yes if you want a bucket-list summit finish on the highest peak in the Northeast. Skip if you want distance, technical terrain, or a mass-participation festival atmosphere. Skip if remote New Hampshire logistics are a dealbreaker. Non-negotiables: Hill climb-specific gearing — compact crankset with 34x32 or lower; Sustained threshold climbing — 45-60 minute continuous climbing intervals in training; Cold-weather summit layers — vest, arm warmers, full-finger gloves

### Gran Fondo Nove Colli
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 124 mi | 12,513 ft | Cesenatico, Emilia-Romagna, Italy | May | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/nove-colli/

The world's oldest gran fondo. Nine hills. 200 km. Pantani's hometown. Since 1971. Nine punchy hills strung across 200 km of Emilia-Romagna countryside, starting and finishing at the Adriatic coast. The hills are not long — the longest is 9 km — but they are steep, with Barbotto's 18% pitches standing out as the route's signature gradient. Between hills, the terrain rolls through vineyards, olive groves, and small stone villages. The route is a loop from Cesenatico, climbing inland into the pre-Apennine foothills before descending back to the coast. The rhythm is distinctive: climb hard for 15-30 minutes, descend, roll through the valley, then climb again. Nine times. Signature challenge: Barbotto at 18% — the steepest sustained gradient on the route and the climb that gives Nove Colli its fearsome reputation among punchy-hill specialists. But the real challenge is the cumulative effect: nine hills over 200 km, each one requiring an effort that leaves you slightly more depleted for the next. The ninth hill is always harder than the first. Nove Colli is where the gran fondo was born. In 1971, 17 riders set out from Cesenatico — Marco Pantani's hometown, cycling club named after Fausto Coppi — to ride nine hills in the Emilia-Romagna countryside. Fifty-five years later, 10,000 riders do the same. The hills are not Alpine monsters — they are short, steep, punchy climbs that reward explosive power and punish weak legs. Barbotto at 18% is as steep as anything in the Tour de France. The route is 200 km of rolling Italian countryside, stone villages, vineyards, and espresso stops. It is not the most spectacular event in Europe, not the hardest, and not the most prestigious. It is simply the original. Yes if you want to ride the original gran fondo in the homeland of Italian cycling legends. Yes if nine punchy hills with 18% gradients over 200 km of Romagnol countryside is your pace. Skip if you want Alpine spectacle — these are hills, not mountains. Skip if you need English-language support — this event is authentically Italian. Non-negotiables: Steep gradient training — repeated efforts on 12-18% grades; Repeated-climb endurance — 5+ distinct climbs in a single training ride; 200 km distance capability with climbing — full dress rehearsal

### Rad Dirt Fest
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 62 mi | 8,202 ft | Salida, Colorado | September | gravel
