Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/rad-dirt-fest/

The Rad serves up champagne gravel and Spanish Peaks views, but that 31-mile no-service stretch will expose every training shortcut you took. 100 km, 2,500 m. 80% gravel, 20% dirt. Colorado Rockies—high altitude (2,200-3,000 m), technical climbs. Technical rating: 4/5. Signature challenge: 100 km + 2,500 m elevation + high altitude (2,200-3,000 m). Colorado Rockies. Rad Dirt Fest is Colorado Rockies adventure—100 km, 2,500 m elevation. Founded ~2018. 80% gravel, 20% dirt. High altitude (2,200-3,000 m), technical climbs. 500-800 riders. Salida, CO (great town). September autumn conditions (10-25°C). Affordable entry ($150-$200). If you want Colorado Rockies high-altitude gravel with stunning scenery, this is it. If you need sea-level race, skip it. Altitude is the challenge. If you can hold steady watts for five hours at altitude without external validation, this is your race. The gravel is buttery smooth, but 8,900 feet doesn't negotiate with your sea-level fitness. Show up undertrained and that remote backcountry section becomes a very expensive therapy session.

### Southern Cross Gravel
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 100 mi | 5,000 ft | Dahlonega, Georgia | March | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/southern-cross-gravel/

North Georgia's wine country serves up proper climbing with a civilized finish—Woody Gap separates the tourists from the locals. Constant elevation change with no extended flats. Punchy, steep pitches (8-12% typical) spread throughout. This is NOT a power race—it's a climbing and bike-handling test. North Georgia Appalachian terrain: relentless rollers, technical descents, and forest road gravel. Signature challenge: The first major test: 1,200 ft over 5 miles (avg 5%, pitches to 12%). You're barely warmed up when it starts. Riders on road gearing walk here. Sets the tone for the day. Southern Cross is what happens when you build a gravel race for climbers, descenders, and wine drinkers. This is NOT Kansas. It's the North Georgia Appalachians—steep, punchy, unrelenting. The Montaluce Winery start/finish is chef's kiss: you suffer for 5-7 hours on red clay climbs, then roll into a vineyard where volunteers hand you wine, BBQ, and a chair with a mountain view. It's the only gravel race where you might actually want to stay at the finish line longer than it took to ride the race. The course is honest suffering—no gimmicks, just real North Georgia topography. If you're a diesel engine who thrives on sustained power, this will hurt. If you're a punchy climber who can recover on descents, you'll love it. If you can handle 5,000 feet of punchy Georgia climbs and aren't fazed by potential mud lottery conditions, this delivers solid suffering with the best post-race scene in the Southeast. Skip it if you need extensive aid station coddling or panic at the thought of 35-degree starts. Compact gearing and 40mm+ tires aren't suggestions—they're survival equipment.

### Vermont Overland
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 54 mi | 7,000 ft | Woodstock, Vermont | August | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/vermont-overland/

Vermont's steepest 55 miles disguised as a pastoral New England postcard. 54 miles of Vermont pavé. 7,000 ft elevation. 60% gravel, 30% dirt, 10% pavement. 7 sectors rough unmaintained roads. Technical rating: 4/5. Signature challenge: 7,000 ft elevation over 54 miles = relentless. Vermont pavé sectors = technical. Vermont Overland is OG gravel—founded 2009, one of oldest US events. 54 miles, 7,000 ft elevation. Vermont pavé—7 sectors of rough unmaintained roads like Paris-Roubaix but dirt. 500+ riders. Woodstock tourist town. Short distance (54mi) but brutal elevation (7,000 ft = relentless). Technical climbs + pavé sectors reward skills. If you want OG gravel history and Vermont technical terrain, this is it. If you want smooth roads, skip it. Those eight Class 4 sectors will humble anyone who shows up thinking this is a leaf-peeping tour. You need solid climbing legs for 7000 feet of punishment and bike handling skills for unmaintained roads that range from sketchy to bike-breaking. The 1000-rider field sells out in hours because riders who've done it know something the rest of us don't.

### Almanzo 100
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 100 mi | 6,500 ft | Spring Valley, Minnesota | May | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/almanzo-100/

The granddaddy of gravel racing where loose stone and 17% grades separate tourists from riders. The granddaddy of gravel racing rolls through Minnesota's Driftless region where glaciers never flattened the terrain. Six thousand feet of climbing over endless farm roads that transition from hard-pack early to soul-crushing loose stone as you fall apart. The hills never stop coming, and neither does the sensation you're riding through pioneer country on roads that were never meant for bikes. Signature challenge: Oriole Road at mile 90 is the soul-crusher that defines this race. GPS readings hit 17% grades on loose gravel when your legs have nothing left after 6,000 feet of climbing. It doesn't matter how strong you are—this hill breaks everyone. If you've got 6+ hours in your legs and can push through loose gravel that feels like riding in sand, this is gravel racing church. The Oriole Road climb at mile 90 will humble anyone undertrained, and the second-half stone layer turns strong groups into survival shuffles. Skip it if you need aid stations or can't handle Minnesota spring weather ranging from hypothermia-inducing rain to scorching sun.

### Arlberg Giro
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 93 mi | 8,200 ft | St. Anton am Arlberg, Tyrol, Austria | August | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/arlberg-giro/

150 km of Austrian Alpine gran fondo perfection — two passes, world-class scenery, and ski-resort-level organization in one of the Alps' most charming villages. A circular alpine gran fondo starting and finishing in St. Anton am Arlberg, crossing two major mountain passes through the Austrian states of Tyrol and Vorarlberg. The route demands sustained climbing ability and confident descending on well-maintained alpine roads with world-class mountain scenery throughout. Signature challenge: The Silvretta Hochalpenstrasse climb: 22.5 km at 5.8% average to the Bielerhöhe at 2,032m — the defining ascent of the Arlberg Giro, arriving after 90+ km of riding and the Arlbergpass already in the legs. The Arlberg Giro is the kind of event that makes you understand why European gran fondos exist as a distinct category. 150 kilometers through the Austrian Alps with 2,500 meters of climbing, two major passes, and scenery that belongs on a postcard. The organization — run by the St. Anton tourism association with 13 editions of experience — is polished without being corporate. The weekend package of night sprint, professional criterium, and Sunday gran fondo creates a genuine cycling festival in one of the Alps' most charming villages. It is not the hardest gran fondo in Austria (the Otztaler has that locked), and it is not the most prestigious (no UCI sanction, no lottery mania). But it may be the most enjoyable — the balance of challenge, scenery, organization, and village atmosphere is hard to beat. If you want a well-organized Alpine gran fondo with stunning scenery and a genuine festival atmosphere — absolutely. This is European cycling at its most enjoyable. If you are chasing UCI points, maximum vertical, or global prestige — the Maratona dles Dolomites or Otztaler Radmarathon may be better targets. If you are coming from outside Europe, combine it with a broader Alps cycling trip to justify the travel. Non-negotiables: Sustained climbing endurance; Alpine weather preparation; Descending confidence

### Bootlegger 100
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 100 mi | 10,000 ft | Dahlonega, Georgia (North Georgia mountains) | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/bootlegger-100/

Wilson Creek Gorge will eat your lunch and serve it back with a side of North Carolina suffering. 100 miles, 10,000 ft. 80% gravel, 20% dirt. North Georgia mountains. Steep climbs, technical descents. Technical rating: 4/5. Signature challenge: 10,000 ft / 100mi steep North Georgia mountains + technical descents. Bootlegger 100 is Southeast gravel staple—100 miles, 10,000 ft of North Georgia mountains. Founded ~2013. 80% gravel, 20% dirt. Steep Appalachian climbs, technical descents (loose gravel). 500-600 riders. Dahlonega small mountain town. Mid-April spring conditions (55-75°F). If you want Southeast mountain gravel, this is it. If you want flat or beginner-friendly, skip it. Technical rating 4/5. 15-20% DNF. You need proper gravel legs and mechanical confidence before diving into Pisgah Productions' playground. The terrain doesn't negotiate, and being 100 miles deep in the NC mountains isn't the place to discover your limits. Show up undertrained and you'll spend more time walking than riding.

### Copper Triangle — Colorado's Three-Pass Alpine Loop
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 79 mi | 6,500 ft | Copper Mountain Resort, Colorado | August | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/copper-triangle/

3 passes. 79 miles. Never below 9,000 feet. Colorado's premier high-altitude loop. A three-act alpine loop that never lets you forget where you are. The Copper Triangle traces a triangle through the heart of the Colorado Rockies, connecting three mountain passes through three distinct valleys. You climb past industrial mining heritage on Fremont, descend into America's highest city at Leadville, ascend through WWII military history at Camp Hale, and close the loop on the car-free Vail Pass bike path. The entire route sits above 9,000 feet — there is no low-altitude recovery. The grades are moderate, the road surfaces are good, and the scenery is relentless. What makes this ride hard is not the climbing profile on paper. It's the thin air that makes every gradient feel steeper than it reads. Signature challenge: The cumulative altitude. No single pass is a leg-breaker. The grades are moderate, the distances between passes are manageable. But the Copper Triangle never lets you below 9,000 feet. By the time you're climbing Vail Pass — your third summit above 10,000 feet — the oxygen deficit has been compounding for 65 miles. The thin air is the opponent, not the gradient. The Copper Triangle is a masterclass in what altitude does to honest cycling. 79 miles and 6,500 feet of climbing sounds manageable until you register the fine print: the entire route sits above 9,000 feet, the high point reaches 11,318, and you never get a breath of thick air. Three mountain passes — Fremont, Tennessee, Vail — through three Colorado valleys, past Leadville and Camp Hale and Minturn, finishing where you started at Copper Mountain. Twenty years of raising money for the Davis Phinney Foundation. Not the longest ride. Not the most climbing. But an altitude event that quietly exposes the gap between your sea-level fitness and what the Rocky Mountains actually demand. Yes if you want to experience what genuine high-altitude cycling feels like across three mountain passes in a single day. Yes if you value a well-organized charity ride through Colorado's most historic mountain terrain. Skip if you need race competition or if riding above 10,000 feet is a medical concern. Non-negotiables: Sustained climbing endurance — consecutive threshold efforts of 30-45 minutes; Altitude acclimatization or preparation for riding above 9,000 ft for the entire day; Early start and pacing plan that clears all three passes before 1:00 PM

### Cycle Oregon — Rally
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 250 mi | 13,000 ft | Southern Oregon — Grants Pass and Jacksonville basecamps (2026). Route and region change annually. | August | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/cycle-oregon/

Oregon's iconic multi-day touring event. 600 riders. Five days. A different Oregon every year. Cycle Oregon is Oregon distilled into a cycling event. Since 1988, this nonprofit organization has been taking riders into the corners of Oregon that most Oregonians have never seen — the Wallowa Mountains, the Applegate Valley, the high desert, the coast — and doing it with a level of care and support that turns five days on a bike into something approaching a life experience. The 2023 sunset of the legendary 7-day Classic was a genuine loss for American cycling, but the 5-day Rally format preserves the essence: a rolling community of 600 riders exploring Oregon's most spectacular landscapes, fully supported, with meals and camping and live music and the camaraderie that only comes from sharing the road for multiple days.

The price tag is real — $1,400-1,600 is not casual money — but what you get is five days of worry-free riding through terrain that will rearrange your understanding of Oregon. The route changes every year, meaning you could do Cycle Oregon for a decade and never repeat a mile. The Cycle Oregon Fund has donated millions to the rural communities that host the event, so your registration dollars are doing genuine good. The community is warm, the organization is impeccable, and the landscapes are world-class. If you want to race, go somewhere else. If you want to ride through one of the most beautiful states in America with 600 people who feel the same way, Cycle Oregon is it. You do not race Cycle Oregon — you experience it. Yes if you want a premium, fully supported multi-day cycling tour through some of the most beautiful scenery in the American West. Yes if you value community, adventure, and discovery over competition. Skip if you need a competitive event, if the $1,400+ price tag is prohibitive, or if you want a massive social scene (600 riders is intimate, not a festival). Non-negotiables: Multi-day endurance base — consecutive days of 40-60 mile rides; Climbing endurance — sustained efforts on 4-7% grades; Gear tested: bike, camping setup, layers

### GFNY Bali
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 85 mi | 7,316 ft | Lebih, Klungkung, Bali, Indonesia | February | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gfny-bali/

Climb from Bali's black-sand coast to a volcanic crater rim through rice terraces and Hindu temples — the most spectacular setting in the GFNY franchise. A point-to-summit-and-back volcanic mountain gran fondo that starts at Bali's coast, climbs relentlessly through the island's cultural and agricultural heartland to the rim of the Kintamani volcanic caldera, then descends back to the sea. The course is defined by one sustained ascent and one sustained descent, bookended by rolling coastal terrain. The climbing is not Alpine-steep but it is Alpine-long, and the tropical heat transforms moderate gradients into savage efforts. The Kintamani crater rim provides the emotional and physical summit — a view of Lake Danau Batur and Mount Batur that rewards every watt spent getting there. Signature challenge: The Kintamani volcano ascent. A 25-30 km sustained climb from the lowland rice terraces to the volcanic crater rim at 1,500 meters, performed in equatorial tropical heat. The gradient is steady (4-6% average, 8-12% pitches) but the length and conditions make it brutal. This is where the race breaks apart every year — the lead group of 15-20 fragments to 5-7 by the summit. The crater rim view of Lake Danau Batur is the payoff: one of the most dramatic summit finishes (mid-course) in world gran fondo cycling. GFNY Bali is the rare franchise event that transcends its brand. Strip away the GFNY timing chips and age-group classifications, and you still have one of the most visually spectacular road cycling courses on earth: a 134 km loop from a black-sand volcanic coast through UNESCO-worthy rice terraces and Hindu temple villages to the crater rim of an active volcano, then back down through monkey forests and the cultural heart of Ubud. The Kintamani ascent — 25-30 km of sustained climbing in equatorial heat to 1,500 meters — is a genuine test that combines European mountain-pass difficulty with Southeast Asian thermal punishment. The field is regionally competitive but internationally thin, and the franchise structure means the prestige caps below what this setting deserves. But no other GFNY event — and frankly, very few road cycling events anywhere — can match the combination of volcanic terrain, tropical ecology, and living spiritual culture that Bali delivers from the saddle. Yes if you want a world-class adventure cycling experience wrapped in a GFNY competitive framework. Yes if volcanic terrain, tropical conditions, and Balinese culture sound like the ride of a lifetime. Yes if you're based in the Asia-Pacific region and want a serious climbing gran fondo with professional organization. Skip if field depth matters to you — the competitive front is thin. Skip if you can't handle sustained climbing in equatorial heat. Skip if 20-30 hours of travel from the Americas is a dealbreaker. Non-negotiables: Tropical heat acclimatization protocol; Sustained climbing endurance at tempo in heat; Aggressive hydration and cooling strategy tested in heat

### Gran Fondo Il Lombardia
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 68 mi | 5,577 ft | Cantu, Como, Lombardy, Italy | October | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gran-fondo-il-lombardia/

The last Monument of the season — now yours to ride. Muro di Sormano at 27%. Madonna del Ghisallo. Lake Como in the falling leaves. A race of two legendary climbs bookended by the rolling Brianza hills. The route starts and finishes in Cantu, diving immediately into the undulating roads of the Brianza — the gentle, green foothills south of Lake Como that lull you into thinking this will be manageable. It is not. Within 30 km, the road tilts upward toward the Colma di Sormano, and then the gradient goes vertical: the Muro di Sormano, 1.9 km at 15.8% average with ramps touching 27%. After the Muro's savage descent, the route winds along Lake Como toward the second act: the Madonna del Ghisallo, 8.5 km of steady climbing to the church of cycling's patron saint. A sweeping 20 km descent to Merone gives temporary respite before the final 20 km of short, sharp climbs back to Cantu destroy whatever is left in your legs. The autumn colors, the lake views, the chapel at the Ghisallo summit — this course is as beautiful as it is brutal. Signature challenge: The Muro di Sormano. 1.9 kilometers at 27% maximum gradient. The road that was removed from the professional Monument for being too cruel. The road that was reinstated because cycling realized it needed places where the strongest riders separate from the merely strong. On the Gran Fondo, the Muro is where your training, your gearing, your body weight, and your willingness to suffer converge into a single, vertical question: can you ride this wall? There are five Monuments in professional cycling. Five races that define the sport's soul. Il Lombardia is the last of them — the autumn closer, the Classic of the Falling Leaves, the race that has crowned the season's strongest one-day rider since 1905. And now you can ride it. The Gran Fondo Il Lombardia puts you on the Muro di Sormano — 27% gradient, the road so brutal it was removed from the professional race for three decades. It sends you up the Madonna del Ghisallo, past the church of cycling's patron saint, with Lake Como spread out below like a postcard that became reality. It wraps you in the autumn colors that gave the race its name. RCS Sport — the people who run the Giro d'Italia — handle the organization. Felice Gimondi's name is on the event. And the day after you finish, you can stand on the same roads and watch Pogacar, van der Poel, and Evenepoel race the Monument itself. This is as close as amateur cycling gets to the sacred. Yes if you want to experience a cycling Monument from the inside — the same climbs, the same roads, the same autumn atmosphere that has defined professional cycling's season finale since 1905. Yes if the Muro di Sormano's 27% gradient is the kind of challenge that excites rather than terrifies you. Yes if UCI age-group competition and World Championship qualifying matter to your cycling ambitions. Skip if you want a pure gravel or off-road challenge. Skip if the October rain risk in northern Italy concerns you. Skip if you prefer large, festival-atmosphere events over the focused intensity of a competitive Italian gran fondo. Non-negotiables: Sustained steep climbing — repeated intervals at 12-20% gradient to simulate the Muro di Sormano; Back-to-back climb simulation — 2+ hours of continuous climbing with varied gradients; Descending confidence — practice technical descents on narrow roads at speed under fatigue

### BGY Airport Granfondo (Granfondo Felice Gimondi)
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 101 mi | 10,007 ft | Bergamo, Lombardy, Italy | May | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/granfondo-felice-gimondi/

30 years in the saddle of a legend. Six valleys. Selvino's 19 hairpins. Bergamo's cycling soul, named for the man who won all three Grand Tours. Six Bergamasque valleys connected by sustained climbs, starting and finishing in the cultural city of Bergamo. The climbs are not short punchy walls — they are genuine valley ascents of 7-11 km at 5-7% average gradients, with steeper pitches up to 12%. Selvino's 19 hairpins are the signature feature: nearly 11 km of climbing through switchbacks named after local cycling legends, reaching 946m above sea level. Between climbs, the valleys offer rolling terrain through stone villages, river gorges, and alpine meadows. The rhythm is valley-floor recovery, gradual approach, sustained climb, hairpin descent, repeat. The long route does this six times across 148 km. Signature challenge: Selvino's 19 hairpins — 10.9 km of sustained climbing through switchbacks named after Bergamasque cycling legends. It is not the steepest climb on the route (Colle Gallo has steeper pitches), but it is the most sustained and the most iconic. The 19 bends create a hypnotic rhythm that either carries you upward or slowly grinds you down. On the long route, Selvino comes early enough to be manageable but late enough that the remaining four climbs are ridden on Selvino-depleted legs. The Granfondo Felice Gimondi is what happens when a cycling-obsessed city names its signature ride after its greatest son. Felice Gimondi — born in Sedrina, 10 km from Bergamo — won the Tour de France, the Giro d'Italia three times, and the Vuelta a Espana. One of only seven riders to complete the Grand Tour triple crown. The event bearing his name threads through six Bergamasque valleys, climbs 19 hairpins up to Selvino where each bend carries a local cycling legend's name, passes the Sanctuary of Our Lady of Cyclists at Colle Gallo, and returns to a medieval city that breathes cycling the way other cities breathe football. Thirty years of continuous operation. Bianchi co-branding. A EUR45 entry fee that feels like a gift. This is not the most famous gran fondo in Italy — it is perhaps the most authentically Italian. Yes if you want an authentically Italian gran fondo in cycling's heartland — great value, deep heritage, and honest climbing through beautiful pre-Alpine valleys. Yes if Gimondi's name means something to you. Yes if EUR45 for a well-organized FCI event with a Santini jersey option sounds like a bargain. Skip if you want Alpine drama at 2,500m — Selvino tops out under 1,000m. Skip if you need a massive international field and English-language everything — this event is proudly Italian first. Non-negotiables: Sustained climbing endurance — repeated 7-11 km efforts at 5-7% gradient; Multi-climb day simulation — 4+ distinct climbs in a single training ride; Hairpin descending confidence — technical descents from 900m+ through switchbacks

### Gravel Burn
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 62 mi | 8,202 ft | Mpumalanga, South Africa | October | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gravel-burn/

A stage race trying to be Unbound with safari views, but the Karoo heat and seven-day commitment separate the curious from the committed. 100 km, 2,500 m. 85% gravel, 15% dirt. South African highveld—rolling red dirt roads. Technical rating: 3/5. Signature challenge: South African highveld rolling terrain + October heat. Red dirt roads. Gravel Burn is South African highveld classic—100 km, 2,500 m elevation. Founded ~2018. 85% gravel, 15% dirt. Red dirt roads (iconic South African). Rolling highveld terrain. 500-700 riders. Mpumalanga scenic region. October spring conditions (15-30°C). Affordable entry (R1,500-R2,000 / $80-$110). If you want African gravel adventure with stunning scenery, this is it. If you want local/domestic race, skip it. International destination race. If you've never done a multi-day gravel event, this isn't where you start—the Karoo semi-desert doesn't forgive poor pacing or inadequate hydration systems. You need solid base fitness for 100km+ daily efforts and the mental fortitude for a week of accumulating fatigue. The full-service camps cushion the suffering, but those long drags to places like Conical Peak will expose fitness gaps quickly.

### Grinduro California
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 60 mi | 6,500 ft | Quincy, California (Plumas County) | September | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/grinduro-california/

A party disguised as a race where the descents are timed, the climbs are social, and your bike handling matters more than your FTP. ~60 miles, ~6,500 ft. Enduro format: 4-5 timed descents + untimed climbs. 50% singletrack, 30% gravel, 20% dirt. Technical rating: 4/5. MTB skills required. Signature challenge: Enduro format—timed descents reward MTB skills. Untimed climbs = social. Party vibe. Grinduro California is party gravel with timed segments. Founded 2015—OG location that spawned global Grinduro series (Germany, France, Scotland, Japan). Enduro format: 4-5 timed descents (compete) + untimed climbs (social). 50% singletrack, MTB skills required. 400-600 riders. Quincy small Sierra Nevada town. Legendary post-race party—beer, music, camping. Vibe: 60% party, 40% race. If you want serious racing, skip it. If you want fun event with technical terrain and epic party, this is bucket-list. Come if you want good vibes and can handle singletrack on drop bars—this isn't pure gravel racing. You need actual mountain bike skills for the technical descents and 40-45mm tires that grip. Skip it if you're chasing serious race results or can't ride technical terrain without white knuckling.

### Maraton Franja BTC City
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 97 mi | 9,258 ft | Ljubljana, Slovenia | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/maraton-franja/

Europe's oldest gran fondo. 43 editions since 1982. UCI World Series founding member. EUR 44 entry. The Slovenian institution that proves longevity beats hype. A 156 km loop through the Ljubljana Basin and Julian Alps foothills. The route leaves Ljubljana, climbs gently over the Vrhnika Hill, traverses the Logatec Plateau, descends through the historic mercury-mining town of Idrija, then tackles the defining Kladje climb — 7 km at 6.3% average with a 10% maximum pitch. After a long, rough descent through alpine forest, the route passes through medieval Skofja Loka before rolling back to Ljubljana across pastoral terrain. The course is diverse: urban start, rolling plateau, alpine forest climbs, historic towns, and flat agricultural finish. Maximum elevation is 801m — this is a foothills event, not an Alpine epic. Signature challenge: The Kladje climb: 7 km at 6.3% average with a maximum gradient of 10%. It arrives at approximately km 90 after the plateau crossings and the Idrija valley, on legs that have already absorbed 60+ km of rolling terrain. The climb itself is honest — no false flats, no respite sections. But the real test comes immediately after: a 10 km descent on rough road surface that demands concentration when you most want to coast. Maraton Franja is not the hardest gran fondo, not the most scenic, not the most famous. What it is, without qualification, is one of the most genuine. Running since 1982 — before 'gran fondo' was even a recognized category — this Slovenian institution has quietly built a 43-year legacy of accessible, well-organized road cycling through the Ljubljana Basin and Julian Alps foothills. Named after a secret WWII partisan hospital hidden in a mountain gorge, the event carries historical weight that no Italian monument or French classic can replicate. The Kladje climb will test you. The descent will scare you. The finish in Ljubljana will satisfy you. And the EUR 44 entry fee will make you wonder why everyone else charges three times as much. Yes if you want a well-organized UCI gran fondo qualifier at a fraction of the cost of Italian or French alternatives. Yes if the historical connection to a WWII partisan hospital resonates. Yes if you want to discover Slovenia — an underrated European cycling destination. Skip if you need Alpine drama — the maximum elevation is 801m. Skip if you need a globally recognized name on your cycling resume. Non-negotiables: Sustained climbing endurance — multiple 20-40 minute climbs in a single long ride; Technical descending confidence on imperfect surfaces; Full race simulation — 140+ km with 2,500m+ climbing and nutrition plan

### Paris to Ancaster
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 62 mi | 4,000 ft | Paris to Ancaster, Ontario, Canada | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/paris-to-ancaster/

Canada's spring classic—where mud is the main competitor and surviving is winning. A multi-surface chaos ride through Ontario farmland. Fast flat rail trail sections contrast with hike-a-bike mud chutes. The racing is European spring classic style—surge for position before the bottleneck sectors, survive the mud, then sprint for the final climb. Point-to-point from Paris to Ancaster. Signature challenge: The mud chutes—deep clay sectors that turn racing into survival and break equipment and spirits. Paris to Ancaster is Canada's answer to Paris-Roubaix—a spring classic where mud is the defining variable. It's not the longest or hardest on paper, but the combination of cold, mud, and mechanical carnage makes it uniquely brutal. Founded in 1994, it predates the modern 'gravel' movement. If you want to test yourself in conditions that break bikes and spirits, P2A delivers. Yes if you want Canada's flagship gravel event and can handle mud chaos. Yes if you like spring classics vibes. Skip if you hate cold/wet or can't accept potential mechanical disaster.

### Peaks Challenge Falls Creek
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 146 mi | 13,123 ft | Falls Creek, Victoria, Australia | March | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/peaks-challenge-falls-creek/

Australia's definitive mountain challenge. Three alpine summits. 235 km. 4,000 meters of climbing in a single day. A linear epic across the Victorian Alps. The three-peak structure gives the ride a natural narrative: conquer Hotham, survive the valley heat, endure Tawonga, and somehow find the legs for Falls Creek at the end. The terrain alternates between long sustained climbs to cool alpine summits and hot, exposed valley roads where the sun beats down relentlessly. There is almost no flat ground — you are either climbing, descending, or wishing you were descending. Signature challenge: The cumulative weight of 235 km and 4,000m. No single climb is the hardest you'll ever ride, but stacking three alpine summits in a single day — with valley heat between them — creates an event where the whole is devastatingly greater than the sum of its parts. Peaks Challenge Falls Creek is Australia's answer to the great European mountain cyclosportives, and it holds its own. The format is brutally simple: climb three alpine summits, cover 235 km, gain 4,000 meters. The Victorian High Country provides the backdrop — eucalyptus forests, alpine meadows, panoramic ridgeline views — but the real story is the sheer physical and mental challenge of the day. Mount Hotham's 30 km climb is the physical crux. The final ascent to Falls Creek at kilometer 220, on legs that have been working for 10+ hours, is the emotional one. If you finish, you know exactly what you're made of. Yes if you want a genuine ultra-distance mountain cycling challenge outside Europe. Yes if completing Australia's most respected single-day cycling event is on your list. Skip if you can't commit to training for 10-14 hours in the saddle. Skip if the 4.5-hour drive from Melbourne (or long-haul flight from overseas) is a dealbreaker. Non-negotiables: Multiple rides of 180+ km with 3,000+ meters of climbing; Heat acclimatization and hydration strategy testing; Full dress rehearsal — 200+ km day with 3,000m+ climbing and nutrition plan

### Rad am Ring
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 93 mi | 11,024 ft | Nurburgring, Nurburg, Rhineland-Palatinate, Germany | July | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/rad-am-ring/

Race the Green Hell. 12,000 cyclists on the world's most famous motorsport circuit — the Nurburgring Nordschleife. A relentless, undulating closed circuit combining the legendary Nordschleife (20.8 km) with the modern Grand Prix circuit (5.1 km). No flat sections exist — you are always either climbing or descending through 84 turns per lap. The forest-enclosed Nordschleife feels otherworldly, while the GP circuit provides the F1 arena start/finish spectacle. Perfect tarmac throughout, but the elevation profile punishes anyone who starts too fast. Signature challenge: The Hohe Acht climb at 17% gradient — the roof of the Nordschleife where every lap extracts its toll. Combined with the relentless undulation of the full circuit (560m elevation per lap), there is no recovery. The Green Hell simply grinds you down. Rad am Ring does something no other cycling event can: it puts you on the Nurburgring Nordschleife. The same 20.8 km of relentless, forest-enclosed circuit where motorsport legends were forged since 1927. Where Rudi Altig won the Road World Championship in 1966. Where Jackie Stewart coined the name 'Green Hell.' Nearly 12,000 cyclists now invade this sacred motorsport ground every July, racing through 84 turns per lap, surviving the 17% Hohe Acht climb, and hitting 100 km/h on the Fuchsrohre descent. The 24h race adds a surreal dimension — riding the Nordschleife at 3 AM through fog with only a headlamp. This is not gravel, this is not a gran fondo — this is road cycling on the most storied racing circuit on Earth, and the experience is absolutely singular. If you have any connection to motorsport culture, this is a mandatory pilgrimage on two wheels. The 24h race is the crown jewel — 24 hours of closed-circuit racing through the Nordschleife, including surreal night laps through forest fog. The public races (25/75/150 km) offer the venue experience in a single day. For German and European cyclists, this is one of the continent's most unique events. For international visitors, combine with a broader European cycling trip. Non-negotiables: Repeated climbing endurance; High-speed descending confidence; Lap pacing strategy

### Rule of Three
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 300 mi | 15,000 ft | Emporia, Kansas (same roads as Unbound Gravel) | September | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/rule-of-three/

Three hundred miles on Unbound roads without the Unbound circus—just you, Kansas, and the kind of suffering that builds character. 300 miles, 15,000 ft. 95% gravel, 5% pavement. Kansas Flint Hills—same roads as Unbound. Ultra distance (15-25 hours). Technical rating: 2/5. Signature challenge: 300 miles ultra distance. Flint Hills rollers never stop. 15-25 hours. 30-40% DNF. Rule of Three is 300 miles on Unbound roads. Same Kansas Flint Hills rollers, but 3x the distance. Founded ~2018. Ultra distance (15-25 hours). Self-supported (aid stations but no SAG). ~100 riders. 30-40% DNF. Early September late summer heat (60-85°F). If you want ultra distance suffering on Unbound roads, this is it. If you want manageable distance or beginner-friendly, skip it. 300 miles is brutal. Mental game critical. This is for riders who want the full Unbound experience without paying Unbound prices or fighting Unbound crowds. You need legitimate endurance fitness—300 miles doesn't negotiate, and the self-supported nature means your mistakes compound quickly. Come prepared for mental warfare disguised as a bike race.

### Les Cinglés du Mont Ventoux
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 85 mi | 14,436 ft | Bédoin, Vaucluse, Provence, France | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/cingles-du-mont-ventoux/

Three faces. One mountain. 4,400 meters of climbing. The most honest challenge in cycling — EUR 20 and a brevet card against the Beast of Provence. Three ascents of the same mountain from three different directions, linked by rolling Provençal country roads through lavender fields and vineyards. Each climb has a distinct personality: Bédoin is the classic — dense forest giving way to exposed moonscape, the route the Tour de France made famous. Malaucène is the underestimated equal — steep early ramps, varied terrain, fewer cyclists. Sault is the long, deceptive approach — gentle gradients through lavender plateau that lull you before the final shared summit cone brutalizes you. All three routes converge in the last 6 km, where the trees vanish and the white limestone begins — a barren, lunar landscape that looks like no other place in cycling. The observatory tower at 1,912m is visible from kilometers away, growing imperceptibly closer with each pedal stroke. You pass the Tom Simpson memorial three times. You stand at the summit three times. And three times, you descend. Signature challenge: The third summit. The first ascent is excitement — the mountain is fresh, the legs are willing, the scenery is extraordinary. The second ascent is determination — the mountain is familiar, the legs are heavy, the summit seems farther away. The third ascent is reckoning. Your body has already climbed 3,150 meters. The Sault approach's gentle gradients offer false hope. The final 6 km of exposed limestone — the same white moonscape you have already climbed twice — must be climbed one more time. The observatory tower is visible. The Tom Simpson memorial is ahead. The wind is blowing. The third summit is where the Cinglés challenge becomes exactly what its name promises: crazy. Les Cinglés du Mont Ventoux is not a race. It is not a sportive. It is not an organized event. It is a personal reckoning with the most revered mountain in cycling, repeated until either you or the mountain blinks. Three ascents — Bédoin's relentless forest grind, Malaucène's savage opening ramps, Sault's deceptively gentle approach — each delivering you to the same barren, windswept summit at 1,912m where an observatory tower stands like a lighthouse above the clouds and Tom Simpson's memorial reminds you that this mountain does not care about your suffering. The genius of the Cinglés is in its simplicity: EUR 20, a brevet card, three stamps, three summits, midnight to midnight. No marshals. No aid stations. No chip timing. Just you, the mountain, and 4,400 meters of vertical truth. The Club des Cinglés has existed since 1988 because Christian Pic understood something fundamental: some challenges do not need organization. They need audacity. The word 'cinglé' means 'crazy' in French, and by the third ascent — when your legs remember the first two and the summit looks impossibly far and the Mistral is shredding your jersey — you understand that it is not a name. It is a diagnosis. Yes if you want to experience the most complete day possible on a single mountain — three ascents, three landscapes, one barren summit that never gets less shocking. Yes if you value self-reliance and the purity of a challenge that has no finish-line arch, no announcer, no medal ceremony — just a stamped card and the knowledge of what you did. Yes if Mont Ventoux means something to you. Skip if you need organized event support, medical coverage, and feed stations. Skip if Provençal summer heat is a deal-breaker. Skip if you want to race against other people rather than against yourself. Non-negotiables: Sustained climbing endurance — 4+ hours of continuous climbing in a single session; Heat acclimatization and hydration strategy tested in training; Full reconnaissance of all three routes — or detailed route plan with GPS

### Collegiate Gravel Nationals
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 62 mi | 4,921 ft | Turkey, Texas | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/collegiate-gravel-nationals/

The inaugural collegiate nationals that trades established prestige for first-year chaos in the Texas panhandle. 100 km, 1,500 m. 80% gravel, 20% dirt. Texas Panhandle—rolling plains, red dirt. Technical rating: 2/5. Signature challenge: 100 km + Texas Panhandle wind + rolling plains. National Championship competitive field. Collegiate Gravel Nationals is USA Cycling National Championship—100 km, 1,500 m elevation. Founded ~2019. 80% gravel, 20% dirt. Texas Panhandle—rolling plains, red dirt. 200-400 collegiate riders. Turkey, TX (remote). April spring conditions (15-25°C, wind). Affordable entry ($50-$75). If you're collegiate and want National Championship, this is it. If you're not collegiate, you can't race. National Championship prestige. If you're college-aged with decent fitness and want to claim bragging rights as the first-ever winner, this is your shot. The 62-mile mix of gravel roads, rail trail, and singletrack won't destroy you, but March in Texas means unpredictable weather and zero race history to guide your prep. Show up ready for anything because nobody knows what they're doing yet.

### GFNY New York
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 100 mi | 8,500 ft | Fort Lee, New Jersey / Hudson Valley, New York, USA | May | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gfny-new-york/

100 miles from the George Washington Bridge to Bear Mountain and back. The world championship of gran fondos, started by two people who paid $50K to close the busiest bridge in America. An out-and-back loop that starts with one of cycling's most dramatic send-offs — 5,000 riders rolling across the closed George Washington Bridge — and then heads north through the Hudson Valley's mix of river flats, suburban rollers, and genuine mountain terrain. The first half builds toward Bear Mountain, the course's physical and emotional apex. The second half punishes you with the Rockland County ridgeline — a relentless sequence of sharp climbs and technical descents that hit when your legs are already compromised. The final miles back along 9W are a flat-road time trial to the finish in Fort Lee. The course rewards riders who can climb, recover on descents, and still produce power in the final 30 miles. Signature challenge: Bear Mountain gets the name recognition, but the Rockland County ridgeline is the real race-breaker. Gate Hill and Cheesecote hit when you're 55-65 miles deep with 5,000+ feet already in the legs. The 18% maximum on Cheesecote stops riders who are already cooked. The course is designed to punish poor pacing — it's back-loaded by design. GFNY New York is the race that proved American gran fondos could be taken seriously. When Uli and Lidia Fluhme paid the Port Authority $50,000 to close the George Washington Bridge for a cycling event in 2011, they were making a bet that the US market wanted European-style competitive cycling with real timing, real anti-doping, and real racing — not another charity ride with a fruit cup at the finish. Fifteen years and 30+ global editions later, that bet has paid off spectacularly. The NYC World Championship draws 5,000 riders from 90+ countries to race 100 miles through the Hudson Valley, summit Bear Mountain, and grind through the Rockland County ridgeline. The course is demanding without being mythical. The organization is professional without being corporate. And that start on the closed GWB — with the Manhattan skyline receding behind you as 5,000 riders roll out together — remains one of the most visually stunning moments in amateur cycling. Yes if you want the most competitive, most international gran fondo in North America. Yes if starting on a closed George Washington Bridge with the NYC skyline behind you is on your bucket list. Yes if you want anti-doping, real timing, and a world championship classification. Skip if you want Alpine grandeur — this is the Hudson Valley, not the Dolomites. Skip if NYC-area costs are a dealbreaker. Non-negotiables: Back-to-back climbing endurance over 5+ hours with repeated efforts; Pacing discipline for a back-loaded course; Tested nutrition strategy for 5-8 hours of variable-intensity riding

### Gran Fondo Alberto Contador
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 91 mi | 8,327 ft | Oliva, Valencia, Spain | September | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/granfondo-alberto-contador/

Ride with a legend. 146 km through the Costa Blanca mountains, alongside the man who won the Tour, the Giro, and the Vuelta — and still shows up to pedal with the field. A circular route from the Mediterranean coast into the limestone mountain passes of the Costa Blanca interior and back. The first kilometers are flat — the Valencian coastal plain around Oliva is rice-paddy territory, sea-level warm-up before the mountains begin. Then the road tilts upward and does not truly relent for 80 km. The climbing is concentrated through the Marina Alta and Vall de Gallinera: a series of 5-8 km passes at 5-6% average gradient, connected by rolling valley roads through ancient stone villages. Coll de Rates is the signature — the most famous cycling climb on the Costa Blanca, a timed section that separates those who paced wisely from those who did not. Sa Creueta is the high point, with its sharp final kilometer and panoramic ridge views. The Vall d'Ebo descent through bare limestone landscape feels like riding through a geology textbook. The return to Oliva via Pego is mercifully flat, a Mediterranean homecoming with the salt air announcing the finish before you see it. Signature challenge: Coll de Rates — the timed section that defines the competitive day. 6.6 km of consistent climbing at 5.3% average, arriving early enough in the route (km 30) to tempt riders into going too hard. The climb itself is not the hardest on the route — Sa Creueta is steeper, Vall d'Ebo is longer — but the timed section creates a race-within-a-race dynamic. Ride it as a time trial and pay later. Ride it as a tempo effort and carry strength through the remaining 116 km. Every year, Coll de Rates sorts the field into those who raced the climb and those who raced the event. The Gran Fondo Alberto Contador is the rare event where the name on the marquee actually shows up and rides with you. Alberto Contador — two-time Tour de France winner, two-time Giro champion, three-time Vuelta conqueror, one of the seven members of the Grand Tour triple crown club and arguably the most devastating pure attacker of his generation — does not just lend his name to this event. He starts at the front, drops back to wait for the last rider, and works his way through the entire peloton, greeting cyclists, posing for photos, and probably putting in a few accelerations that remind everyone within visual range exactly why he won seven Grand Tours. The route through the Costa Blanca's interior mountains — Coll de Rates, Sa Creueta, the Vall de Gallinera — is a concentrated dose of Mediterranean climbing at its finest. The Fundacion Alberto Contador channels the event's proceeds into cycling development, stroke awareness, and providing bikes to disadvantaged communities. This is not a vanity project. It is a UCI-sanctioned qualifier with real competitive structure, ridden through genuinely beautiful mountains, organized by professionals, and anchored by a champion who clearly still loves the bike. Yes if you want the once-in-a-lifetime experience of riding alongside Alberto Contador through Mediterranean mountain passes. Yes if a UCI qualifier with timed climbing sections suits your competitive instincts. Yes if the Costa Blanca's combination of mountain cycling and beach recovery sounds like the perfect September weekend. Skip if you want pure mountain extremity — the passes top out under 800m. Skip if you need a massive international field and English-language everything — this event is proudly Spanish first. Skip if September Mediterranean weather risk gives you anxiety. Non-negotiables: Heat-adapted sustained climbing — repeated 6-8 km efforts at 5-6% in warm conditions; Pacing discipline on timed climbs — the ability to climb at 85% when 100% is tempting; Multi-pass endurance in heat — 100+ km training rides with 1,500m+ climbing in warm weather

### Granfondo Eddy Merckx
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 101 mi | 8,674 ft | Andenne, Wallonia, Belgium | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/granfondo-eddy-merckx/

The greatest cyclist's namesake gran fondo. 162 km, 18 climbs, 2,644m of Walloon Ardennes punishment. The Cannibal's roads. A race of 18 successive walls through the Walloon Ardennes, bookended by the Meuse river valley at Andenne. The route begins with an immediate climb — the Cote de Peu d'Eau, 2.7 km of La Fleche Wallonne terrain that sets the tone from the gun. There is no warmup. From there, the course winds through the Condroz plateau — the rolling upland between the Meuse and Ourthe valleys — hitting classified climb after classified climb in a rhythm that is uniquely Belgian: short, steep, partial recovery, repeat. The signature moment is the Triple Mur de Monty — 1.9 km with three successive ramps hitting 20-24%, each one arriving just as you think the worst is over. Between the named climbs, the terrain never flattens. The Condroz is a landscape of gentle ridges and hidden valleys, and every false flat tilts upward. The descents are fast but technical — narrow communal roads through forest, hedgerows tight against the tarmac, the occasional farm vehicle adding authenticity. The final approach back to Andenne delivers panoramic views over the Meuse valley, a finishing reward for six-plus hours of Ardennes punishment. On a clear June day, the green Walloon countryside — stone farmhouses, church spires, cows in fields that slope toward hidden streams — is hauntingly beautiful. On a wet one, it is Belgium. Signature challenge: The Triple Mur de Monty. Three successive walls hitting 20-24% gradient over 1.9 km. A sign at the steepest point reads 23.9%. Tougher than the Mur de Huy that decides La Fleche Wallonne. On the Granfondo Eddy Merckx, the Triple Mur is where you discover whether your training, your gearing, your body weight, and your willingness to suffer are sufficient for Belgian hills. The Cannibal would have attacked on the first ramp and been over the top before most riders reached the second. You are not The Cannibal. There is one name in cycling that sits above all others. Not Coppi. Not Anquetil. Not Hinault. Eddy Merckx — The Cannibal — won 525 races, 11 Grand Tours, all five Monuments at least twice, three World Championships, and the Hour Record. He was so dominant that rivals stopped trying to win and raced for second. And this gran fondo bears his name. The Granfondo Eddy Merckx sends you out of Andenne on the Cote de Peu d'Eau — La Fleche Wallonne terrain — and then hammers you across 162 km of Walloon Ardennes hills. Eighteen classified climbs. The Triple Mur de Monty with its three successive 20%+ ramps. 2,644 meters of climbing that never lets you recover. This is the terrain that forged Belgian cycling's greatest generation. Golazo runs it with typical Belgian efficiency. The UCI sanctions it for World Championship qualifying. And at EUR 44, it costs less than dinner in Brussels. Yes if you want to ride in the name of the greatest cyclist who ever lived — through the Ardennes terrain that forged Belgian cycling's dominance. Yes if 18 climbs in 162 km excites rather than terrifies you. Yes if UCI age-group competition and World Championship qualifying matter to your ambitions. Yes if Belgium's cycling culture — the deepest in the world — is something you want to experience from the inside. Skip if you want warm weather guarantees. Skip if you prefer large festival-atmosphere events over focused European gran fondo racing. Skip if the relentless, puncheur-style climbing of the Ardennes is not your terrain. Non-negotiables: Repeated steep climb intervals — back-to-back efforts at 15-20%+ gradient to simulate the Triple Mur de Monty and Belgian walls; Sustained climbing endurance — 4+ hours of riding with continuous climb repetitions; Puncheur power development — explosive efforts at VO2max followed by immediate recovery and repeat

### Highlands Gravel Classic
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 67 mi | 4,850 ft | Goshen, Arkansas | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/highlands-gravel-classic/

The only US path to UCI Gravel Worlds—67 miles of Ozark smashfest. A concentrated smashfest through Ozark gravel. Tree canopy covers much of the course, providing shade but limiting descent visibility. The gravel is chunky and sharp—not smooth hardpack. Over a dozen climbs in 67 miles means you're either going up or bombing down. No recovery. Signature challenge: The relentless rollers on chunky sharp gravel—death by a thousand cuts with technical descents mixed in. Highlands Gravel Classic is the only US path to UCI Gravel World Championships—that alone makes it significant. The race itself is a 67-mile smashfest through chunky Ozark gravel with constant climbs and technical descents. It's short enough to race hard and long enough to hurt. For anyone chasing UCI qualification, this is mandatory. Yes if you're chasing UCI qualification. Yes if you want a short, intense race on technical terrain. Skip if you want ultra-endurance or smooth gravel.

### Granfondo Sestriere — Colle delle Finestre
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 64 mi | 10,400 ft | Cesana Torinese, Piedmont, Italy | July | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/marmotte-granfondo-sestriere/

The only granfondo in the world with Colle delle Finestre's legendary unpaved summit. Giro d'Italia dirt at 2,178 meters. A focused Alpine assault centered on one defining climb. The route through the Susa and Chisone Valleys is scenic and demanding, but everyone is here for the same reason: Colle delle Finestre. The course builds through steady valley climbs and fast descents before delivering you to the base of the Finestre's 18.5 km ascent. The first 10 km on tarmac are relentless (9.2% average), but the real event begins when the road surface changes. The final 7.8 km on dirt — switchbacking through larch forests with gradients reaching 14% — is the granfondo's entire reason for existence. The descent and return climb to Sestriere at 2,035m ensures the legs never fully recover. Signature challenge: The 7.8 km of unpaved road on Colle delle Finestre. The transition from tarmac to dirt at approximately 1,700m altitude — on gradients that reach 14%, at oxygen levels measurably reduced by altitude — is the single most distinctive feature of any road granfondo in the world. You can train for the climbing. You cannot fully prepare for the psychological impact of losing your road surface when the gradient kicks hardest. The Granfondo Sestriere exists for one reason: Colle delle Finestre. And that one reason is enough. This is the only organized cycling event in the world that sends you up the 7.8 km of unpaved road that has produced some of the most dramatic Giro d'Italia stages in modern history. The event itself is young and still finding its identity after splitting from the Marmotte brand, but the mountain does not care about branding. When the tarmac ends and the gravel begins at 1,700m, you are riding the exact surface that broke champions and made legends. Everything else — the Susa Valley, the Olympic infrastructure, the finish at Sestriere — is context for that singular experience. Yes if you want to ride cycling's most famous unpaved climb in an organized event. Yes if you value Giro d'Italia heritage and want to feel what the professionals felt on that dirt. Yes if you want a high-value Italian Alpine experience without the logistics of deep-Alpine events. Skip if you need a large, prestigious international granfondo. Skip if you want a 150+ km all-day epic — at 103 km, this is more focused than sprawling. Non-negotiables: Sustained climbing endurance — 2-3 hour continuous climbing efforts in training; Road bike setup tested on rough surfaces — tire choice and pressure dialed; Full dress rehearsal — 3+ hours of climbing with nutrition and descending strategy

### Rock Cobbler
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 100 mi | 5,000 ft | Bakersfield, California | February | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/rock-cobbler/

The gravel scene's greatest shitshow — equal parts bike race, agricultural obstacle course, and fever dream cooked up by someone who thinks cyclocross isn't weird enough. 100 miles of Central Valley gravel and dirt. 5,000 ft elevation. 60% gravel, 30% dirt, 10% pavement. Chunky rocky sections. Technical rating: 3/5. Signature challenge: February weather roulette. Chunky rocks. Early season fitness test. Rock Cobbler is California's early season gravel classic—100 miles of Central Valley rocks, dirt, and Havilah climb. Founded by Stephen Fry in 2012. 600+ riders. February timing means weather roulette: 2023 was rain/mud (25%+ DNF), 2024 was perfect. Bakersfield is affordable ($80-120 lodging). Chunky rocky sections reward technical skills. Peter Stetina & Lauren De Crescenzo won 2024. If you're California-based and want early season test, it's great value ($80-$120). If you're flying in, weather risk is real. If you need your races predictable and your routes announced more than a week out, keep scrolling. This is for riders who want their fitness tested by 30% run-ups, cattle dodging, and whatever Sam Ames dreamed up the night before. Your FTP matters less than your ability to laugh while hike-a-biking through a ball pit. Show up undertrained and the scree fields will humble you in ways Strava can't measure.

### The Gralloch
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 124 mi | 11,483 ft | Scottish Highlands, Scotland | September | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/the-gralloch/

Scotland's gravel crown jewel serves up 1,700 meters of climbing through Galloway's forests with a side of Highland hospitality. 200 km, 3,500 m. 75% gravel, 25% singletrack/dirt. Scottish Highlands—remote technical terrain. Technical rating: 4/5. Signature challenge: 200 km ultra + Scottish weather (40% rain, cold) + remote Highlands. 25-35% DNF. The Gralloch is Scottish Highlands ultra—200 km, 3,500 m elevation. Founded ~2017. 75% gravel, 25% singletrack/dirt. Remote Highlands terrain, technical sections. 200-300 riders. September autumn conditions (8-18°C, 40% rain). 25-35% DNF. Ultra distance (12-18 hours). Self-sufficient required. If you want Scottish ultra brutality with remote Highlands scenery, this is it. If you want manageable distance or good weather, skip it. Brutal but unique. You need solid climbing legs and comfort on technical gravel—that opening 11km ascent eliminates pretenders fast. The 4:15 PM cutoff at 67km isn't generous, so show up fit or get shuttled to the shortened route. Perfect for riders who want UCI World Series prestige without the chaos of American mega-events.

### Tour de Big Bear
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 100 mi | 8,500 ft | Big Bear Lake, California, USA | August | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/tour-de-big-bear/

100 miles at 7,000 feet with legendary aid stations that serve ribs, have DJs, and make you forget you are dying on a bike. A deceptive course that lulls you with rolling lakeside roads for 50 miles before delivering the altitude hammer in the second half. The Onyx Summit climb at 8,443 feet is the crown jewel, and the Cedar Falls descent-and-return is where tired legs at altitude learn about humility. Signature challenge: Onyx Summit at 8,443 feet. The highest paved road in Southern California, reached after 50+ miles of riding at altitude. The KOM/QOM competition makes the climb honest. Then you descend 2,000 feet to Cedar Falls and must climb back up. The Tour de Big Bear is what happens when a Southern California cycling community builds their dream ride: 100 miles at 7,000 feet with 8,500 feet of climbing, legendary aid stations that serve BBQ ribs and have DJ booths, and a mountain lake setting that delivers 55-65F start temperatures while the rest of SoCal melts in August.

The climbing is real. Onyx Summit at 8,443 feet is the highest paved road in Southern California, and the KOM competition up its slopes draws genuine efforts. The profile is back-loaded and punishing: rolling lakeside roads lull you for 50 miles, then the Onyx push and Cedar Falls loop deliver the hurt. The 12:30 PM cutoff at Blue Sky Meadows is there for a reason -- undertrained riders bonk at altitude.

But the aid stations are what make this event legendary. Full buffets, DJs, couches, ribs, live music. The volunteers are locals who have been doing this for 15 years and take genuine pride in their hospitality. Named the #1 Gran Fondo in North America by Gran Fondo Guide in 2023. If you are a SoCal cyclist, this is your annual pilgrimage. Yes if you want the definitive SoCal mountain cycling experience with altitude, climbing, and aid stations that redefine hospitality. Skip if you cannot handle 7,000+ foot altitude, need a sanctioned race, or live far from California. Non-negotiables: Altitude acclimatization strategy; Century-distance climbing fitness; Heat management for Cedar Falls descent

### BWR Arizona
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 104 mi | 6,600 ft | Cave Creek / McDowell Region, Arizona | February | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/bwr-arizona/

Technical desert suffering through sand, rock, and singletrack—a true BWR experience in Sonoran landscapes. A desert gravel experience that emphasizes technical unroad over pure climbing. Sand washes, rocky singletrack, and punchy desert climbs through Sonoran landscapes. The BWR formula adapted to high-desert terrain. Signature challenge: The combination of sand, rock, and technical singletrack in desert conditions—'more unroad' means the terrain difficulty compensates for lower elevation gain. BWR Arizona delivers the BWR suffering formula in a desert package—technical singletrack, sand, rocks, and punchy climbs through Sonoran landscapes. It's less climby than California but compensates with unroad technical demands. Not an major series race, but attracts that caliber of competition. The 'lowest vert BWR' is still a beast. Yes if you want technical gravel racing with genuine unroad terrain. Yes if you're chasing BWR cred without major series pressure. Skip if you expect smooth gravel roads or hate sand and rocks.

### Cyclassics Hamburg
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 68 mi | 1,150 ft | Hamburg, Germany | August | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/cyclassics-hamburg/

Free entry. 22,000 riders. Closed roads through Germany's second city. The Waseberg at 16%. Europe's biggest cycling party — not its hardest ride. The Cyclassics Hamburg is not a race that tests your body. It tests your appreciation for what cycling events can be. Twenty-two thousand riders on closed roads through a major European city, alongside a UCI WorldTour professional race, with free registration — that combination exists nowhere else in the world. The physical challenge is minimal by any serious cyclist's standard: 109 km of flat roads with one 700-meter climb on the Waseberg, which is over before your heart rate settles into zone 5. You will not suffer here. You will not dig deep into reserves you did not know you had. What you will do is experience cycling as a mass cultural event — a city that shuts its streets for bikes, spectators lining the Waseberg and Mönckebergstraße, the energy of twenty thousand riders rolling through Hamburg's best scenery.

The prestige comes from the pro race connection. Since 1996, the Cyclassics has been Germany's flagship professional road race — the country's only event at cycling's highest level since the Deutschland Tour disappeared in 2009. Elia Viviani, Andre Greipel, Tyler Farrar, Peter Sagan, Alexander Kristoff — the sport's greatest sprinters have won on these roads. Riding the same tarmac, on the same day, on closed roads, with the same finish line on Mönckebergstraße: that means something. The Waseberg climb in Blankenese — 700 meters at gradients touching 16%, rising sharply from the Elbe's north bank — is the one moment where the amateur and professional experiences converge in physical effort. The rest is about the atmosphere, the city, and the sheer improbability of free entry to an event of this caliber.

If you want a cycling challenge, go elsewhere. If you want to experience European cycling culture at its most accessible and democratic — the biggest party on two wheels, in one of Europe's great cities, for zero euros — Hamburg in August is the answer. Yes if you want to experience the scale and energy of one of Europe's largest mass-participation cycling events. Yes if the WorldTour pro race connection matters to you. Yes if free registration and world-class ASO organization sound too good to pass up. Yes if a cycling festival weekend in Hamburg appeals. Skip if you need climbing, technical terrain, or a genuine physical test — this is flat and short. Skip if you want adventure, wilderness, or remoteness — this is a city sportive. Non-negotiables: Group riding confidence — comfortable at 30-35+ km/h in a peloton of 100+ riders; Short steep climb preparation — 1-3 minute all-out efforts after extended flat riding; Wind riding experience — ability to echelon and draft in crosswinds on flat, exposed roads

### Sea Otter Fuego XL
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 70 mi | 7,800 ft | Monterey, California | April | mtb
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/fuego-mtb/

Sea Otter's sandbox climbs and corporate chaos create a perfectly mediocre gravel experience wrapped in mountain bike tradition. Fort Ord's sandy climbs and loose-over-hard descents turn your bike into a wrestling match. Two laps of relentless XC terrain where the sandstone gets looser and the ruts get deeper as 2,000 riders churn the course into a minefield of blown lines and shattered legs. Signature challenge: The sandy climbs don't negotiate with your fitness or technique. Fort Ord's sandstone terrain punishes every inefficient pedal stroke and rewards only those who've learned to dance with loose dirt. By lap two, the course has been churned into a minefield where clean lines disappear and mechanical sympathy becomes survival. If you can hold 250+ watts for three hours and enjoy fighting sandy ruts while dodging expo traffic, this works. The course isn't technical but those loose-over-hard descents will humble cocky roadies, and April weather swings from 40 to 90 degrees without warning. Skip it if you want pure gravel culture – this is MTB pageantry with drop bars allowed.

### Gran Fondo Hincapie
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 83 mi | 8,100 ft | Travelers Rest, South Carolina | October | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gran-fondo-hincapie/

George Hincapie's Tour de France training roads. Skyuka Mountain at 8.7%. The Southeast's premier gran fondo with 15 years of pedigree. George Hincapie trained on these roads for 17 Tours de France, and the Gran route makes you understand why. The 83-mile course from Hotel Domestique punches north into the Blue Ridge foothills, crossing into North Carolina's Polk County for the signature climbs before looping back to the Travelers Rest finish. Three timed segments — Skyuka Mountain, Green River Cove, and the final climb to the finish — structure the suffering around sustained, gradient-heavy climbing. Between the major climbs, rolling Blue Ridge countryside and Lake Road's technical turns keep the legs honest. The autumn foliage through dense forest canopy creates postcard scenery that's easy to miss when you're redlined on Skyuka's 8.7% gradient. This is a road event on paved surfaces throughout, but the mountainous character, the altitude variation from 1,100 ft to nearly 3,000 ft, and the relentless vertical accumulation make it one of the hardest gran fondos in the Eastern United States. Signature challenge: Skyuka Mountain. Four miles at 8.7% average gradient, 1,814 feet of vertical gain, a summit at 2,967 feet, and a technical descent so steep that carbon clincher rim brakes are banned from the course. This is where the Gran Fondo Hincapie earns the right to carry a Tour de France veteran's name. George Hincapie spent 17 years as professional cycling's most trusted domestique — the rider who sacrificed personal glory so others could stand on the Tour de France podium. His retirement project is the opposite philosophy: an event designed entirely for your experience. Gran Fondo Hincapie routes through the Blue Ridge foothill roads where Hincapie actually trained for July in France, summiting Skyuka Mountain at 8.7% and navigating Green River Cove's switchbacks — the same climbs that built the engine behind nine Tour de France champions. The 83-mile Gran with 8,100 feet of climbing is legitimate suffering, not a branded fun ride. But the Hotel Domestique base, the Michelin-starred chef dinner, the post-ride festival, and the parade of cycling legends create an experience layer that most gran fondos can only aspire to. The 2024 Hurricane Helene pivot — when the event raised $750,000 for the devastated Polk County communities that had hosted the fondo for 13 years — showed that this isn't corporate cycling tourism. It's an event with a soul. Yes if riding a Tour de France domestique's personal training roads alongside cycling legends appeals to you. Yes if you want a legitimate 83-mile, 8,100-foot climbing challenge wrapped in a luxury cycling weekend. Skip if you need competitive racing depth — this is participatory, not a race. Skip if premium pricing for a branded experience isn't your thing. Non-negotiables: Sustained climbing fitness — repeated 15-30 minute threshold efforts on 6-9% gradients; 80+ mile endurance base with 6,000+ feet of climbing in training; Descent confidence — practice technical switchback descents at speed

### Gran Fondo Marco Pantani
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 90 mi | 7,054 ft | Cesenatico, Emilia-Romagna, Italy | September | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gran-fondo-marco-pantani/

Il Pirata's training roads. His family's event. Cesenatico to the Romagnol hills and back. Since 2005. Three routes of increasing ambition, all starting from the Marco Pantani monument on the Cesenatico waterfront and heading inland through the Romagnol hills. The terrain transitions from flat Adriatic coastal roads to rolling countryside to the punchy, steep-gradient hills that characterize the pre-Apennine foothills. The Lungo route ventures into the Marche border country, adding the Botticella and additional climbing to the Ciola and Montevecchio that feature on all routes. The rhythm is distinctly Romagnol — short, sharp climbs separated by rolling valleys with small villages, vineyards, and stone walls. These are not long Alpine grinds; they are the repeated punchy efforts that Pantani used to build explosive power before translating it to the high mountains. Signature challenge: The cumulative climbing across the Ciola, Botticella, and Montevecchio is honest rather than heroic — 2,150m on the Lungo route. The real challenge is emotional. These are the roads where Marco Pantani trained. The gradients he ground out on winter mornings. The hills he repeated until his legs could dance on the pedals at 90 rpm uphill. Riding them with the knowledge of what he became — and how it ended — adds a dimension that no elevation profile can capture. The Gran Fondo Marco Pantani is not the hardest ride in Italy, not the most scenic, and not the most famous. It is the most personal. Organized by the Pantani family, starting from the bronze statue of Il Pirata on the Cesenatico waterfront, following the roads he rode as a teenager and as a Tour de France champion, passing the monument at Montevecchio where his spirit lives in the hills. Marco Pantani — born in Cesenatico 1970, dead in Rimini 2004, the last man to win the Giro and the Tour in the same year — built his legendary climbing legs on these Romagnol hills before he ever touched the Galibier or the Mortirolo. This event lets you ride in his ghost. The climbs are honest, the countryside is charming, and the emotional weight is unlike anything else in amateur cycling. Yes if Pantani's story moves you and you want to ride the roads where he built his legendary climbing engine. Yes if September in Italy with warm weather and honest Romagnol hills sounds like your kind of weekend. Skip if you want pure athletic challenge — Nove Colli in the same town offers more distance and climbing. Skip if you have no connection to Pantani's legacy — without that context, this is a pleasant but mid-tier Italian sportive. Non-negotiables: Hill repeat training — sustained efforts on 6-8% gradients for 15-25 minutes; Multi-climb endurance — 4+ distinct climbs in a single training ride; 145 km distance capability with climbing — full dress rehearsal

### Gravel and Tar Classic
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 99 mi | 6,562 ft | Manawatū-Whanganui, New Zealand | February | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gravel-and-tar-classic/

New Zealand's most beautiful way to discover that punctures and time cuts don't negotiate. 160 km, 2,000 m. 70% gravel, 30% paved. New Zealand countryside—rolling hills, farmland. Technical rating: 3/5. Signature challenge: 160 km long distance + 2,000 m elevation. New Zealand countryside. Gravel and Tar Classic is New Zealand countryside—160 km, 2,000 m elevation. Founded ~2018. 70% gravel, 30% paved. New Zealand countryside—rolling hills, farmland. 300-500 riders. Manawatū-Whanganui, NZ. February summer conditions (18-28°C). Affordable entry (NZ$150-$200 / $90-$120). If you want New Zealand countryside with long distance and affordable entry, this is it. If you need short distance or local/domestic, skip it. 160 km is long. International destination race. If you can handle 99 miles where the gravel sectors exist specifically to fracture the field and leave riders scattered across the Manawatū countryside, this is your kind of suffering. The 25% gravel ratio sounds friendly until you're dealing with punctures while chasing time cuts that historically claim a significant chunk of the field. Show up undertrained and you'll join the long list of DNFs who underestimated what 'toughest one-day race in Oceania' actually means.

### Gravel Roubaix
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 100 mi | 8,000 ft | Palmerton, Pennsylvania | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gravel-roubaix/

A proper gravel beatdown disguised as a backyard Pennsylvania sufferfest. Barry-Roubaix throws everything at you except actual cobblestones. Eighty percent gravel roads with pavement breaks, sand traps, mud wallows, and one brutal mile of rocky two-track that separates pretenders from finishers. The terrain rolls constantly through Michigan farmland, never letting you settle into a rhythm. Signature challenge: Sager Road is the course's true character test—a narrow, unmaintained seasonal road that can be sandy, rocky, or muddy depending on conditions. It's where separation occurs in the front of the race because fitness alone won't save you when your bike handling skills get exposed on technical terrain at race pace. Show up ready for 8,000 feet of climbing and terrain that switches moods faster than spring weather. The Three Sisters will expose weak legs early, and Sager Road's sandy chaos separates pretenders from contenders. You need solid bike handling skills and the fitness to climb repeatedly—this isn't a gentle introduction to gravel racing.

### Grinduro Canada
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 67 mi | 5,900 ft | Saint-Urbain-de-Charlevoix, Quebec, Canada | August | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/grinduro-canada/

A mountain bike race disguised as gravel, where meteorite-shaped terrain separates the prepared from the delusional. Grinduro Canada drops you inside a 450-million-year-old meteorite crater where Mother Nature has been plotting your demise since the Ordovician period. Four timed segments demand everything from singletrack handling to sand survival, connected by 'neutral' sections that aren't neutral at all. This isn't gravel racing—it's enduro with a gravel entry fee. Signature challenge: The course pretends to be gravel but demands full mountain bike skills across four timed segments that expose every weakness in your technical repertoire. Ancient meteorite impacts created terrain that laughs at road bike geometry and narrow tires—show up on the wrong equipment and the crater will humble you publicly. If you panic on technical singletrack or think 25mm tires are acceptable, stay home. This demands MTB skills, fat rubber, and comfort with getting your ego adjusted by Quebec sand pits. The festival vibes and excellent food soften the blow, but won't save you from walking the steep, loose climbs that eat narrow tires alive.

### Pan-Mass Challenge (PMC)
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 177 mi | 6,800 ft | Worcester to Provincetown, Massachusetts, USA (multiple routes across Massachusetts) | August | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/pan-mass-challenge/

6,500 riders. 177 miles. $1.125 billion raised. The bike ride that fights cancer. A two-day crossing of Massachusetts on paved roads, from the rolling hills of central Mass to the windswept tip of Cape Cod. Day 1 is the harder day — 100 miles from Worcester featuring genuine New England climbing in the first 50 miles (long, rolling ascents and fast descents), gradually flattening as the route approaches the Cape Cod Canal and the finish at Mass Maritime Academy in Bourne. The overnight at MMA is a PMC institution — thousands of riders sleeping in dorms, tents, and ship quarters, fueling for Day 2. Sunday's 77 miles from Bourne to Provincetown are rolling-to-flat Cape Cod terrain with ocean breezes, cranberry bogs, and the charming towns of the outer Cape, punctuated by short steep climbs in Truro before the emotional finish at the Provincetown Monument. Signature challenge: The emotional weight. The PMC's signature challenge is not a climb or a headwind — it is riding 177 miles while carrying the names and stories of people fighting cancer. The physical effort is real. The emotional effort is greater. The Pan-Mass Challenge is not a bike ride. It is a $78-million-a-year cancer research funding machine that uses bicycles as its engine. Since 1980, when Billy Starr convinced 36 riders to cycle from Springfield to Provincetown, the PMC has raised $1.125 billion for Dana-Farber Cancer Institute — 100% of every rider-raised dollar going directly to research, treatment, and patient care. Today, 6,500 riders cross Massachusetts on routes from 25 to 177 miles, supported by 3,500 volunteers and an organization refined over 46 years. The cycling is honest New England terrain — rolling hills, coastal roads, and a two-day format that demands real fitness. But the experience transcends sport. You ride past signs that say 'Thank you — my daughter is alive because of Dana-Farber.' You pedal alongside survivors wearing jerseys with their diagnosis dates.
