Ned Gravel is a high-altitude mountain gravel race in Colorado's Front Range with significant climbing and thin air. Nederland sits at 8,000+ feet, and the course climbs to 10,300 feet through ghost towns and mining country. The Colorado Rockies setting and altitude challenge elevate it above typical regional events, but the field remains mostly regional. Near Boulder with moderate access. Well-organized with four distance options. If you're in Colorado and want a mountain gravel challenge near Boulder with real altitude—yes, it's a solid event. If you're from out of state—there are more prestigious options. Non-negotiables: Altitude acclimatization; Mountain climbing endurance; Mountain weather preparation

### Pelotonia
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 100 mi | 2,500 ft | Columbus, Ohio, USA | August | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/pelotonia/

$339 million raised. 7,500 riders. 100% of every dollar to cancer research. The terrain is Ohio. The mission is everything. A point-to-point ride from downtown Columbus across Central Ohio's gently rolling farmland. The route leaves the urban grid within the first 10 miles, transitioning through suburbs and into genuinely rural countryside — corn and soybean fields, white farmhouses, quiet county roads lined with hardwood trees. The terrain is a series of short rollers, none individually significant but cumulatively honest over 100 miles in August heat. The 100-mile route culminates at Kenyon College in Gambier — a hilltop liberal arts campus that transforms into a finish line celebration. The 165-mile two-day option adds a Sunday return from Kenyon through different countryside to New Albany. Signature challenge: The heat. Not the terrain, not the distance, not the climbing — the August Ohio heat and humidity that turns an otherwise moderate century into a genuine test of preparation and resilience. Every experienced Pelotonia rider will tell you the same thing: respect the weather. Pelotonia is the rare cycling event where the suffering has a point beyond personal satisfaction. $339 million raised for cancer research — every dollar from riders going directly to the Ohio State Comprehensive Cancer Center, operational costs covered by corporate partners so nothing leaks to overhead. The riding itself is honest but unremarkable: flat-to-rolling Ohio farmland in August heat, paved roads, no technical demands. What makes Pelotonia extraordinary is the community — 7,500 riders who have raised thousands of dollars each, many riding for deeply personal reasons, united by a single goal that is bigger than any of them. The Opening Ceremony will give you chills. The finish line might make you cry. The terrain will not. Yes if you want to be part of one of the most impactful cycling communities in America. Yes if cancer has touched your life and you want to ride with meaning. Yes if you value community and mission over scenery and competition. Skip if you cannot commit to the fundraising requirement, need competitive racing, or want dramatic terrain. Non-negotiables: Heat acclimatization training — train in heat and humidity before August; Hydration and electrolyte strategy tested on rides of 60+ miles; Fundraising commitment fulfilled before ride weekend

### USA Cycling Gravel Nationals
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 131 mi | 7,000 ft | La Crescent, Minnesota (2025-26); previously Gering, Nebraska (2023-24) | September | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/usa-cycling-gravel-nationals/

The national championship with open entry—stars and stripes on Driftless gravel with UCI Worlds pathway. Driftless Region 'champagne gravel' with punchy blufftop climbs. The terrain looks Midwestern-gentle but delivers relentless 10-15% gradients throughout. Apple orchards, gravel quarries, and river valley views provide scenery between efforts. Signature challenge: The relentless punchy climbs—not big sustained efforts, but repeated 10-15% gradients that accumulate suffering throughout the championship distance. USA Cycling Gravel Nationals delivers what the name promises—a legitimate national championship with open entry and UCI Worlds qualification. The Driftless Region course is harder than it looks with punchy 10-15% climbs and 'champagne gravel' that gets loose when freshly graded. Young event (est. 2023) finding its identity, but the jersey means something. Yes if you want a legitimate national championship jersey and Worlds qualification. Yes if you appreciate accessible championship racing. Skip if you want destination adventure over competitive credentialing.

### Vapor Trail 125
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 125 mi | 9,000 ft | Leadville, Colorado | September | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/vapor-trail-125/

125-mile high-altitude Colorado mountain gravel in Leadville. High-altitude mountain gravel for 125 miles. Signature challenge: 125-mile distance at extreme altitude (10,000ft+) with mountain climbing. Vapor Trail 125 is a 125-mile high-altitude gravel race in Leadville, Colorado—mountain terrain with extreme altitude (10,000ft+). The combination of 125 miles and extreme altitude is a serious challenge, but the moderate organization and regional field keep it in Tier 3. Leadville provides moderate access. Summer mountain weather at altitude adds complexity. If you want a high-altitude endurance test—yes, it's a serious challenge. If you want organization or pro field depth—look elsewhere. Non-negotiables: Century+ endurance at altitude; Extreme altitude acclimation; High-altitude pacing

### Boulder Roubaix Road Race
Tier 2 | Score: 61/100 | 30 mi | 1,200 ft | Boulder Reservoir, Boulder County, Colorado, USA | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/boulder-roubaix/

Colorado's 34-year spring classic. 61% dirt, USAC-sanctioned, and unapologetically hard. Rolling mixed-surface through North Boulder County agricultural landscape. The 2026 course is a new 30-mile (48 km) loop — 61% unpaved, 39% paved — that starts and finishes at Boulder Reservoir. The terrain is constant undulation through working farm and county roads that can be anywhere from smooth to extremely rutted depending on weather. No single dramatic climb, but the accumulated rolling and the wind across open terrain create a war of attrition. The finishing stretch along the Boulder Feeder Canal and across the reservoir dams adds a challenging technical element to the final miles. Signature challenge: The wind. Not the terrain, not the climbing, not the distance — the wind across open North Boulder County farmland is what defines Boulder Roubaix. In 2024, it shut down the race. On any given edition, it determines whether the dirt roads are fast-rolling or soul-crushing. Combined with the unpredictable surface conditions and spring weather variability, the wind transforms a rolling 30-mile circuit into a legitimate test of tactical racing and survival. Boulder Roubaix is what happens when you take the spirit of Paris-Roubaix and transplant it to Colorado's Front Range farm roads. Since 1992, this biennial race has sent riders onto the unpaved county roads north of Boulder — roads that were 'gravel' decades before gravel became a category. The course does not need cobblestones to create chaos: rutted dirt roads, spring wind that can shut down the race, and surface conditions that change hourly deliver the unpredictability that defines a classic. It is a proper USAC road race with categorized fields and a pro purse, not a recreational ride. The 2026 edition moves to Boulder Reservoir with a new 30-mile loop that is 61% unpaved — further embracing the mixed-surface identity that has always defined this event. Yes if you are a competitive road or gravel racer who wants a legitimate USAC road race on mixed surfaces. Yes if you live in Colorado and want to test your early-season form on the defining spring race. Yes if you want to experience the event that was doing 'gravel racing' before the term existed. Skip if you want a long-distance endurance challenge (most categories are 30-60 miles), or if you need a destination event with a national profile. Non-negotiables: Mixed-surface tire selection tested on dirt roads; Wind-exposure training on open terrain; Self-sufficiency kit: pump, spare tube, multi-tool

### Cape Rouleur
Tier 2 | Score: 61/100 | 373 mi | 19,685 ft | Franschhoek, Western Cape, South Africa | March | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/cape-rouleur/

600 km through the Cape Winelands on closed roads with pro support. Cycling's most luxurious sportive experience. The Cape Rouleur unfolds across five stages radiating from Franschhoek into the Western Cape's finest cycling terrain. Each stage delivers a different character: wine country rolling roads through Stellenbosch, mountain pass climbs through the Hottentots Holland range, coastal sections with views of False Bay, and the dramatic landscapes around Paarl and Wellington. The riding is demanding — 120 km per day average with 1,200m climbing — but the closed roads and support infrastructure remove every variable except your legs and the scenery. The final day typically includes an approach into Cape Town, setting up the optional Cape Town Cycle Tour. Signature challenge: The queen stage mountain passes. After three prior stages of cumulative fatigue, the most demanding climbing of the week arrives. The Western Cape's mountain passes are not Alpine monsters, but at 30C with the Cape Doctor blowing and four days of riding in the legs, 1,500m of climbing in a single stage is absolutely honest. The Cape Rouleur is what happens when you give 200 amateur cyclists the professional treatment in one of the world's most beautiful cycling destinations. HotChillee's formula is proven: closed roads, motorbike outriders, lead car nutrition, mechanics with spare wheels, a race doctor, and a broom wagon. You don't think about traffic, navigation, or mechanical issues. You just ride.

The setting makes it sing. Five stages through the Cape Winelands — Franschhoek, Stellenbosch, and the surrounding wine country — deliver vineyards, mountain passes, and coastal roads in succession. The hospitality in Franschhoek between stages is exceptional. Wine tastings, gourmet South African food, poolside recovery. This is cycling as luxury experience.

The price reflects the quality: GBP 1,350+ entry is steep, and flights to Cape Town add another GBP 500-1,200. But for the cycling tourist who wants five days of world-class riding with zero logistics burden, there is nothing better. Yes if you want a premium multi-stage cycling holiday with world-class support in a stunning setting. Yes if the combination of serious daily riding and Franschhoek wine country hospitality appeals to your inner cycling tourist. Skip if the GBP 3,000-4,000 total cost is prohibitive. Skip if you want a competitive event with timing and rankings. Non-negotiables: Multi-day block training — 5 consecutive days of 100+ km rides to simulate stage race fatigue; Heat acclimatization — riding in warm conditions (25C+) for extended periods; Wind riding practice — headwind and crosswind group riding skills

### Cohutta Gravel Grinder
Tier 2 | Score: 61/100 | 100 mi | 9,000 ft | Chatsworth, Georgia | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/cohutta-gravel-grinder/

100-mile Georgia mountain gravel through Cohutta Wilderness. Mountain gravel for 100 miles. Signature challenge: 100 miles with 9000 feet of mountain climbing through Cohutta Wilderness. Cohutta Gravel Grinder is a 100-mile mountain gravel race through north Georgia's Cohutta Wilderness—significant climbing with wilderness scenery. The organization is solid, and the Georgia reputation is decent. However, it's a regional field, keeping it in Tier 3. Chatsworth provides moderate access. The 100 miles and 9000 feet of climbing are serious challenges. If you want a 100-mile mountain challenge with significant climbing—yes, it's a solid event. If you want pro field depth or cooler conditions—look elsewhere. Non-negotiables: Century mountain endurance; Mountain climbing fitness; Heat preparation

### Finnish Gravel
Tier 2 | Score: 61/100 | 110 mi | 2,129 ft | Lahti, Paijanne Tavastia, Finland | August | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/finnish-gravel/

F1 glamour meets Nordic gravel in a UNESCO Geopark—premium experience, premium price tag, unique bucket-list appeal. Fast, flowing Finnish gravel through UNESCO Geopark terrain. Well-maintained gravel roads, lakeside routes, and Nordic ski paths create a high-speed experience rather than a technical challenge. The continuous undulations and steep kickers separate riders, but this is racing, not survival. Riders average 35 kph on the twisting ski path sections. Signature challenge: Not one killer climb, but the relentless accumulation of steep kickers and undulations that 'will separate' riders. Plus the bugs at every stop. FNLD GRVL is what happens when an F1 driver falls in love with gravel. It's not the most challenging course or the deepest pro field, but for a unique Nordic cultural experience wrapped in world-class event production, it delivers. The midnight sun, the UNESCO Geopark, the Finnish hospitality, and the SBT GRVL pedigree create something genuinely special. Yes if you want a unique Nordic cultural experience with world-class event production. Yes if you're an F1/Bottas fan. Skip if you're chasing big climbing or watching your budget. International travelers should weigh the high costs against the unique experience.

### TD Five Boro Bike Tour
Tier 2 | Score: 61/100 | 40 mi | 780 ft | New York City, New York | May | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/five-boro-bike-tour/

32,000 riders claim New York City's streets for one spring Sunday. The largest cycling event in America. Flat and festive. This is a city tour, not an athletic course. The 40 miles wind through all five boroughs on car-free streets, crossing five major bridges. The route begins in Lower Manhattan at Bowling Green, heads north via Sixth Avenue through Central Park, continues into Harlem and the Bronx via the Madison Avenue Bridge, re-enters Manhattan along the FDR Drive, crosses the Queensboro Bridge into Queens, traverses the Pulaski Bridge into Brooklyn, rides the Brooklyn-Queens Expressway and Gowanus Parkway, and culminates with the crossing of the Verrazzano-Narrows Bridge into Staten Island. The Finish Festival at Fort Wadsworth and the free Staten Island Ferry ride back to Manhattan complete the experience. Signature challenge: There is no physical challenge. The signature experience is the Verrazzano-Narrows Bridge crossing — the only day of the year this bridge opens to cyclists. The longest suspension bridge in the US, with New York Harbor spread beneath you and the Manhattan skyline behind you. The Five Boro Bike Tour is not about the cycling. It is about New York City handing its streets to 32,000 people on bicycles and saying: this is yours today. Forty miles of car-free NYC — the FDR Drive, Central Park, the Queensboro Bridge, the Verrazzano — is an experience that exists nowhere else on earth. The ride itself is flat, easy, and non-competitive. The experience is transcendent. You will not get fitter. You will not get faster. You will get a memory that lasts forever. You don't 'race' the Five Boro Bike Tour — you experience it. Yes if you want the once-in-a-lifetime thrill of cycling car-free through all five NYC boroughs and crossing the Verrazzano-Narrows Bridge. Yes if you want to ride alongside 32,000 people from 65 countries. Skip if you need athletic challenge, competitive racing, or technical terrain. Non-negotiables: Comfort riding in dense groups — practice pack riding; Rain gear and layering strategy — check forecast; Post-ride exit plan — know the ferry logistics

### L'Etape Denmark by Tour de France
Tier 2 | Score: 61/100 | 99 mi | 4,593 ft | Various start points across Jutland, Denmark — all routes finish in Viborg | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/letape-denmark/

ASO's Tour de France franchise meets Denmark's ancient Viking road. 7,000 riders, five routes up to 300 km, TdF jerseys, and a finish festival in Viborg. A point-to-point journey along Denmark's ancient glacial ridge — the Haervejen road that Vikings, pilgrims, and merchants traveled for a thousand years. The terrain is rolling rather than mountainous, with the Jutland ridgeline delivering constant undulation through heathlands, forests, and historic towns. The landscape is understated and pastoral — wide horizons, open farmland, ancient churches, and Viking-era landmarks. Wind is the primary adversary, channeled across the open terrain by prevailing westerlies. Each route starts from a different Jutland town and converges on Viborg, Denmark's former capital, for a shared finish celebration. The 300 km Queen Stage begins across the border in Flensburg, Germany, adding an international dimension to an already mythic route. Signature challenge: The 300 km Queen Stage from Flensburg to Viborg — 186 miles of sustained effort along the Viking road with 2,500 meters of accumulated climbing. The challenge is not any single climb but the relentless accumulation of effort across rolling terrain, exposed to Jutland's winds, for 14-18 hours. Denmark does not have mountains, but it has distance, wind, and the psychological weight of a route that stretches from Germany to the heart of the Danish peninsula. L'Etape Denmark is what happens when ASO's Tour de France franchise lands in the flattest country in Scandinavia — and somehow it works. The Haervejen, an ancient road traveled by Vikings and pilgrims for centuries, provides the narrative backbone that Denmark's topography cannot. Seven thousand riders from 30 countries converge on five routes, all finishing in Viborg — a city older than the Tour de France by about 900 years. The TdF jersey classifications are legitimate, the feed stations are legendary, and the 300 km Queen Stage from Germany to Denmark is a genuine endurance test. DGI's grassroots organization and 1,000+ local volunteers give the event a community warmth that pure commercial sportives cannot replicate. The climbing is modest by any international standard — Mur de Refsgaarde at 8.2% for 550 meters is the hardest the course gets — but the wind across open Jutland will remind you that flat does not mean easy. Yes if you want a well-organized TdF franchise experience in Scandinavia with genuine community atmosphere. Yes if the 300 km ultra-distance Queen Stage appeals to your endurance ambitions. Yes if you want an accessible entry point to international road cycling events — the five distance options accommodate everyone. Skip if you want serious climbing — Denmark's glacial ridgeline is charming but not challenging by Alpine or Pyrenean standards. Skip if you prioritize deep competitive racing over mass-participation experience. Non-negotiables: Sustained endurance for 6-18 hours depending on route choice; Wind riding technique and mental preparation; Nutrition strategy tested over target ride duration

### Ring of Kerry Charity Cycle
Tier 2 | Score: 61/100 | 106 mi | 5,774 ft | Killarney, County Kerry, Ireland | July | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/ring-of-kerry-charity-cycle/

170 km around the Iveragh Peninsula. Ireland's biggest one-day cycling event. EUR 22 million raised. The Atlantic coast is the scenery. Charity is the point. A counterclockwise loop around the Iveragh Peninsula on Ireland's Atlantic seaboard. The route leaves Killarney heading west through Killorglin, follows the coast south through Cahersiveen, Waterville, and Caherdaniel, climbs Coomakista for Skellig views, continues east through Sneem to Kenmare, then returns to Killarney via Moll's Gap — the highest and toughest climb of the day. The terrain is rolling throughout with two significant climbs. The character alternates between exposed Atlantic coast road and sheltered inland valleys. It is not an extreme route by European granfondo standards, but 170 km with 1,760m of climbing and the possibility of Atlantic headwinds makes it a demanding day for recreational riders. Signature challenge: Moll's Gap at 140 km — the highest point on the route with poor road surface and steady gradient. It is the moment when under-prepared riders discover that 170 km of Atlantic coast riding has consequences. The climb itself is moderate, but the context is not. The Ring of Kerry Charity Cycle is not a race. It has no timing chips, no results board, no podium. What it has is 44 years of history, EUR 22 million raised for 200+ charities, 1,200 volunteers who make the roads of Kerry safe for thousands of cyclists, and 170 km of Atlantic coast scenery that ranks among the most beautiful cycling routes in Europe. The Iveragh Peninsula delivers the Skellig Islands, Coomakista's ocean views, Moll's Gap, and the approach through Killarney National Park. The climbing is honest but not savage. The weather is a gamble with the Atlantic. The community spirit is not manufactured — it is the DNA of the event. Founded in 1982, this is Ireland's largest one-day cycling event and one of Europe's most significant charity rides. You do not ride the Ring of Kerry to prove anything. You ride it to contribute to something larger than yourself, and the Atlantic scenery is the reward for showing up. Yes if you want one of Europe's great scenic charity rides — the Iveragh Peninsula coastline is spectacular. Yes if community and charitable purpose matter as much as personal achievement. Skip if you need competition, timing, or race results. Skip if Atlantic weather uncertainty is a dealbreaker for a trip to western Ireland. Non-negotiables: Wet-weather riding kit and preparation; Endurance base — minimum one 150+ km training ride on rolling terrain; Nutrition strategy tested on long training rides

### STP — Seattle to Portland
Tier 2 | Score: 61/100 | 206 mi | 5,100 ft | Seattle to Portland, Washington/Oregon, USA | July | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/seattle-to-portland/

206 miles from Seattle to Portland. 6,000 riders. 47 years. The Pacific Northwest's cycling pilgrimage. A 206-mile south-bound corridor from Seattle to Portland on paved country roads, avoiding Interstate 5. The route threads through the green lowlands of western Washington and Oregon — Puget Sound farmland, the Nisqually and Chehalis river valleys, the Cowlitz corridor, and the Columbia River crossing into Oregon. The terrain is predominantly flat to gently rolling, with one honest climb near Puyallup and energy-sapping rollers between Chehalis and Napavine. Small towns punctuate the route every 15-25 miles, each one a checkpoint and a morale boost. The course crosses from Washington into Oregon via the Lewis and Clark Bridge over the Columbia River. The final miles run through St. Helens and Scappoose before crossing Portland's St. Johns Bridge to the finish at Holladay Park. The character is rural, green, and relentlessly forward — 206 miles in one direction, no turning back. Signature challenge: The distance itself. STP has no single feature that defines the course — no brutal climb, no technical descent, no unrideable sector. The challenge is 206 miles of sustained forward motion, managing nutrition and hydration across 8-14 hours of saddle time, and the psychological discipline of riding one direction for an entire day (or two). The Chehalis-to-Napavine rollers on tired legs are the closest thing to a crux, but the real opponent is the odometer. STP is less a bike ride and more a regional migration. Every July, 6,000+ cyclists ride 206 miles from Seattle to Portland on country roads, through farmland and small towns, sleeping in a field in Centralia, and finishing at Holladay Park with a medal and a beer. The terrain is flat. The competition is nonexistent. The technical demands are zero. None of that matters. What matters is that for 47 years, this ride has been the Pacific Northwest's defining cycling event — a two-day rolling community of thousands, stretching from the Puget Sound to the Willamette Valley, covering 206 miles that transform from intimidating to accomplished to annual tradition. You ride STP once because you want to see if you can. You ride it again because it's July and that's what you do. Yes if you want one of America's great city-to-city cycling experiences. Yes if community and tradition matter more than competition. Yes if riding 206 miles through the PNW countryside with thousands of fellow cyclists sounds like your idea of a perfect July weekend. Skip if you need racing, climbing, technical terrain, or anything beyond flat-road endurance. Non-negotiables: Double-century fueling strategy — tested in training at 100+ miles; Minimum one 130+ mile training ride (two-day) or 160+ mile ride (one-day); Heat contingency plan — hydration, electrolytes, cooling strategy for 90F+

### Transcordilleras
Tier 2 | Score: 61/100 | 120 mi | 15,000 ft | Colombian Andes, Colombia | — | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/transcordilleras/

Colombian Andes mountain gravel adventure. Colombian Andes mountain gravel. Signature challenge: Colombian Andes extreme mountainous terrain at altitude. Transcordilleras is a regional event in Colombian Andes—extreme mountainous terrain with massive elevation at high altitude. The Colombian Andes are stunning, but the difficult/expensive access, extreme altitude, and regional field keep it firmly in Tier 3. If you have a big budget and want an extreme Colombian experience—maybe. For most riders—there are better options. Non-negotiables: Altitude training; Mountain endurance; Budget planning

### RBC GranFondo Whistler
Tier 2 | Score: 61/100 | 76 mi | 7,546 ft | Vancouver to Whistler, British Columbia, Canada | September | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/whistler-granfondo/

122 km on a closed highway from Vancouver to Whistler. The Sea-to-Sky belongs to bikes for one day — and it's magnificent. A point-to-point journey from sea level to the mountains along one of North America's most scenic highways. The course starts in Vancouver's Stanley Park, crosses the Lions Gate Bridge, and follows the Sea-to-Sky Highway north along Howe Sound's coastal fjord before turning inland at Squamish to climb through the Coast Mountains to Whistler Village. The first half is coastal and rolling — dramatic ocean-and-mountain views with punchy climbs at Furry Creek and Britannia Beach. The second half is the grind — a sustained ascent from Squamish through the Cheakamus Valley and Garibaldi Highlands, where the ocean recedes and the mountains close in. The highway is fully closed to traffic, giving riders a car-free lane on pavement that was resurfaced for the 2010 Winter Olympics. The finish in Whistler Village at 670 meters elevation feels earned — you started at the ocean and climbed through the clouds to get here. Signature challenge: The second half — from Squamish to Whistler. The coastal scenery fades, the sustained climbing begins, and the relentless 2-4% grade through the Cheakamus Valley separates the trained from the hopeful. Most of the 2,300m of climbing loads into the final 60 km. There is one day a year when the Sea-to-Sky Highway — British Columbia's most famous road, the corridor that connects Vancouver's glass towers to Whistler's snow-capped peaks — belongs entirely to bicycles. That day is GranFondo day. You roll out of Stanley Park at dawn. You cross the Lions Gate Bridge with 5,000 riders and the sun rising over the North Shore. You ride along Howe Sound's fjord-blue water with mountains falling into the ocean on both sides. You climb through the Coast Range on pavement so smooth and so empty of cars that you forget this is normally a highway. You finish in Whistler Village, where a beer and a party and the disbelief that you just did that are all waiting. The RBC GranFondo Whistler is not a hard-man's race. It's not technical, it's not remote, and it won't destroy you. But it will give you 122 km of closed-highway riding through some of the most spectacular scenery in North America, and that experience — the sheer, rare luxury of a car-free highway through the mountains — is something you simply cannot get any other way. Yes if you want one of North America's premier mass-participation cycling experiences with world-class scenery and bulletproof organization. Yes if riding a closed highway through the Coast Mountains sounds like your idea of paradise. Skip if you want a competitive road race, technical terrain, or a budget-friendly weekend. Non-negotiables: Build endurance for sustained climbing in the second half; Layering strategy for the sea-to-mountain temperature transition; Nutrition plan for 3.5-8.5 hours of sustained effort

### WYO 131
Tier 2 | Score: 61/100 | 131 mi | 7,500 ft | Lander, Wyoming | September | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/wyo-131/

Regional Wyoming ultra-distance through mountain and high desert. Mix of mountain and high desert terrain through Wyoming wilderness. Signature challenge: 131-mile ultra-distance at high altitude through Wyoming wilderness. WYO 131 offers a genuine ultra-distance challenge—131 miles through Wyoming mountain and high desert terrain. It's a well-organized regional ultra event with good support, but the field is regional and it's remote central Wyoming. The adventure of riding through Wind River Range country is the main appeal, along with the ultra-distance challenge. If you're in Wyoming or the Rockies and want an ultra-distance challenge—yes, it's a well-organized event with genuine wilderness appeal. If you're traveling from far away—there are more prestigious ultra events elsewhere. Non-negotiables: Ultra-distance endurance; Altitude preparation; Pacing discipline

### America's Most Beautiful Bike Ride
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 72 mi | 4,024 ft | Stateline, Nevada, USA | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/americas-most-beautiful-bike-ride/

72 miles around Lake Tahoe at 6,300 feet. The name is audacious. The scenery makes it true. A clockwise circumnavigation of Lake Tahoe on paved highways through both Nevada and California. The route hugs the lakeshore for most of its 72 miles, delivering a constantly shifting panorama of emerald water, granite peaks, and dense pine forest. Two meaningful climbs — Emerald Bay and Spooner Summit — break up the rolling lakeshore terrain. The entire ride sits between 6,200 and 7,100 feet, giving even the flat sections a high-altitude character that sea-level riders will feel in their legs and lungs. Signature challenge: Emerald Bay — the 800-foot climb on narrow road to one of the most dramatic overlooks in the Sierra Nevada. The scenery is the reward, and the narrow road with 2,500 riders is the test. America's Most Beautiful Bike Ride has the most audacious name in cycling — and the scenery to back it up. Circumnavigating Lake Tahoe at 6,300 feet delivers 72 miles of emerald water, granite peaks, and pine-scented air that would make a European alpine pass jealous. The climbing is moderate. The competition is nonexistent. The charity mission adds emotional weight. None of that matters once you're riding the west shore with Tahoe glittering below you and the Sierra Nevada rising into a cloudless June sky. The name isn't marketing. It might actually be true. Yes if you want one of America's most scenic cycling experiences. Yes if you want a well-supported charity ride with community and meaning. Skip if you need competition, serious climbing, or technical terrain. Non-negotiables: Altitude-adjusted fitness — train with heart rate awareness for 6,200+ ft; 60+ mile training ride with sustained climbing efforts; Sun protection and hydration strategy for high-altitude dry conditions

### Bighorn Gravel
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 62 mi | 6,000 ft | Gypsum, Colorado | June | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/bighorn-gravel/

Regional Colorado mountain gravel with high altitude and significant climbing near Vail. Mountain gravel with significant climbing at high altitude. Signature challenge: 6000 feet of climbing at 7000-9000+ feet altitude. Bighorn Gravel offers a genuine Colorado mountain gravel challenge with high altitude and significant climbing. Gypsum is a small town near Vail with basic amenities. The course is challenging with 6000 feet of climbing at 7000-9000+ feet elevation. It's a well-organized regional event with a moderate field. The main appeal is the mountain setting near Vail and altitude challenge. If you're in Colorado or the Front Range and want a mountain gravel challenge—yes, it's a well-organized event with genuine altitude and climbing. If you're traveling from far away—there are more prestigious mountain events elsewhere. Non-negotiables: Altitude preparation; Climbing strength; Thunderstorm awareness

### Granfondo Colnago
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 87 mi | 6,594 ft | Desenzano del Garda, Lombardy, Italy | April | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/granfondo-colnago/

Colnago's annual cycling festival on Lake Garda: three days, 4,000 riders from 30+ countries, morainic hills, and the brand that built bikes for champions. Rolling morainic terrain through the wine and olive country south of Lake Garda, building toward pre-Alpine foothill climbs in the second half. The route leaves Desenzano along the lakefront, climbs into the Valtenesi — a landscape of vine-covered ridges and medieval villages — and pushes into the Val Sabbia on the long course. The morainic hills never let you rest: even the nominally flat sections are rolling, and the constant up-and-down accumulates. The climbs — San Michele, Coste, Colle San Gallo — are sustained rather than savage, but Colle San Gallo saves a 12.8% summit ramp for the last kilometers when legs are tired from 110+ km of undulation. The final 30 km of ups and downs between Bedizzole and Lonato through the green hills of Garda is a war of attrition against the morainic rollers. Signature challenge: Colle San Gallo's summit ramp — 12.8% after 9 km of building gradient and 110+ km of riding. The morainic hills have been draining you since kilometer 10. The two previous climbs have taken their toll. And then the road tilts to nearly 13% for the final push. It is not the longest or steepest climb in Italian cycling, but its placement in the route — late, after relentless undulation — makes it the moment where the Granfondo Colnago stops being a scenic ride and starts being a test. The Granfondo Colnago is what happens when one of cycling's most legendary manufacturers throws a party at one of Italy's most beautiful lakes. Ernesto Colnago started building frames in Cambiago in 1954 — bikes for Merckx's Hour Record, for Saronni's rainbow jersey, for the peloton's aristocracy. Sixty years later, the brand's annual festival draws 4,000 riders from over 30 countries to Desenzano del Garda for a three-day celebration that includes gravel rides, bike demos, an expo village, and the Sunday granfondo through the morainic hills of Valtenesi. The riding is honest: 140 km through olive groves and vineyards, 2,010 meters of climbing over rolling morainic terrain, a late sting from Colle San Gallo's 12.8% summit ramp. Lake Garda provides the scenery. Colnago provides the brand wrapper. What you're buying is not the hardest or most prestigious gran fondo in Italy — it's the most complete cycling weekend on one of Europe's prettiest lakes. Yes if you want a complete cycling weekend rather than a single-day event — the three-day festival with gravel rides, expo, and granfondo is the strongest draw. Yes if the Colnago name means something to your cycling soul and you want to ride under that banner. Yes if you want to combine serious riding with Lake Garda tourism. Skip if you want alpine suffering — 2,010m of climbing is honest but not transformative. Skip if you want a granfondo with deep competitive racing history. Skip if brand-sponsored events feel more like marketing than cycling to you. Non-negotiables: Sustained endurance base for 140 km of constant morainic undulation; Late-race climbing capacity — train for hard efforts after 100+ km; Rain-ready kit and wet descending confidence

### Granfondo Via del Sale
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 97 mi | 7,260 ft | Cervia, Emilia-Romagna, Italy | March | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/granfondo-via-del-sale/

The Salt Road. From Cervia's ancient salt pans to the Romagna hills. Pantani showed up at the first one. A road gran fondo that traces the ancient salt trading route from the Adriatic coast into the Romagna hills. The route starts flat from Cervia at sea level, passes the historic salt pans, and heads inland through rolling countryside to a series of punchy climbs. The hills are not long — the longest is 10 km — but gradients reach 18%. The rhythm is distinctive: flat coastal start, progressive climbing through the Romagna foothills, emotional climax at Cima Pantani, a final timed climb, and 32 km of flat back to the Adriatic finish. The salt road runs both ways. Signature challenge: Cima Pantani on Montevecchio — not the longest or the steepest climb, but the one that matters. 373m of gain at up to 14% with Pantani's name painted on the road. The Grand Prix Via del Sale is awarded here. But the real challenge is cumulative: five distinct climbs across 156 km, with the timed segment at Paderno-Collinello coming after 85+ miles of riding. The salt road demands endurance, not just climbing power. The Granfondo Via del Sale traces the ancient route of Cervia's salt miners — from the Adriatic coast's salt pans into the Romagna hills. Twenty-nine editions, a famously low entry fee, and the legend of Marco Pantani showing up unannounced at the first race in 1997. The climbs are not Alpine — Monte Cavallo at 438m, Ciola at 557m — but gradients reach 18% on Santa Maria Riopetra, and Cima Pantani on Montevecchio has 'VAI MARCO... FORZA PIRATA' painted on the road where the champion once rode. This is a solid Italian gran fondo at an exceptional price point, steeped in salt and cycling history. Yes if you want an affordable, well-established Italian gran fondo with genuine historical narrative and a Pantani pilgrimage. Yes if punchy Romagna hills with 18% gradients at EUR35 sounds like your kind of value. Skip if you want Alpine spectacle — these are foothills, not mountains. Skip if you need a large international field — this is an Italian event for Italian cyclists. Non-negotiables: Steep gradient readiness — repeated efforts on 12-18% grades; Wind management — sustained riding in crosswind and headwind conditions; Multi-climb endurance — 4+ distinct climbs in a single training ride over 130+ km

### L'Etape Australia by Tour de France
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 89 mi | 8,861 ft | Wollongong to Kiama, New South Wales, Australia | November | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/letape-australia/

The Tour de France comes to Australia -- when it actually happens. Mountainous coastal road course replicating a Tour de France stage -- 143 km with 2,701 m of climbing on fully closed sealed roads through the NSW South Coast, Shoalhaven, and Illawarra escarpment. Signature challenge: Two ascents of Macquarie Pass (Race) separated by 40+ km of rolling terrain -- the second climb at 70+ km on tired legs is where the event is won and lost. L'Etape Australia brings Tour de France branding and ASO production values to the NSW South Coast -- 143 km of fully closed roads, 2,701 m of climbing, classification jerseys, Shimano support, and 3,000+ riders chasing the yellow jersey dream. Chris Froome rode the inaugural edition in the Snowy Mountains in 2016. The experience is genuinely special: cowbells, closed roads, professional timing, and that unmistakable TdF atmosphere. But this event has been plagued by cancellations -- severe weather, council opposition, road landslips -- to the point where showing up and actually racing feels like an achievement in itself. The move from the spectacular Snowy Mountains to the Wollongong-Kiama coastal route was forced by local politics, not course design ambition. At ~$300 AUD it is expensive for a sportive, though the included jersey and full support soften that. If you can actually get to a start line that exists, the riding is excellent. If you are based in Australia and want the closest thing to riding a Tour de France stage without leaving the Southern Hemisphere, this is your event. The 143 km course with 2,701 m of climbing on fully closed roads is a genuine challenge, the TdF atmosphere is infectious, and the included jersey is a legitimate keepsake. But you need to accept the cancellation risk: this event has been cancelled three times in four years. Do not book non-refundable international flights. Do not build your entire season around it. Train for it, enter it, hope for the best, and have a backup Australian sportive on your calendar. If you are traveling from overseas specifically for this event, consider the original L'Etape du Tour in France instead -- same brand, better reliability, more iconic climbs.

### L'Etape Czech Republic by Tour de France
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 82 mi | 5,577 ft | Prague and Central Bohemia Region, Czech Republic | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/letape-czech-republic/

ASO's Tour de France franchise in Prague: 3,000 riders on closed Bohemian roads, strong organization, and Europe's best post-ride beer. Rolling Bohemian countryside on fully closed roads. The hilly stage winds through the Central Bohemia region, passing through the Krivoklat forests, Berounsko river valley, and the Czech Karst -- a landscape of limestone formations, deep forests, ancient alleys, and historic villages. The terrain is characterized by punchy hills rather than sustained mountain climbs: categorized ascents of 2-5 km with gradients that reward explosive power as much as steady-state climbing. The 2024 edition introduced four new summits to freshen the route. Descents wind through forested sections on well-maintained roads. The course delivers a classic European hilly sportive profile -- genuinely challenging but not destructive. Signature challenge: The accumulated vertical across punchy Bohemian hills. No single climb is a monster, but 1,700 m of elevation gain delivered in short, sharp efforts across rolling terrain demands repeated high-intensity efforts. The long route's 25 km/h minimum average time limit adds pressure -- you cannot soft-pedal the hills and expect to make cutoff. L'Etape Czech Republic delivers a polished Tour de France experience on fully closed roads through the scenic Bohemian countryside, starting and finishing in one of Europe's most appealing capitals. The organization -- under ASO patronage and run by Petr Cech Sport -- is genuinely impressive for a 6-year-old event: closed roads, classification jerseys, Shimano support, celebrity riders, and thousands of enthusiastic Czech fans lining the route. Bradley Wiggins rode the 2025 edition and praised the atmosphere. The course through Krivoklat forests and the Czech Karst is beautiful, if not brutal -- 1,700 m of climbing over 132 km is honest work but not Alpine suffering. The year-round Bootcamp community program is a smart differentiator that builds loyalty beyond race day. Prague as a host city is the real bonus: affordable, beautiful, excellent food and beer, direct flights from everywhere. Yes if you want a professionally organized, closed-road sportive with Tour de France branding in one of Europe's most appealing and affordable cities. Yes if you want the L'Etape experience without Alpine-scale suffering. Skip if you need Alpine passes and 5,000 m of climbing to feel challenged. Skip if you want a globally prestigious event with international field depth -- this is still regional. Non-negotiables: Repeated-effort hill fitness -- 10-15 climbs of 2-5 km in a single ride; Sustained tempo at 25 km/h average including climbs; Group riding confidence in a 1,000-rider peloton

### Rasputitsa Spring Classic
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 60 mi | 5,000 ft | Burke, Vermont | March | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/rasputitsa-spring-classic/

Vermont spring mud season mountain gravel in Burke. Mountainous Vermont gravel with spring mud. Signature challenge: Vermont mountain climbing in spring mud season conditions. Rasputitsa Spring Classic is a Vermont spring gravel race—mountain terrain with spring mud season conditions. The unique spring mud challenge, significant climbing, and Vermont reputation elevate it above typical regional events, but the regional field keeps it in Tier 3. Burke provides moderate access. Well-organized for a spring mud event with 300-600 riders embracing the unique conditions. If you want a unique spring mud mountain challenge—yes, it's a legitimate test. If you want dry conditions or pro field depth—look elsewhere. Non-negotiables: Mud handling skills; Climbing endurance; Cold/wet weather preparation

### GranFondo Banff
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 88 mi | 5,249 ft | Banff, Alberta, Canada | August | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/rbc-gran-fondo-banff/

Five years of closed-road cycling through Canada's most famous national park. Discontinued but irreplaceable. A journey through one of the world's great mountain parks. The course looped through the Bow Valley on the Trans-Canada Highway and Bow Valley Parkway, past Lake Minnewanka, and through old-growth forest with the Canadian Rockies as a constant, humbling backdrop. The riding was moderate in difficulty but immense in scenery. Signature challenge: Riding through Banff National Park at altitude. The challenge was not the gradient or the distance but the cumulative effect of 4,500+ feet of altitude, variable mountain weather, and the emotional weight of being alone on a highway in one of Earth's most beautiful places. The GranFondo Banff existed for five glorious years and then vanished. From 2012 to 2016, it offered something no other cycling event in North America could match: closed-road access to Banff National Park, a UNESCO World Heritage Site in the heart of the Canadian Rockies.

The riding was honest: 142 km with 1,600 meters of climbing at 4,500+ feet of altitude. Not extreme, but the altitude and mountain weather added dimensions that the raw numbers do not capture. The Bow Valley Parkway through old-growth forest, the Lake Minnewanka loop with its turquoise glacial water, the jagged Rockies framing every kilometer -- this was cycling in a landscape that dwarfs human ambition.

It is discontinued. That is the brutal truth. The organizers redirected their energy to the RBC GranFondo Whistler and other events. The successor in the Canadian Rockies is the Gran Fondo Jasper, which offers a similar national park experience 400 km to the north. But Banff is Banff, and nothing quite replaces it. You cannot -- it is discontinued. For the closest current experience: Gran Fondo Jasper (June, Jasper National Park). For the same organizer's flagship event: RBC GranFondo Whistler (September, Sea-to-Sky Highway). Non-negotiables: Altitude acclimatization; Mountain weather layering; Endurance base for altitude effort

### Sagan Fondo
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 75 mi | 7,000 ft | Truckee, California | June | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/sagan-fondo/

California Sierra Nevada/Tahoe gravel in Truckee. Tahoe/Sierra mountain gravel with significant climbing and altitude. Signature challenge: Tahoe/Sierra mountain climbing at 6000ft+ altitude. Sagan Fondo is a regional event in Truckee, California—Sierra Nevada/Tahoe mountain terrain with significant elevation and altitude. Named after Peter Sagan, it has good organization and the Tahoe/Sierra setting is spectacular. However, the regional field and California costs keep it in Tier 3. Truckee provides good access. If you're in California or visiting Tahoe—yes, it's a solid event with spectacular terrain. If you're from out of state—consider the costs and regional field. Non-negotiables: Mountain climbing endurance; Altitude preparation; Mountain pacing at altitude

### THE EQUALIZER - Missouri State Championship
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 100 mi | 5,000 ft | Marthasville, Missouri | July | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/the-equalizer/

Missouri's state championship is a heat-soaked slog through wine country rollers that separates the prepared from the hospitalized. Rolling Missouri River Valley terrain. 5,000 ft over 100 miles = constant ups and downs, no sustained climbs. Mix of smooth gravel county roads (85%) and paved connectors (15%). Terrain is rolling but not extreme. Heat is the defining challenge. Signature challenge: Peak temps (90-95°F) hit hardest mid-race. No shade. Heat index over 100°F. Where heat exhaustion happens. THE EQUALIZER is Missouri's state gravel championship—100 miles through wine country in late July heat. The course is rolling but not brutal (5,000 ft, constant ups and downs, no mountain climbs). The challenge is the weather: 90-95°F with 70-85% humidity means heat index over 100°F. This is a hydration and heat-management test more than a fitness test. The wine country setting is scenic, and Missouri gravel is smooth compared to mountain states. But the heat is relentless—15-20% DNF rates are heat-related. State championship brings some fast racers, but the field is mostly recreational (200-300 riders). St. Louis airport is 1 hour away, making travel easy. Marthasville is small, but nearby St. Charles has lodging. This is an emerging Midwest gravel scene event—not iconic, but solid. If you can handle 95°F with 80% humidity while grinding constant rollers for six hours, this is solid Midwest racing. The course won't break you—smooth gravel, manageable elevation—but July heat will cook anyone who shows up with two bottles and a prayer. Perfect for riders wanting state championship bragging rights without the technical demands of mountain races.

### Vuelta Altas Cumbres
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 100 mi | 12,000 ft | Córdoba, Argentina | March | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/vuelta-altas-cumbres/

Argentine Andes mountain gravel challenge. Argentine Andes mountain gravel. Signature challenge: Argentine Andes extreme mountainous terrain at altitude. Vuelta Altas Cumbres is a regional event in Argentine Andes—extreme mountainous terrain with massive elevation at high altitude. The Argentine Andes are stunning, but the difficult/expensive access, extreme altitude, and regional field keep it firmly in Tier 3. If you have a big budget and want an extreme Argentine experience—maybe. For most riders—there are better options. Non-negotiables: Altitude training; Mountain endurance; Budget planning

### Wasatch All-Road
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 62 mi | 6,000 ft | Francis, Utah | August | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/wasatch-all-road/

Regional Utah mountain gravel with high altitude and significant climbing in Wasatch Range. Mountain gravel with significant climbing at high altitude. Signature challenge: 6000 feet of climbing at 6000-8000+ feet altitude in Wasatch Range. Wasatch All-Road offers a genuine mountain gravel challenge with high altitude and significant climbing in the beautiful Wasatch Range. Francis is a small town but proximity to Park City and Salt Lake City provides good access and amenities. The course is challenging with 6000 feet of climbing at 6000-8000+ feet elevation. It's a well-organized regional event with a moderate field. If you're in Utah or the Wasatch Front and want a mountain gravel challenge—yes, it's a well-organized event with genuine altitude and climbing. If you're traveling from far away—there are more prestigious mountain events elsewhere. Non-negotiables: Altitude preparation; Climbing strength; Thunderstorm awareness

### Superior Morgul Road Race
Tier 2 | Score: 59/100 | 56 mi | 6,000 ft | Superior, Boulder County, Colorado, USA | May | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/superior-morgul-road-race/

Colorado's most storied road racing circuit — The Wall, the Coors Classic, LeMond, and American Flyers. History on two wheels. A 7-mile paved circuit on Highway 128, fully closed to traffic. Wide, smooth asphalt through rolling Colorado Front Range terrain. The course is dominated by one feature: The Wall (Super Flag Hill), a steep climb of approximately 750 vertical feet that riders face on every lap. The Wall is the selection point — it is where the race is won and lost on every lap. Between climbs, the course rolls through open grassland with views of the Flatirons. Wind on the exposed loop can fracture a field. The corner at McCaslin Boulevard is the tactical setup point — positioning into The Wall determines who attacks and who suffers. For the Pro/1/2 category doing 8 laps, the cumulative fatigue of repeated Wall efforts is the race's defining challenge. Signature challenge: The Wall (Super Flag Hill) — repeated every lap. For Pro/1/2 riders doing 8 laps, that is 8 times up the most historically significant climb in American road racing. The cumulative fatigue of repeated Wall efforts, combined with wind on the exposed loop and tactical positioning at McCaslin Boulevard, makes this a race that rewards power, repeatability, and racing intelligence. The Superior Morgul Road Race is not the biggest race on the calendar. It is not the hardest. It is not the most prestigious event in its current form. But the ground you race on carries more American cycling history per square meter than almost anywhere else in the country. The Morgul-Bismarck circuit hosted the Red Zinger and Coors Classic — the races that introduced America to professional road cycling. LeMond, Hinault, Phinney, Carpenter, and Kiefel attacked The Wall here. Kevin Costner charged up it in American Flyers. The 1987 National Championships were decided on this loop. Today it is a regional circuit race organized by passionate locals. The field is Colorado-strong, not nationally deep. The course is a modified 7-mile version of the original 13-mile Morgul-Bismarck, compressed by suburban development. But when you crest The Wall and see the crowd lining the road, you are standing where American cycling stood up. Yes if American cycling history moves you and you want to race the same circuit as LeMond and Hinault. Yes if you are a Colorado road racer looking for a well-organized circuit race on closed roads with state championship stakes. Skip if you want a long-distance endurance event or a nationally competitive field. Non-negotiables: Repeated-effort climbing intervals in training; Altitude acclimation for sea-level visitors; Race reconnaissance — ride the course before race day

## Data Schema

Each race profile (JSON) contains:
- `race.name`, `race.slug`, `race.tagline`
- `race.vitals`: distance_mi, elevation_ft, location, date, terrain_types, field_size
- `race.gravel_god_rating`: overall_score, tier, 14 dimension scores, discipline
- `race.course_description`: character, suffering_zones, signature_challenge
- `race.terrain`: primary, surface, technical_rating, features
- `race.climate`: primary, description, challenges
- `race.logistics`: airport, lodging_strategy, official_site
- `race.history`: founded, origin_story, notable_moments
- `race.final_verdict`: one_liner, should_you_race, alternatives
- `race.non_negotiables`: training requirements with deadlines
- `race.citations`: source URLs
- `race.tire_recommendations`: primary tires, pressure tables

Access individual profiles at: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/{slug}/
Machine-readable JSON index: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race-index.json
REST API: https://gravelgodcycling.com/api/v1/docs
